Welcome back. In the previous two parts, we covered lexing and parsing — turning raw source code into tokens, then building a tree that captures the grammatical structure of your program. Today we're going one level deeper: semantic analysis, and then the part most people find surprising — how compilers actually generate code that runs faster than the code you wrote.

So let's start with what semantic analysis actually does. The parser confirms your code is grammatically legal. Semantic analysis asks whether it's meaningful. Consider this analogy: "The rock ate ambition quietly" is a grammatically perfect English sentence. Subject, verb, object, adverb. But it's nonsense. A semantic analyzer is the part of the compiler that catches the equivalent in code — things like adding a string to an integer, or calling a function with the wrong number of arguments. The grammar allows it; reality doesn't.

This phase is where type checking lives. The compiler walks the syntax tree and annotates each node with type information, then verifies that operations are applied to compatible types. This is also where symbol tables come in — essentially a structured record of every variable, function, and class in your program, tracking what they are and where they're defined.

Now here's where it gets genuinely interesting. After semantic analysis, many compilers translate your tree into something called intermediate representation, or IR. LLVM — which powers Clang, Rust, and Swift — uses an IR called LLVM IR, a kind of abstract assembly language that isn't tied to any specific hardware. This layer is crucial because it enables what comes next: optimization.

And this is the counterintuitive part. Your intuition might be that a compiler translates what you wrote into machine instructions. But modern compilers routinely produce output that doesn't resemble your code at all — and that's a feature, not a bug.

Consider loop unrolling. If you write a loop that runs four times, the compiler might delete the loop entirely and just write out the four operations in sequence. No branching, no counter checking. It's faster, even though it looks like code no human would write.

Or constant folding — if you write `x = 2 * 3 * 7`, the compiler computes `42` at compile time. Your program never performs that multiplication at runtime.

John Cocke at IBM pioneered many of these techniques in the 1970s while building optimizing compilers for the System/360. His work demonstrated something profound: the best target for a compiler isn't what the programmer wrote. It's what the programmer *meant* — the underlying computation — expressed in whatever form runs fastest on the target machine.

That's the real job of a compiler. Not translation. Transformation.

In part four, we'll look at register allocation — how the compiler decides which values live in CPU registers versus memory, and why getting this wrong can quietly destroy your program's performance. See you there.

Welcome back. In the previous three parts, we built up Gödel's core argument: that any sufficiently powerful formal system contains true statements it cannot prove. Today I want to focus on what this actually means for mathematics as a practice, because there's a widespread misunderstanding worth clearing up.

People often hear "incompleteness" and conclude that mathematics is broken, or that truth is subjective, or that we can never know anything for certain. None of that follows. Let me show you why.

Think of a formal system like a board game. Chess has rules. Those rules are complete in the sense that every move is either legal or illegal. But now ask a different question: can the rules of chess, applied only to moves on the board, tell you whether chess is a *good* game? Whether it's beautiful? Whether a particular position is strategically interesting? Obviously not. That's not a failure of chess. It's just a limitation on what any single rule-set can adjudicate.

Gödel showed something structurally similar. His incompleteness theorems, published in 1931, demonstrate that within any consistent formal system powerful enough to express basic arithmetic, there exist statements that are true but unprovable *within that system*. The key phrase is "within that system." You can often prove the statement by stepping outside, into a stronger system. But then that stronger system has its own unprovable truths. The horizon keeps moving.

Here's the non-obvious part that most summaries miss. Gödel's proof doesn't identify some exotic fringe statement as unprovable. His famous sentence essentially says "this statement is not provable in this system." If the system is consistent, the statement must be true. But the system cannot prove it without contradicting itself. That's not a bug someone introduced. It's a structural feature of any sufficiently expressive formal language.

Now, mathematician David Hilbert spent much of his career trying to build exactly the kind of complete, consistent foundation for all mathematics that Gödel proved impossible. His program, formulated around 1900, was ambitious and reasonable. Gödel's result in 1931 didn't destroy mathematics. It redirected it. Mathematicians didn't stop proving theorems. They gained a precise understanding of what formal systems can and cannot do.

And here's what's genuinely remarkable: unprovability is rare in practice. The vast majority of mathematical statements you'll encounter in a career are either provable or disprovable within standard systems like ZFC set theory. The incompleteness theorems describe a ceiling, but most of mathematics never gets anywhere near it.

So the honest takeaway is this: Gödel revealed that no finite set of rules can capture all mathematical truth. That's a profound philosophical result. But it means mathematics is *larger* than any formal system, not smaller. The map is incomplete. The territory is fine.

Next time, we'll look at the second incompleteness theorem and what it says about a system's ability to verify its own consistency. See you then.

Welcome back. In the last three parts we covered dot-product attention, multi-head attention, and positional encodings. Today we're going to focus on one specific problem that standard attention doesn't solve well: attending to very long sequences efficiently.

Here's the core issue. In vanilla self-attention, every token attends to every other token. If your sequence has n tokens, you're computing n-squared attention scores. For a sequence of 512 tokens, that's 262,000 pairs. For 16,000 tokens, you're at 256 million pairs. Memory and compute scale quadratically, which becomes a hard wall when you need to process long documents, codebases, or audio.

So the question researchers asked was: does every token actually need to attend to every other token?

In most cases, no. Nearby tokens are usually more relevant to each other. A word in the middle of a sentence rarely depends on something from 10,000 tokens earlier. This observation led to sparse attention patterns, and the clearest early implementation of this was the Longformer, published by Allen AI in 2020.

Longformer combines two attention patterns. First, sliding window attention: each token only attends to a fixed window of neighboring tokens, say 512 on each side. This is linear in sequence length, not quadratic. Second, global attention: certain designated tokens, like the classification token at the start of a document, attend to and receive attention from all other tokens. This lets task-relevant information propagate globally without making the whole matrix dense.

In PyTorch terms, if you were implementing this manually, you'd mask the attention score matrix so that position i can only write non-negative infinity values in positions outside its window before the softmax step. Everything outside the window gets zeroed out after softmax, meaning it contributes nothing to the weighted sum.

Longformer was benchmarked on long-document tasks like the WikiHop and TriviaQA datasets, where documents can exceed 4,000 tokens, and it significantly outperformed BERT-style models that had to truncate input to 512 tokens. The architectural change wasn't about making a better model conceptually, it was about making a feasible one for the data that actually exists.

A more recent and hardware-aware approach is FlashAttention, from Tri Dao and colleagues at Stanford in 2022. Instead of changing which tokens attend to which, FlashAttention keeps full attention but rewrites how the computation is executed on GPU memory. It avoids materializing the full n-squared attention matrix in high-bandwidth memory by tiling the computation into blocks that fit in the much faster SRAM on-chip cache. The result is two to four times faster attention with identical mathematical output. It's now implemented natively in PyTorch 2.0 under scaled dot-product attention.

The takeaway: attention is a flexible operation. Its efficiency depends both on which connections you compute, as in Longformer, and how you execute those computations on hardware, as in FlashAttention. Both dimensions matter in practice.

Next part we'll look at cross-attention, which is how the decoder in a sequence-to-sequence model reads the encoder's output.

Getting a job offer isn't just about being qualified — it's about being prepared in a way most candidates aren't. And the difference usually comes down to three things you do before you ever walk into the room.

Start with company research, but go deeper than the About page. Most candidates skim the website and call it done. Instead, read the company's most recent earnings call transcript if they're public, or look up recent press coverage from the last 90 days. Then cross-reference that with the job description. You're looking for the gap between where the company is and where they need to go — because that gap is your job. If you can name a specific challenge they're facing and connect it to your experience in the interview, you immediately sound like someone who's already thinking like an employee.

Once you understand the role, use the STAR method to prepare your answers — Situation, Task, Action, Result. This isn't new, but most people use it wrong. They spend 80% of their answer on the situation and task, and rush through the action and result. Flip that. The action is what you specifically did, not what the team did. The result should be quantified whenever possible. "I improved response time" is forgettable. "I reduced customer response time from 48 hours to 6 hours by restructuring the ticketing workflow, which brought our satisfaction score from 3.2 to 4.6" is what gets you remembered. Prepare four or five of these stories that can flex to answer different questions — leadership, conflict, failure, success.

The third step is practicing out loud, and this is the one people skip. Research from the University of Waterloo on retrieval practice shows that actually speaking information out loud, rather than just reviewing it in your head, significantly improves how fluently and accurately you recall it under pressure. Record yourself on your phone answering three common questions: tell me about yourself, why do you want this role, and describe a challenge you overcame. Watch it back once. You'll immediately notice filler words, vague answers, or moments where you lose energy. Fix those before you're live.

These three steps build on each other deliberately. Research gives you the content. STAR structures the content into compelling stories. And out-loud practice makes those stories feel natural instead of rehearsed.

The one thing you can do today: pull up a job posting you're actually interested in, read it carefully, and write down the single biggest problem that role is meant to solve. That one sentence becomes the thread you pull through your entire interview. Every answer you give should connect back to your ability to solve that specific problem.

That's not a trick. That's just showing up as someone who's done the work.

So in the last two videos we built up the core idea: shared resources tend to get depleted because the individual incentive and the collective interest point in opposite directions. Each person captures the full benefit of using more, but shares the cost with everyone else. The math always pushes toward overuse.

But here's what I want to dig into today, because this is where most explanations stop too early. The tragedy of the commons is not actually inevitable. And the reason we know this isn't just theoretical optimism — it's because Elinor Ostrom spent decades documenting real communities that solved it.

Ostrom won the Nobel Prize in Economics in 2009, and her central finding was striking: communities around the world had been successfully managing shared resources for centuries without either privatizing them or handing control to a central authority. Swiss villagers managing mountain pastures. Japanese fishing communities managing coastal waters. Spanish farmers sharing irrigation systems. These weren't failing. They were stable across generations.

So what were they doing differently?

Think about it this way. Imagine you and eleven neighbors share a well. The standard tragedy-of-the-commons story says everyone will pump too much and the well runs dry. But now imagine you've all lived next to each other for thirty years. You know each other's names. You see each other every morning. If someone pumps too much, you notice, and you say something. Next year's harvest affects your neighbor's kid, and you know that kid.

What Ostrom found is that the tragedy unfolds most reliably under specific conditions: when users are anonymous, when they can't communicate, when there are no agreed-upon rules, and when there's no way to monitor or sanction behavior. Remove those conditions, and the outcome changes dramatically.

Her research produced a set of design principles — things like clear boundaries around who can use the resource, rules that match local conditions, systems for monitoring usage, and graduated sanctions for violations. Not a single universal policy. A framework for letting communities build their own solutions.

Here's the counterintuitive part. We often assume that solving collective action problems requires either privatization — give someone ownership so they have an incentive to protect it — or government regulation — impose rules from outside. Ostrom's work showed there's a third path that humans have actually been using far longer than either of those institutions existed. It requires communication, trust, and accountability among the people directly involved.

This doesn't mean the tragedy never happens. It clearly does. But it means the question isn't just "how do we manage a shared resource?" The deeper question is: what social conditions need to exist for people to coordinate well? And that turns out to be a much more useful question, because it points toward things we can actually build.

Next time, we'll look at what happens when those social conditions break down at scale — and why larger groups face structurally harder versions of this problem.

If you only remember one name in the entire history of modern biology, it should be… two names. Because the first biologists were a pair of freaky intellectual twins, just like Newton and Leibniz. But with more barnacles and monkeys. Let’s meet Charles Darwin and Alfred Russel Wallace. [INTRO MUSIC PLAYS] In the early 1800s, England was already moving away from the traditional way of thinking about life, which was then called “natural theology.” In this belief system, the living world was created by a kindly but hands-off God. And there were four aspects of Creation: One, there was a divine Creator. But simply believing in God didn’t prevent you from asking how life works. Two, there were species that didn’t change, ever. This idea was known as the fixity of species. But keep in mind, the French had already worked out that species do change over time and go extinct. They just hadn’t figured out how. The third aspect of natural theology was a short creation, in which the world was only about six thousand years old. But geologists had pushed the age of the earth back to millions of years. And the fourth idea was the one that was still contentious in 1800—a perfect design for each species. The idea went: if God made, say, a turtle, he knew what he was doing. Even if that turtle was stupid or ugly, it was still a designed-by-God original, and there wasn’t too much sense trying to understand the mechanism, the how, of that design process. (Note: we here at Crash Course love turtles! There are no stupid ugly turtles!) Today, natural theology is associated with philosopher William Paley, perhaps because he wrote a book in 1802 called... Natural Theology. This book influenced a young scholar named Charles Darwin. But wanted to understand the “how.” Young Chuck originally wanted to become a physician, but he hated the sight of blood. So he went to Cambridge to study beetles but skipped a lot of lectures. He earned Gentleman’s Cs and graduated at twenty two with no direction in life, just a huge collection of beetles. Which, to be honest, is more than I had. So Chuck’s family did what any rich family with a beetle-obsessed son would do: they sent him to South America on a ship with a cute name: The HMS Beagle. Chuck was seasick the entire five years. But the voyage was more than worth the lost lunches: it turned Darwin from a mere collector into an extraordinary theorist. For one, the voyage gave Darwin time to read the geological theories of Charles Lyell and think about gradual change over long ages. Wouldn’t it be nice to like get on a boat with a couple of books and just think for 5 years, and puke a lot? Near Concepción, Chile, Darwin saw a volcano and felt an earthquake: he got to live through Lyell’s theory of how geological changes happen! The voyage also gave him lots of opportunity to collect and compare fossils. For instance, Darwin found fossils of a species that looked like a giant sloth, one like a giant capybara, and one like a giant armadillo. And Darwin thought, what if these giant creatures did not just coincidentally look like modern animals, like sloths, rodents, and armored mammals, but were actually ancestors of them? But the main thing the voyage did was give Chuck access to hundreds of specimens from similar-looking species that lived close to each other, but in slightly different environments. The most famous examples, of course, come from the Galapagos Islands off the coast of Ecuador, home to land and marine iguanas, diverse mockingbirds and thrushes, tortoises by the dozen, and—say it with me—finches! Like beetles before and barnacles later, finches became an absolute obsession for Chuck. He had to catch them all. Lucky for him, the people who lived near the Galapagos even explained to him that the different species seemed to vary according to island. This turned out to be one in a number of clues that would lead Chuck to developing the theory of evolution by natural selection. When he got back to London, Darwin wrote up his meticulous fieldnotes. He published the Zoology of the Voyage of HMS Beagle in volumes from 1838 to 1843. This alone made Darwin a serious naturalist. His generous sharing of specimens made him not only a member of the London scientific world, but a leader in it. He sent specimens to naturalists including John Gould, who helped Darwin confirm that his finches were actually different species, and not just varieties of one species. Major clue! And then Darwin read the Essay on the Principle of Population by a cranky reverend named Thomas Robert Malthus. Malthus argued that population increases geometrically, but food only increases arithmetically: the logical result must be famine. This was the final clue—a relationship between the environment and the reproduction of populations. Darwin reasoned that living beings compete over resources, and only the most fit for a given region survive. It’s as if nature selects them. Hence his choice of the term “natural selection,” for the primary mechanism of evolution. Compare this to natural theology: there’s no Creator involved. Species aren’t fixed. The process takes aeons. And design—what design? Useful traits emerge over time. At the same time that he was solving the biggest problem facing the study of “what is life?”, Darwin was also busy performing Victorian gentleman-ness: he married his cousin, Emma Wedgwood, in 1839. He had babies and moved to a manor in Kent. He got REALLY into billiards. But, what Darwin was not doing? Publishing a complete theory of evolution. What was Darwin’s next move? Pigeon fancying. He bred pigeons in order to understand artificial selection, or how humans design organisms. Because breeding pigeons is a lot faster than watching finches change over millennia. And then? Eight years studying barnacles. He worked on his theory, writing to his friends for advice about it. But he didn’t publish it. Darwin wanted to wait until he had incontrovertible proof. Then in 1858, Darwin received a letter from one Alfred Russel Wallace. Wallace had also discovered natural selection. He sincerely wrote to Darwin for advice: would he be interested in, uh, an evidence-based theory explaining how organisms evolve? Darwin’s mentor, Lyell, told him that he had no choice now. So Darwin and Wallace published a joint letter in the Journal of the Proceedings of the Linnean Society of London. And then Darwin wrote a compelling, five hundred-page book detailing his theory. In one year. In 1859, On the Origin of Species by Means of Natural Selection, or just “the Origin,” debuted as a modest edition of twelve hundred copies but soon became a smash hit. It was a scientific bestseller, intended for a wide audience. No footnotes! The book explained how “descent with modification” or “transformism” actually works. In any population of the same species, you can see a natural variation in traits: some finches have longer beaks; some have shorter beaks. Over time, small changes in the environment add up, favoring some traits over others. Natural selection modifies the population: the fittest survive and reproduce, passing on their traits. Say, the long beaks get to more food and survive when food becomes scarce. Unfit species die out. Populations diverge into new species! Darwin’s writing, influenced by Malthus, shifted away from a harmony of nature and toward a competition for resources—a war. But Darwin’s theory wasn’t perfect. In fact, he included a chapter on the “difficulties” facing natural selection. These included a lack of transitional forms in the fossil record: how do we get from fish to amphibians? Even more basically, where do variations come from? How are they passed on from one generation to the next? We have answers to these questions today—stayed tuned!—but Darwin didn’t, and he admitted as much. This was… refreshing. He didn’t claim to be a genius. He didn’t bash earlier theories. The Origin didn’t even mention God, much less where humans come from. Instead, Darwin focused on what he knew: variation in beetles, finches, pigeons, barnacles, and fossil animals. His theory, backed by so much evidence, accounted for how new species evolve. Even more importantly, Darwin united many branches of natural history into a single synthetic theory and proposed a bunch of clear and important questions for future research. In fact, Origin marks a kind of evolution of natural history, which was focused on observation and description, into biology, which is more focused on testing theories about living things. Darwin’s skill as a writer is one reason his name eclipsed Wallace’s. Another is that my dude was rich! Like, born rich, invested well, married rich… He was doing all right. And Wallace? Introduce him, ThoughtBubble: Al was born to a family with money problems. He quit school at sixteen to work surveying canals with his brother. And at twenty, he quit surveying to teach. Wallace read Malthus. He also read Darwin’s details of his American voyage and became a lifelong fan. Wallace met Henry Walter Bates in 1844. They became science besties and decided to figure out how evolution works in order to apply that knowledge to human society and save people from greed and individualism. Wallace and Bates went to the Amazon from 1848 to 1852 to collect specimens for museums in London. And he was super successful… until he set off back to England. His boat, brimming with specimens, caught fire and sank. Wallace watched from his lifeboat as all of his hard work—his carefully trapped and cataloged monkeys and his parrots—sank into the dark waves. He was not rescued for ten days. But, like Galileo after his trial by the Inquisition, Wallace never gave up. In debt, Wallace decided to continue collecting more specimens to pay the bills, this time in Southeast Asia in 1854. He sent specimens back regularly, developing a brilliant reputation. And Wallace was doing a more than collecting: traveling from island to island across what is now Indonesia, Malaysia, and Singapore, he observed that different environments seem to produce different populations of organisms… perhaps even new species. While sick in bed in 1858, Wallace suddenly recalled the essay on populations by Malthus, and an entire theory clicked into place. In his words: A “self-acting process” meant that, for any species, “the fittest would survive.” The colleague he thought would most appreciate this wild new theory? A slightly older naturalist in London… Thanks ThoughtBubble. After the joint paper with Darwin, Wallace continued working in Southeast Asia until 1862. He published his major work, The Malay Archipelago, in 1869. Wallace observed that there is a sort of invisible line—“Wallace’s line”—in Indonesia: to the west, species resemble those in Asia. To the east, they resemble those in Australia. In fact, Wallace invented the discipline of biogeography, or the study of plants, animals, and geological formations together. So, between them, Darwin and Wallace were reaching a lot of people with their ideas. And it’s true that some people with conservative religious values were outraged at the public acceptance of a Creation that had no elegant design, just random variation. But the reaction to natural selection was mostly acceptance. Darwin’s writing, and the depth of his and Wallace’s evidence, settled the matter for many readers. In fact, in some ways, Darwinism was accepted too easily. Darwinism quickly became equated with the term “survival of the fittest,” which was coined in 1864 by biologist Herbert Spencer and appeared in the fifth edition of Origin. This term isn’t a bad summary of natural selection, in terms of animals and plants. But it was increasingly applied to human society, as “social Darwinism,” in a way that Darwin and Wallace would not have approved of. An Industrial Revolution has just brought radical change to England. A new class of capitalists saw themselves as more “fit” to govern the world than the nobles they replaced or the workers they controlled. Next time—Darwin’s cousin, Frank Galton, will try to apply Chuck and Al’s theory to society. Yes, it becomes strange, and even tragic. So be sure to come back. Today we're continuing our RAG series, and we're going to focus on a specific failure mode that breaks most production systems: context window stuffing.

Here's the problem. You retrieve your top five chunks from a vector database, you concatenate them, and you pass everything to the language model. But those chunks often contain redundant information, contradictory passages, or irrelevant sentences that dilute the actual signal. The model doesn't ignore the noise. It attends to it.

This is called the lost-in-the-middle problem, and it was documented in a 2023 paper by Liu et al. from Stanford. They showed that language models consistently underperform when the relevant information appears in the middle of a long context window. Performance is highest when relevant content sits at the beginning or the end. If you're stuffing ten chunks indiscriminately, you're likely burying the answer.

The solution is a two-stage retrieval pipeline, sometimes called retrieve-then-rerank.

In the first stage, you retrieve broadly. Use a fast approximate nearest neighbor search, something like FAISS or Pinecone, to pull back maybe twenty candidate chunks based on embedding similarity. This stage prioritizes recall. You want the right answer to be somewhere in that set, even if there's noise.

In the second stage, you rerank. You pass each candidate chunk alongside the original query to a cross-encoder model. Unlike bi-encoders, which encode the query and document separately, a cross-encoder looks at the query and document together and produces a single relevance score. This is slower but significantly more accurate. Cohere's Rerank API does exactly this. So does the sentence-transformers library with models like cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2.

After reranking, you select only the top three or four chunks. You've gone from twenty noisy candidates down to a tight, high-relevance context window.

Here's a concrete example. Suppose you're building a RAG system over a legal document repository. A user asks about termination clauses in a specific contract type. Your bi-encoder retrieval might return chunks about termination clauses in general, about different contract types, and about related penalty structures. After reranking with a cross-encoder, you surface only the two passages that are jointly about termination and that specific contract type. The model now has a focused context and gives a precise answer instead of hedging across multiple interpretations.

The practical tradeoff is latency. Cross-encoders are computationally heavier. Running twenty cross-encoder passes adds roughly 200 to 400 milliseconds depending on your infrastructure. For many applications this is acceptable. For real-time chat with strict latency budgets, you'd want to cache reranker results or reduce your candidate pool.

The takeaway: retrieval quality and context quality are separate problems. Vector search solves the first. Reranking solves the second. You need both to build a RAG system that's actually reliable.

Next part, we'll cover chunking strategies and why fixed-size chunking is usually the wrong default.

Welcome back. In the last three parts we covered researching your market rate, timing your negotiation, and handling the first offer. Today we're going deeper into the actual conversation — specifically what to do when an employer pushes back.

Pushback is normal. Research from Salary.com found that 84% of employers expect candidates to negotiate, yet most people interpret the first "no" or "that's our budget" as a final answer. It isn't. It's a position, not a verdict.

So here's how to navigate it in three moves.

First, stay anchored to data, not emotion. When an employer says the number doesn't work, resist the urge to apologize or immediately concede. Instead, return to your market research. Say something like: "I understand there may be constraints. Based on my research on Levels.fyi and Glassdoor, the median compensation for this role in this market is around $X. Can you help me understand where that gap is coming from?" You're not being aggressive — you're being specific. That specificity forces a real conversation rather than a vague standoff.

Second, if salary is genuinely fixed, expand the negotiation surface. This is a strategy called "expanding the pie," and it comes from the negotiation research of Adam Grant and others studying integrative bargaining. The idea is that there's rarely just one variable on the table. If base pay won't move, ask about signing bonuses, remote work flexibility, accelerated performance reviews, additional PTO, or professional development budgets. A $5,000 signing bonus costs the company less in the long run than raising your base, so finance departments often have more flexibility there. Don't accept "the package is the package" at face value — ask which parts of the package have flexibility.

Third, give them a specific number when you counter, not a range. When people say "I'm looking for somewhere between $95,000 and $105,000," employers anchor to $95,000. That range signals uncertainty and gives away your floor. Instead, say: "Based on everything we've discussed, I'd like to propose $104,000." The precision signals confidence and research. It also gives the employer a clear target to work toward or negotiate from, which moves the conversation forward faster.

One thing to keep in mind throughout all of this — pushback often has nothing to do with your value. It's frequently a budget cycle issue, an internal equity constraint, or a hiring manager who needs to get approval from someone above them. Staying curious rather than defensive keeps the conversation collaborative.

Here's your takeaway for today: before your next conversation, write down three specific things outside of base salary that would genuinely improve the offer for you. Know that list before you walk in. If the salary discussion hits a wall, you'll have real alternatives ready — and that preparation alone shifts the entire dynamic.

Last time we covered approximate nearest neighbor search and why exact search breaks down at scale. Today we're going into indexing strategies — specifically why HNSW has become the default choice in production systems, and where it actually fails.

The problem is straightforward. You have a million vectors, each with 1536 dimensions — the output size from OpenAI's text-embedding-ada-002 model. You want to find the 10 most similar vectors to a query. Brute force cosine similarity means doing a million dot products every single query. That's manageable in a prototype. It's not manageable when you're serving thousands of requests per second.

HNSW stands for Hierarchical Navigable Small World. The paper is by Malkov and Yashunin, published in 2018, and it's worth reading. The core idea borrows from skip lists. You build multiple layers of a graph. The top layer is sparse — only a few nodes connected with long-range links. The bottom layer is dense — every vector is present, connected to its nearest neighbors. At query time, you enter at the top, greedily navigate toward your target, then drop down to the next layer and repeat. You're narrowing in on the answer quickly without checking everything.

In practice, Qdrant, Weaviate, and Milvus all use HNSW as their primary index. The two parameters you'll tune are M, which controls how many edges each node has, and ef_construction, which controls how thoroughly the graph is built during indexing. Higher M means better recall but more memory. In Qdrant's Python client, you'd set this when creating a collection — passing a HnswConfigDiff object with your M and ef values. The default M of 16 gives you roughly 95 percent recall at around 1 millisecond query latency on a million vectors, depending on hardware.

Here's where it breaks down. HNSW is built in memory. A million 1536-dimensional float32 vectors takes about 6 gigabytes before you add the graph structure on top. At 100 million vectors, you're looking at infrastructure that most teams can't afford to keep hot. This is why DiskANN, developed at Microsoft Research, matters. It's designed to run the index from SSD rather than RAM, accepting slightly higher latency — around 5 to 10 milliseconds — but reducing memory requirements by an order of magnitude. Azure AI Search now uses DiskANN under the hood for this reason.

The practical takeaway is this: if your dataset fits in memory and you need sub-millisecond latency, HNSW with M between 16 and 32 is your starting point. If you're working at hundreds of millions of vectors or you're cost-constrained on RAM, look at DiskANN or product quantization to compress the vectors before indexing.

Next video we'll cover filtering — specifically why combining metadata filters with vector search is harder than it looks, and how engines like Qdrant handle it without destroying recall.

Welcome back. In the last four parts, we built up the machinery: formal systems, Gödel numbering, and the construction of a statement that essentially says "this statement is not provable." Today I want to focus on what the theorems actually *mean* — and push back on some popular misconceptions, because the incompleteness theorems are probably the most frequently misapplied results in all of mathematics.

Let's start with a clean restatement. Gödel's first incompleteness theorem says that any consistent formal system powerful enough to express basic arithmetic will contain true statements that cannot be proved within that system. The second theorem says such a system cannot prove its own consistency.

Here's where people go wrong. They hear "unprovable true statements" and conclude that mathematics is somehow broken, or that human intuition transcends logic. Neither follows.

Think about a map. A map of London cannot contain a perfectly accurate map of itself at full scale — the self-reference creates a limit. But this doesn't mean London is chaotic or unknowable. It means the map has a specific, well-defined boundary on what it can represent. Gödel's result is the same kind of structural observation. It describes the edges of a formal system, not a flaw in mathematics itself.

Now here's the non-obvious part. When Gödel constructed his unprovable statement — call it G — G is unprovable *within the system*, but we, standing outside the system and reasoning informally, can see that G is true. Why? Because G says "I am not provable," and if the system is consistent, that statement accurately describes its own situation. So truth and provability come apart. They are not the same thing. This was genuinely shocking in 1931, because Hilbert's program — the dominant project in foundations of mathematics at the time — assumed you could, in principle, mechanize all of mathematical truth. Gödel showed that's impossible.

But here's the careful point: adding G as a new axiom just creates a stronger system — and that stronger system has *its own* Gödel sentence. You can always climb one level, but you can never close the gap entirely. It's not a one-time hole. It's a permanent structural feature.

The second theorem has a sharper practical edge. It means you cannot bootstrap trust in a system from within the system itself. Any proof of consistency has to come from *outside*, from a system with stronger assumptions. This is why mathematical foundations remain an active area of research, not a solved problem.

So what should you take away? Gödel didn't show that truth is subjective, or that some things are forever unknowable in a mystical sense. He showed something precise: formal provability is strictly weaker than semantic truth. That's a mathematical fact about the limits of formal systems — and understanding *exactly* what it says is far more interesting than the vague awe it usually gets.

Today we're covering model serving latency, specifically the gap between offline evaluation metrics and what actually happens when your model hits production traffic.

Here's the problem. You train a model, it scores well on your held-out test set, you deploy it, and suddenly P99 latency is 800 milliseconds. Users are dropping off. Your SLA is 200 milliseconds. The model is technically accurate but practically broken.

This happens because training and serving have completely different computational profiles. During training you process large batches on GPU with no time constraint. During serving you're often handling single requests or small dynamic batches with strict latency budgets. The optimizations that help training hurt serving and vice versa.

Let's make this concrete. Say you've trained a BERT-based text classifier using HuggingFace Transformers. The base BERT model has 110 million parameters. At inference on a single CPU, you're looking at roughly 150 to 200 milliseconds per request. That's already borderline for most applications, and that's before network overhead, preprocessing, and postprocessing.

The standard path forward is a combination of three techniques: quantization, distillation, and ONNX export.

Quantization converts your model weights from 32-bit floats to 8-bit integers. Using PyTorch's built-in dynamic quantization, you can often cut model size by 4x and inference time by 2 to 3x with less than 1% accuracy drop on most classification tasks. One line: `torch.quantization.quantize_dynamic(model, {torch.nn.Linear}, dtype=torch.qint8)`.

Knowledge distillation is more involved. You train a smaller student model to mimic your larger teacher model. DistilBERT, published by HuggingFace, uses this approach to produce a model that's 40% smaller, 60% faster, and retains 97% of BERT's performance on GLUE benchmarks. For most production use cases, that tradeoff is extremely favorable.

ONNX export lets you take your PyTorch model and run it through optimized runtimes like ONNX Runtime, which applies graph-level optimizations including operator fusion and memory layout improvements that PyTorch's default eager mode doesn't apply. In practice, ONNX Runtime often gives you another 20 to 40% latency reduction on top of quantization with zero accuracy impact.

For actual serving infrastructure, Triton Inference Server from NVIDIA handles batching, model versioning, and multiple backend runtimes in a single framework. If you're on a smaller scale, TorchServe is easier to configure and integrates directly with PyTorch models.

The workflow that works in practice: export to ONNX, apply quantization, benchmark with realistic request distributions using a tool like Locust or k6, then profile where time is actually being spent using Triton's built-in metrics or OpenTelemetry traces.

The key insight is that your serving architecture is a separate engineering problem from your modeling problem, and it needs the same rigor. A model that can't meet latency requirements isn't a deployed model, it's a prototype.

Next part we'll cover feature stores and handling training-serving skew, which is where most production ML bugs actually come from.

Welcome back. In the last two episodes we covered how to update beliefs with new evidence and why base rates matter so much. Today I want to push into something more uncomfortable: what happens when your evidence is genuinely good, but your conclusion is still probably wrong.

Let's start from scratch. Bayesian reasoning says your final confidence in a claim equals your prior probability multiplied by how much the evidence shifts things. Simple enough. But here's the piece most people skip: the prior isn't just a number you assign based on gut feeling. It reflects the actual frequency of something being true in the relevant reference class.

Here's a concrete example. Imagine a medical test for a rare disease. The disease affects one in a thousand people. The test is ninety-five percent accurate, meaning it correctly identifies sick people ninety-five percent of the time and correctly clears healthy people ninety-five percent of the time. You test positive. How worried should you be?

Most people say very worried. Ninety-five percent accurate sounds decisive. But run the numbers. In a population of ten thousand people, ten actually have the disease. The test catches nine-and-a-half of them, roughly. But it also falsely flags five percent of the nine thousand nine hundred ninety healthy people, which is about five hundred false positives. So out of roughly five hundred and ten positive tests, only about nine or ten are real cases. Your positive result means you have roughly a two percent chance of actually being sick.

That's not a flaw in the test. That's mathematics. The low base rate of the disease dominates the calculation, and no amount of test accuracy fully overcomes it. This phenomenon is sometimes called the base rate fallacy, and it showed up in real-world HIV screening debates in the 1980s, where public health researchers had to carefully explain to policymakers why broad population screening without targeted risk assessment would produce enormous numbers of false positives.

Now here's the counterintuitive insight I want you to sit with. A strong piece of evidence does not guarantee a strong conclusion. Evidence and conclusions live in different spaces. Evidence tells you how much to shift. Your prior tells you where you started. If you started somewhere very unlikely, even dramatic evidence may only move you to mildly plausible.

This is why scientists replicate findings. A single experiment with a p-value of point-oh-one sounds convincing, but if the hypothesis being tested was implausible to begin with, the prior is so low that one study barely moves the needle. Psychologist Uri Simonsohn and colleagues demonstrated this empirically when auditing social priming research, finding that many "significant" results dissolved when base rates and prior plausibility were properly accounted for.

The practical takeaway is this: when you encounter compelling evidence for a surprising claim, your first question shouldn't be "is this evidence good?" It should be "how unlikely was this claim before I saw anything?"

Evidence quality and claim plausibility are two separate inputs. You need both. See you next time.

We’re going to go through the 7 most important data structures today and explain them as simply as possible. These are extremely important to learn whether its for coding interviews, computer science class, or building projects. We’ll be going through the list from easiest to hardest, so beginners have a better idea of where to begin. We’ll go over a simplified explanation for what each data structure is and talk about common uses for that data structure. I’ll also be putting the time complexity for common operations on screen, but if you don’t know what that is, don’t worry; I’m just including it on the screen for people who want to see. Let’s not waste any time and get right into it. Arrays Arrays are ordered collections of data. Typically the data is all of a similar type, like integers or strings, but some languages also allow for differing data types. An example of a real-life use for an array would be if you had temperatures for the next 5 days, and wanted to store them so your program could access them. Arrays are used all the time and for pretty much everything, so they’re definitely the most important data structure to learn first. One of the amazing benefits of using an array is that it’s very easy to find any element, as each element in an array is assigned a number called an index that you can use to find it. This form of numbering is often called “zero-based indexing”, which just means the first element in the array is at an index of 0. This can often confuse new programmers, because if you want to retrieve the second element, you have to use its index, which is 1, not 2. While the advantage of arrays is that it makes it easy to read elements (O(1)), the disadvantage is that they have a slightly harder time inserting or deleting elements (O(n)). Now, just a quick note. We won’t talk about memory much in this video, but for the first two data structures in this video, the memory component is very simple, and important so you can tell the difference between them. We’ll use our temperature array from earlier as an example. Arrays are stored in contiguous memory, which means each of the elements in the array are next to one another in memory. If a new element is added in the middle of the array, the entire array must shift down. But what would’ve happened if there was already something in the next memory address? To fit this new element in now, the array’s memory will have to be reallocated to an entirely new space where all of the elements fit. While computers are incredibly quick, this is not very efficient. Arrays are very good for reading elements, but can a bit less efficient when it comes to insertion or deletion. Now we’ll take a look at a data structure that’s the opposite. Linked Lists While arrays were fast at reading elements, and a bit slower at inserting or deleting, linked lists are a bit slower at reading elements, but fast at inserting or deleting. Linked lists are similar to arrays, in that they also store ordered lists of data elements. However, a huge difference is in the way they are stored in memory. Each element of a linked list has what we call a pointer, which is basically the address of the next element of the list. As a result, elements in a linked list do not have to be stored beside each other. You can store the next element in any location, and the previous element will point to it if you want to find it. The advantage of this is that it solves our problem with array insertions and deletions. To add a new element, you find a free spot in memory for it, and have the previous element point to it. To remove an element, you just delete the element and have the previous point to the one ahead of the deleted element. The disadvantage of this is that linked lists do not have indexes, as the elements are not stored right beside each other. This means that to find an element, we have to go through the list starting from the beginning. If we want the third element, we have to first look at the first element, see where it points, go to the second, see where it points, and then we find the third. If you think of a huge linked list, you can start to understand why it’s not the fastest at reading. So, to recap. Arrays are faster when it comes to reading, linked lists are faster when it comes to inserting and deleting. HashMap Remember how arrays had values stored, and for each value, there was an index that numbered them? Well, hash maps are essentially the same thing, except that you can choose what the “index” is, which hash maps call a key. The key and it’s value are commonly known as key-value pairs. The other major difference between arrays and hash maps is that hash maps are unordered. Hash maps are fast (O(1)) for both inserting and removing elements. But the real benefit to using hash maps is their ability to search quickly (O(1)). Let’s say you wanted to store a list of capital cities. If we stored these in an array, we would have to know the index for each one to read it. But for a hash map, if we make the keys countries, we can just look up the country to find the capital city. Hash maps go by a few different names. They are sometimes called hash tables, or, if you know Python, they’re called dictionaries. For the purposes of this video, you can assume they’re all the same thing. So, if you know how to use dictionaries in Python, congratulations! You’ve actually been using hash maps. The way hash maps work underneath the hood is really interesting, but a bit out of the scope of this video. I’ll be making specific videos for all of the data structures, so we’ll cover hash maps more in-depth there. For now, just understand that they’re unordered, and their custom keys allow for very quick searching. Stacks & Queues The most simple way to describe stacks is to think of a stack of plates or pancakes. The first plate goes on the bottom, the last plate goes the top. Stacks are LIFO structures, which stands for last in, first out, because the last element in is like the last plate that goes on top the stack. When you go to grab a plate, this last plate on top will be the first you take off. Stacks have three common operations, which are push, pop, and peek. Pushing is when you add a new element to the top of the stack. Popping is when you remove the top-most element from the stack. And peeking is when you’re just taking a look at what the element at the top of the stack is. All of these are very fast, which is why stacks are optimal for certain problems. If you’re wondering when stacks might be used, think of the pancake and plate examples. For any scenario that has a similar structure where the last element in is the first element out, stacks are likely a good data structure to use. Queues are the opposites of stacks. The simplest way to think of a queue is like a lineup at a grocery store. The first person in the line will get serviced first, and every additional person who joins the line goes at the end. Queues are FIFO structures, which stands for first in, first out, because the first element in is the first element to come out. Queues have very similar operations to stacks, which are enqueue, dequeue, and front. Enqueue is like push for a stack, and is when a new element is added to the back of the queue. Dequeue is like pop for a stack, and is when the element on the front of the queue is removed. Front is like peek for a stack, and is when you take a look at the front-most element in the queue. Queues are more frequently used than stacks, especially in real-world programming. Think of YouTube playlists. When you start watching a playlist, you’ll start with the video that was added first, and the last video you watch will be the final one that was added. Trees Trees are a category of data structure that, as you might have guessed by the name, resemble trees. Trees have nodes, which are connected to each other by edges. The first node in a tree is called the root node. Nodes have a parent-child direction, where one is a parent node that leads to another node, which is a child node. Sometimes, the parent nodes are sometimes just called nodes, and the child nodes are called leaves. There are tons of tree-based data structures, but in this video, we’ll be talking about binary trees, and in particular, binary search trees. A binary tree is a tree where each parent node has up to two children nodes. A binary search tree is a type of binary tree, where all left children nodes are less than the parent node, and all right children nodes are greater than the parent node. These binary search trees make it very easy to search through large amounts of ordered values. The classic example is to think of a number guessing game, where one person thinks of a number between 1 and 100, and the other person has to guess. With each guess, they get told whether the correct number is higher or lower than their guess. The strategy is to always guess the middle number, because then you’re eliminating the most amount possible each time. This is what a binary search tree does. We can eliminate a parent node and everything below it or above it, and continue that process until we get to our correct number. This is not only useful for this game though. A more practical example is to think of a digital dictionary. Dictionaries have over 100,000 words. If you give a computer a word, and want it to give you the definition, it would be incredibly slow for it to start at the beginning and look through each word until it finds the correct one (O(n)). Instead, because the dictionary is sorted alphabetically, the computer is able to go right to the word in the middle of the dictionary, and check if the target word comes before or after. It continues sorting like this until it reaches the word (O(logn)). There are tons of other tree-structures like heaps and tries, but we’ll leave those for another video. Graphs Lastly we have graphs. Graphs are basically models for a set of connections. Like trees, graphs are made up of nodes and edges. In fact, trees, and even linked lists to an extent, are technically types of graphs. But graphs can get a lot more complicated. In a graph, there are less restrictions than with trees. Nodes can be connected to any amount of neighbours. Graphs can be directed, where nodes point to other nodes, but they can also be undirected. Some graphs have cycles, where two nodes both point to each other. The edges between nodes can also be weighted, where the path has a value associated with it. As you can see, graphs are very complicated, which is why many people consider them to be one of the hardest data structures to learn. I’m going to make an entirely separate video dedicated to graphs, so we’ll tackle the complexities in a little bit. For now, let’s see an example where graphs are useful data structures. Imagine you’re running errands, and you have five different stores to visit. We can represent this as a graph, where each store is a node, and each edge has a distance. Using this data structure, we can develop an algorithm that allows us to calculate what the shortest route between all five places is. For a real-life example, think of Uber. Every Uber driver and user could be seen as nodes, and the application is constantly trying to optimize so that the waiting time for each rider is as short as possible. Last time we talked about the foundation of professional reputation — showing up consistently and doing what you say you'll do. Today I want to go a level deeper, because consistency alone isn't enough. You also need to be *visible* in the right ways, and that requires a more intentional strategy.

Let's start with what I call the Expertise Surface Area. The idea is simple: your reputation can only travel as far as your work is visible. So the first concrete step is to document your thinking, not just your output. Most people share finished results — a completed project, a final report. But what actually builds reputation is showing *how* you think. Write a short internal memo explaining why you made a key decision. Post a LinkedIn reflection after a project wraps. Send a brief update to stakeholders that includes what you learned, not just what you delivered. Adam Grant's research at Wharton found that people who share knowledge generously — what he calls "givers" — consistently end up at the top of professional networks over time. The mechanism is trust. When people understand your reasoning, they trust your judgment, not just your results.

Second step: actively seek feedback that's specific. Not "how did I do?" but "what's one thing I could have communicated more clearly in that presentation?" This matters because vague feedback gives you nothing to act on, and it also signals to the person giving it that you're serious about growth. When you do this repeatedly with mentors, managers, or even peers, something interesting happens — they become invested in your development. They start advocating for you in rooms you're not in. That's reputation by proxy, and it's extraordinarily powerful.

Third, and this one is underused: create a pattern of public intellectual generosity. This doesn't mean you need a huge audience. It means that when a colleague asks a question in a meeting, you give a thorough answer. When someone in your industry posts a genuinely interesting problem online, you respond with real substance. When you solve a tricky problem at work, you turn it into a reusable template or a short how-to that you share with your team. Over time, people associate your name with clarity and usefulness. That association *is* your reputation.

Here's how these three steps connect: documenting your thinking expands your surface area, seeking specific feedback sharpens your actual skills, and intellectual generosity circulates your name in positive contexts. Each one amplifies the others.

The thing you can do today is simple. Think about one decision you made this week — even a small one — and write two or three sentences explaining why you made it. Share that with one person. That's it. You've just started building something that compounds.

If you watched part one, you already know that remote work effectiveness starts with protecting your attention — specifically, separating shallow communication from deep work. Today we're going one level deeper. We're talking about how you actually sustain output over weeks and months, not just on a good Tuesday.

Let's start with how you structure your week, not just your day. Most people think about productivity at the task level. But Cal Newport's research on knowledge workers shows that your biggest gains come from planning at the weekly level — deciding in advance which days or half-days are reserved for what kind of work. So here's what this looks like in practice: pick two or three mornings per week and block them as protected deep work time. Nothing gets scheduled there. No standups, no Slack catch-ups, nothing. The rest of your week can be more collaborative and reactive. This isn't about being rigid — it's about having a default that you protect rather than a blank calendar that fills itself randomly.

Now, once you have that structure, the second piece is how you handle the energy problem. Remote workers often report that the hardest part isn't distraction — it's that work and rest blur together until you feel like you're always half-working. A 2021 study from Microsoft's Human Factors Lab found that back-to-back video meetings cause measurable spikes in cortisol and beta wave activity — basically, your brain under sustained stress. Their recommendation, which is backed by the data, is building 10-minute gaps between meetings. That sounds tiny, but it functions as a neural reset. Use those gaps to step outside, make tea, or just sit without a screen. You're not wasting time — you're maintaining the cognitive baseline you need for the rest of the day.

Third, build what I'd call an end-of-day shutdown ritual. This is borrowed directly from Newport's digital minimalism work, and it's one of the highest-leverage habits a remote worker can build. The ritual is simple: at a consistent time each day, review your task list, write your top three priorities for tomorrow, then say — out loud or in writing — "shutdown complete." The verbal or written marker matters because your brain is pattern-sensitive. Over time, that phrase becomes a real psychological boundary between work mode and off mode. Without this, remote workers tend to stay in a low-grade work mindset all evening, which destroys recovery.

So here's how these three things connect: weekly planning gives you structure, strategic breaks protect your cognitive capacity within that structure, and the shutdown ritual closes the loop so you actually recover and show up strong the next day.

The one thing you can do today: write down which two mornings this week you're going to block for focused work, and set a recurring calendar event right now before you close this tab.

Most people treat salary negotiation like a confrontation. It's not. It's a collaborative conversation about value, and once you reframe it that way, the whole thing gets easier.

Let's walk through exactly how to do this well.

**Step one: anchor high with a specific number.**

Research consistently shows that the first number in a negotiation has an outsized influence on the final outcome — this is called the anchoring effect, and it's been documented extensively in behavioral economics, including in work by Adam Galinsky at Columbia Business School. The practical implication is simple: don't wait for them to anchor you. Come in first, and come in specific.

Say you're targeting $95,000. Don't say "somewhere in the mid-nineties." Say "$97,500." A precise number signals that you've done the math — that this figure comes from somewhere real. Round numbers feel like guesses. Specific numbers feel like research.

Before you name that number, though, you need to know what it should actually be. Use Levels.fyi for tech roles, the Bureau of Labor Statistics for broader benchmarks, or ask people directly in communities like Blind or relevant subreddits. Your anchor needs to be defensible, not just aspirational.

**Step two: justify your number with a value bridge.**

Once you've named your figure, connect it to the value you bring — not to your personal financial needs. Nobody pays you more because your rent went up. They pay you more because you're worth more to them than what they're currently offering.

A value bridge sounds like this: "Based on my experience leading the migration project at my last company, which reduced infrastructure costs by 22%, and the market data I've reviewed for this role and location, I'm targeting $97,500." That sentence does three things: it references a concrete result, it signals market awareness, and it gives them a reason to move toward your number rather than away from it.

**Step three: get comfortable with silence after the ask.**

This is the step most people skip, and it kills otherwise strong negotiations. After you state your number and your value bridge, stop talking. Seriously. The next person who speaks tends to concede ground. Silence feels uncomfortable, but it's actually neutral — you've made your case, and now it's their turn to respond to it.

If they come back with something lower, your next move isn't to panic or immediately accept. It's to ask: "Is there flexibility on that?" That single question, asked calmly, has moved offers by thousands of dollars more times than you'd expect.

Here's your takeaway for today: before your next salary conversation, write down one specific result from your work history — a number, a percentage, a timeline — and practice connecting it to the salary you want. You don't need to rehearse a script. You just need one concrete example of your value that you can say out loud without flinching.

That's what changes the conversation.

In the last episode, we talked about how TCP gave the internet reliability — a way to guarantee that packets arrived, and arrived in order. But today I want to zoom out and ask a question that often gets skipped: why did the internet's addressing system look the way it did, and what fundamental problem was it actually solving?

Let's start from first principles.

Imagine you're designing a postal system for a country that doesn't exist yet. You don't know how many cities it will have. You don't know if it'll have ten addresses or ten billion. The temptation is to design for what you know right now — keep it simple, keep it small. That temptation is exactly what created one of the most significant engineering crises in networking history.

When IPv4 was formalized in 1981 through RFC 791, addresses were 32 bits long. That gives you roughly 4.3 billion unique addresses. In 1981, that sounded almost absurdly large. The entire internet was a few hundred machines, mostly at universities and defense contractors. Nobody was thinking about smartphones. Nobody was thinking about thermostats with IP addresses.

The design also made a structural choice called classful addressing. Addresses were divided into Class A, B, and C blocks. A Class A block gave a single organization 16 million addresses. Class B gave 65,000. The logic was clean and hierarchical — but the distribution was catastrophically inefficient. Organizations would request a Class B block when they needed maybe a few thousand addresses, and the rest just sat there, unused, permanently allocated.

By the early 1990s, researchers were doing the math and realizing the address space was going to run out — not in theory, but concretely, within years. This led to two parallel responses.

The short-term fix was CIDR — Classless Inter-Domain Routing — introduced in 1993. CIDR abandoned the rigid class system and allowed addresses to be allocated in precise, variable-sized blocks. It also enabled something called route aggregation, which reduced the size of routing tables that were growing dangerously large. One problem was feeding another.

The long-term fix was IPv6, standardized in 1998, which expanded addresses to 128 bits — giving us 340 undecillion addresses. That's a 3 followed by 38 zeros. Practically inexhaustible.

Here's the counterintuitive insight: the internet didn't fail because of a technical limitation. It nearly failed because of an abundance problem misread as a scarcity solution. Forty billion addresses felt infinite in 1981 — and that feeling of "good enough" is precisely what created the crisis.

Engineers call this kind of failure a success disaster. A system works so well, scales so far beyond its original context, that its early assumptions become structural vulnerabilities.

The lesson isn't to over-engineer everything. It's to ask: what would have to be true for my current assumption to become catastrophically wrong?

We'll pick up with the IPv6 transition — and why it's still incomplete — next time.

The internet feels like magic, but it runs on a surprisingly simple idea — and understanding that idea reveals something most people get backwards about how the web actually works.

Let's start from scratch. It's 1969. The U.S. Department of Defense wants to build a communication network that can survive disruption. The key design challenge isn't speed — it's resilience. How do you send a message when some of the roads between you and the recipient might be destroyed?

The old answer was circuit switching. When you made a phone call, the telephone company physically dedicated a wire path between you and the other person for the entire duration of the call. Reliable, but fragile. Destroy one node and the connection dies.

The new answer was packet switching, developed by engineers including Vint Cerf and Bob Kahn. Here's the core idea. Instead of sending a message as one continuous stream along a reserved path, you chop the message into small chunks — packets — and let each packet find its own way through the network independently. Think of it like mailing a book by tearing out each page, putting each page in a separate envelope, and letting the postal system route each envelope however it wants. They might take different routes, arrive out of order, and some might need to be re-sent. But the book arrives.

This became the Internet Protocol, finalized in the landmark 1974 paper by Cerf and Kahn. IP is essentially the addressing and routing layer — it defines what a packet looks like, where it's going, and where it came from. TCP, which sits on top of IP, handles the reassembly, making sure your pages arrive in the right order and requesting replacements for any that went missing.

Now here's the counterintuitive part. Most people assume the internet is a single, centrally managed network — like a water utility or a phone company. It isn't. The internet is a network of networks. There's no central authority routing your data. Thousands of independent networks — run by universities, corporations, and governments — agree to speak the same language, which is IP, and exchange traffic with each other. The protocol is the agreement, not the infrastructure.

This is why the internet is so difficult to take down. You can't destroy the internet by destroying any particular server or cable, because the protocol doesn't depend on any specific path existing. The routing adapts.

What Cerf and Kahn built wasn't a technology in the traditional sense — it was closer to a treaty. A shared language that lets incompatible systems cooperate without trusting each other or deferring to a central authority.

That's actually the quiet revolution: a global communication system that works precisely because no single entity controls it, built on a 1974 document that fits in a college textbook.

Last time we covered human evaluation and basic automated metrics. Today I want to focus on model-based evaluation, specifically how you can use one LLM to grade another, and where that approach breaks down.

The core problem is this: if you're building a system that generates long-form answers, code explanations, or multi-step reasoning, string-matching metrics like BLEU or ROUGE are nearly useless. A response can be semantically correct and score terribly, or it can be fluent nonsense and score fine. Human evaluation is accurate but expensive and slow. So researchers started asking whether a capable language model could act as a judge.

This approach is called LLM-as-a-judge, and it was formalized in a 2023 paper from LMSYS called "Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena." The setup is straightforward. You present a judge model, typically GPT-4, with a question, a candidate response, and a rubric. The judge outputs a score or a preference. MT-Bench specifically uses 80 multi-turn questions across categories like math, coding, and reasoning, then has GPT-4 score responses on a scale of one to ten.

Here's the concrete workflow. You have your model generate an answer. You construct a prompt that says something like: "You are an expert evaluator. Rate the following response for correctness and clarity on a scale of 1-10. Provide a brief justification." Then you parse the judge's output programmatically. Frameworks like LangChain and LlamaIndex have built-in evaluation modules that structure this pipeline. The library `ragas` does this specifically for retrieval-augmented generation systems, measuring things like faithfulness and answer relevancy by querying a judge model against the retrieved context.

Now, where does this break down? Three specific failure modes worth knowing.

First, position bias. When you show a judge two responses side by side, it systematically favors whichever appears first, roughly 60-70% of the time in controlled tests. The fix is to run the comparison twice with order swapped and only count agreements.

Second, self-enhancement bias. Models tend to rate outputs stylistically similar to their own training data more favorably. GPT-4 as a judge will slightly favor GPT-4 outputs over Claude outputs even when quality is equivalent.

Third, length bias. Judge models reliably prefer longer responses, independent of actual quality. This is documented in the original MT-Bench paper and remains an unsolved problem. If your candidate model learns this through RLHF, it will optimize for verbosity rather than correctness.

The practical takeaway: LLM-as-a-judge is genuinely useful for ranking systems at scale, especially when you calibrate it against a small human-labeled validation set first. Treat it as a noisy signal, not ground truth. Use multiple judges when possible, run Alpaca Eval or MT-Bench as standardized benchmarks rather than building entirely custom pipelines, and always audit cases where judge scores diverge significantly from user feedback.

Next video we'll cover factuality-specific evaluation using tools like FactScore and SAFE.

Most people approach networking backwards. They show up to events, collect business cards, and then wonder why none of it leads anywhere. The problem isn't effort — it's sequence. Today I want to walk you through a framework that actually works, built around one core idea: relationships compound when you give before you ask.

Let's start with step one — getting specific about who you actually want to know. Not "people in marketing" or "founders." Go granular. If you're a product designer, maybe it's senior PMs at B2B SaaS companies with fewer than 200 employees. That specificity matters because it shapes everything downstream — where you spend time, what you say, how you follow up. Researcher David Burkus, in his book "Friend of a Friend," calls this identifying your "dormant ties" and "weak ties" — people you already have some thread of connection to, but haven't activated. These relationships convert at a much higher rate than cold outreach to strangers.

Once you know who you're targeting, step two is creating what I call a value artifact before you reach out. This is something tangible — a short analysis of a trend in their industry, a curated list of resources on a problem they're publicly working on, a thoughtful question rooted in something they've written or said. The goal is to arrive in their inbox as someone who already understands their world. A good benchmark: spend 20 minutes researching someone before sending a five-sentence message. That ratio alone puts you ahead of 90% of outreach they receive.

Step three is building a simple follow-up system, because timing is where most networking dies. Use a tool like Notion or even a basic spreadsheet to track three things: who you've contacted, what you offered or discussed, and when to follow up. A two-week interval after an initial reply, then a six-week interval after that, keeps relationships warm without becoming noise. The follow-up doesn't need to be a check-in — it can be forwarding an article, congratulating them on something they announced, or sharing a relevant update from your own work. This keeps the exchange mutual.

These three steps work because they're sequential, not parallel. You can't create a useful value artifact until you know specifically who you're trying to reach. And you can't maintain relationships without a system, because memory alone fails under any real volume.

The thing most people miss is that networking isn't a campaign — it's a practice. Small consistent actions over months build the kind of trust that leads to actual opportunities.

Here's what you can do today: open a new document and write down five specific people you want to build a relationship with this quarter. Not companies — people. Then for each one, spend ten minutes finding one thing you could offer them before your first message. Start there. That's the whole foundation.
