Mechanisms of evolution What is evolution? Evolution is the development of life on Earth. This is a process that began billions of years ago and is still continuing to this day. Evolution tells us how it was possible for the enormous diversity of life to develop. It shows us how primitive Protozoa could become the millions of different species that we see today. Evolution, then, is the answer to the question that we have all asked on seeing a Daschund and a Great Dane together: how is it possible for ancestors to have descendants that look so very different to them? In answering this question, we want to focus on animals, excluding other forms of life such as fungi and plants. The first question to ask is therefore: how can one animal develop into a whole new species of animal? Ah, but just a quick question: what exactly is a species? A species is a community of animals that is capable of producing offspring with one another, with those offspring also being capable of reproducing in turn. To understand this answer better, we need to take a closer look at the following points: the uniqueness of living creatures, guaranteed through the excess production of offspring and heredity, and as a second key point, selection. Let's begin with uniqueness. Every creature that exists is unique, and this is essential for evolution. The members of a species may strongly resemble each other in appearance; however, they all have slightly different traits and characteristics. They may be a bit bigger, fatter, stronger, or bolder than their fellow animals. So, what is the reason for these differences? Let's take a closer look at a creature. Every creature is made up of cells. These cells have a nucleus. The nucleus contains the chromosomes, and the chromosomes hold the DNA. DNA consists of different genes, and it's these genes that are life's information carriers. They contain instructions and orders for the cells, and determine the characteristics and traits that living creatures have, and it's precisely this DNA that is unique to every creature. It's slightly different from individual to individual, which is why each has slightly different characteristics. But how is the enormous range of DNA created? One key factor is the excess production of offspring. In nature, we can observe that creatures generally produce far more offspring than is necessary for the survival of their species, with many offspring dying an early death as a result. Often there are even more offspring than the environment in which they live is able to support. This is one factor in increasing diversity within a species. The more offspring that are produced, the more little differences occur, and this is what nature wants: as many little differences as possible. The second major cause of the uniqueness of individuals occurs in heredity itself. By the way, heredity means the passing on of DNA to offspring. Two very interesting factors come into play in this process: recombination and mutation. Recombination is the random mixing of the DNA of two creatures. When two creatures fall in love and mate, they recombine their genes twice. The first time, they do this separately when they generate the gametes - that is, sperm and egg cells. The gametes take half of the genes and shuffle them. The second recombination occurs when a male inseminates a female. The parents each provide 50% of their DNA, in other words, 50% of their unique traits and characteristics. These are then recombined, or mixed, and the result is new offspring. These offspring have a random mix of the DNA, and therefore the traits and characteristics of their parents. This increases the diversity and differences within a species even further, but mutations are also important for evolution. Mutations are random changes in DNA. These can also be described as copying errors within the DNA, triggered by toxins or other chemical substances, or by radiation. A mutation exists when part of the DNA is altered. These changes are often negative, and may result in illnesses such as cancer. However, they may also have neutral or positive effects, such as the blue eye colour in humans, which is one such random mutation. In all cases, a mutation has to affect a gamete, that is a sperm or egg cell, because only the DNA in the gametes is passed on to the offspring. This is also the reason why we protect our sexual organs during x-rays, whilst other parts of the body are not at risk. In summary then, in the heredity process, creatures pass on their characteristics to their offspring in the form of DNA. Recombination and mutation change the DNA so that each child looks different to its siblings, and receives a random mix of the characteristics of its parents. There's a key word here: random. All of these processes are based on chance. Random recombination and mutations result in individuals with random mixes of traits and characteristics, which in turn mix these randomly, and pass them on. But how can so much be down to chance, when all living creatures are so perfectly adapted to their environment, for example, the stick insect, the hummingbird, and the frogfish? The answer is provided by the second key point: selection. Each individual is subjected to a process of natural selection. As we have learned, each individual is somewhat different to its fellows, and there is extensive variation within a species. Environmental influences have an effect on living creatures. These so-called selection factors include: predators, parasites, animals of the same species, toxins, changes in habitat, or the climate. Selection is a process that each individual is subjected to. Every creature has a unique mix of traits and characteristics. This mix helps them to survive in their environment, or not, as the case may be. Anyone with an unsuitable mix will be selected from the environment. Those with the right mix survive, and can pass on their enhanced traits and characteristics. This is why diversity is so important. This is why creatures make so much effort to produce offspring that are as different as possible. They increase the likelihood that at least one of their offspring passes nature's selection process. They maximize their chances of survival. A good example of this can be seen in a group of finches living on a remote island. They are some of the most famous animals in the world of science, and are known as Darwin finches, after their discoverer, Charles Darwin, and this is the story of those finches. A few hundred years ago, a small group of finches was blown onto the Galapagos Islands in the middle of the Pacific, probably by a big storm. The finches found themselves in an environment that was completely new to them, a real finch paradise: an abundance of food and no predators. They reproduced rapidly and numerously. The islands were soon heaving with finches. This meant that food supplies became increasingly scarce. The finch paradise was threatened with famine, and finch friends became competitors. This is when selection intervened. Their individuality and small differences, in this case their slightly different beaks, meant that some of the birds were able to avoid competing with their fellow finches. The beaks of some of the finches were more suitable for digging for worms. Other finches were able to use their beaks better for cracking seeds. The finches consequently sort out ecological niches. In these niches, they were safe from excessive competition. They soon began to mate primarily with other finches that used the same niche. Over the course of many generations, these characteristics were enhanced, enabling the finches to exploit their niches successfully. The differences between the worm-diggers and the seed-crackers became so large that they were no longer able to mate with one another. Different species emerged as a result. Today, there are 14 different species of finch living on the Galapagos Islands, all of which are descended from the same group of stranded finches. This is how new species are created by evolution: through the interaction of unique individuals, the excess production of offspring, recombination and mutation in heredity, and finally, through selection. Why is this so important? It tells us where the variety of life comes from, and why living creatures are so perfectly adapted to their habitats. But it also effects us personally. Every person is the result of 3.5 billion years of evolution, and that includes you. Your ancestors fought and adapted in order to survive. This survival was an extremely uncertain thing. If we consider the fact that 99% of all the species that have ever lived are extinct, then you can consider yourself part of a success story. The dinosaurs have disappeared, but you are alive, watching this video, because you're incredibly special, just like all the other creatures that exist today: irreproducible and unique in the universe.

- Lexicographers will tell you that time, the word, "T-I-M-E," is the most used noun in the English language. We can't get through the day without talking about time all the time. I think about how we use time, when we actually talk about it. If you say, "Meet me at 7 p.m.," no one panics. No one says like, "Oh my God, what are you talking about with these esoteric concepts about 7 p.m.?" We all know what to do operationally. Time, in some sense, is just a label on different events in the Universe. The Universe happens over and over again at different things we call moments, and time helps us tell the difference between one moment and another. So what time is, I don't think is the problem. The issue- the real puzzles- come about when we talk about the properties that time has. We have a past, we have a present, we have a future. How are they different? Are we moving through it? We have memories of the past, but we have no memories of the future. Why is that? Where does that asymmetry come from? Why are we all born young? Why do we all inevitably age? Why do we think that we can affect the future but not the past? Could we possibly travel back into it? Anyway, there's a lot of questions about the nature of time that are really confusing and many of them we don't know the answer to, but what time is, I don't think it's one of them. One of the most noticeable features of time is that it has a direction, right? That there's a difference between the past and future. Sometimes we think about this as just an intrinsic feature of reality. Like the past already happened, it's in the books- the future is up for grabs. It hasn't happened yet, and the present is where we live. But then, along comes physics. And what people notice about our best theories of physics is that those theories do not distinguish between the past and the future. But in our everyday lives, nothing is more obvious. It really requires a bit of mental discipline to say, "Well, time could exist without an arrow." And one way of thinking about that is there is no intrinsic arrow of space, but there's still space, okay? We live in a three-dimensional world- up, down, left, right, forward, backward- at the level of the fundamental laws of physics, there's no special direction in space. And how you perceive that is imagine you're an astronaut: you're flying around in your little spacesuit. There wouldn't be any difference between any direction you could look. There's no experiment you could do in physics that would point out a direction in the universe, but space still exists. Likewise, time would still exist even if there wasn't an arrow. But here on Earth, we do have an arrow of space. If I pick up a coffee cup and let it go, it will always fall down. There's clearly a distinction between up and down. No one is tempted to think that's a fundamental feature of the Universe. It's not because downness is embedded in the laws of physics. It's because we live in the vicinity of an influential object-the Earth. The arrow of time is exactly the same way. We in our everyday lives, perceive an arrow of time because we live in the aftermath of an influential event: the Big Bang. And that gets us into a realm of the concept of 'entropy.' Entropy is how messy, how disorganized, how random a system is. When things are nice and neat and tidy, they are low entropy. When they're all messy and all over the place, they're high entropy. And there's a natural tendency of things in the Universe to go from low entropy to high entropy. This is called the 'second law of thermodynamics.' The real question is: Why was the world ever low entropy to begin with? Why was the world lower entropy yesterday than it is today? The explanation is not completely satisfying, to be honest. The explanation is the following: because it was even lower entropy the day before yesterday. And why was the Universe even lower entropy the day before yesterday? Because it was even lower entropy the day before that. And this chain of reasoning goes back 14 billion years to the Big Bang, to the origin of our observable universe; in a hot, dense state, a very low-entropy state, and the Universe has been increasing in entropy ever since. And this is called the 'Past hypothesis' by philosophers- David Albert, who's a philosopher of physics, gave it this name. So now we say, "If you know that the world is made of atoms, and you know what entropy is, in terms of rearranging all those atoms, and you know the past hypothesis- that the entropy of the universe started really low- then you can explain everything that happened after that. There's a way of talking about human life and entropy, which I think is misguided, which is that we should think about life. You know, literally living, being a biological organism, taking in food and everything, as a fight against increasing entropy. I think that's wrong. I think that we owe life to the fact that entropy is increasing, because what would it mean if entropy were not increasing? It would mean that nothing is happening. Nothing interesting is taking place. Without entropy increasing, there's no memory of the past. Without entropy increasing, there's no causal effect that we have on the future. You'd just be in what we call 'thermal equilibrium.' Everything would be the same everywhere. It would be the maximally boring universe. But what we do have as a scientific question is: 'Why do complicated complex structures come into existence at all?' It's clear that they need increasing entropy to exist, because if entropy were already maxed out, there would be no complexity. But that doesn't mean they have to come come into existence. Think about a famous example there: The perfume is all in little bottle. It's in a big room. You open it, and it all floats through the room. The entropy of the perfume increases. But if you think about it, when the perfume is all in the bottle, it's very simple. Once it's all spread through the room it's also very simple. It went from low entropy to high entropy, but it went from simple to simple. It's the journey from the simple, low-entropy starting point to the simple, high-entropy ending point, that there's a large space of possibilities where things can be intricate. There's more perfume here over there. There can be swirls caused by the motion of the wind in the room and so forth. The Universe is just like that. Our Universe started out simple and low entropy. In the future, the stars will die, the black holes will evaporate. It'll be dark, empty, and again, simple, but high entropy. It's in between that things like us- complicated, intricate systems that feed off of the increasing entropy of the Universe- can and do come into existence. We don't know the whole story there. I think it's a very fun, active, scientific research area: Why did complex structures like living beings come into existence and exactly the way we did? What is the role of information? What is the down-to-Earth chemistry that is going on here? What is the geology that is going on here? Could it happen on other planets? Very interesting questions- but one thing I do know is that if entropy weren't increasing along the way, none of it would've come to pass.

Last time we covered what vector embeddings are and why cosine similarity matters. Today we're going deep on how vector databases actually index those embeddings to make search fast, because brute force comparison doesn't scale.

Here's the problem. If you have ten million vectors, each with 1,536 dimensions — which is OpenAI's embedding size — computing the distance from a query vector to every single stored vector takes roughly 30 seconds on a single CPU core. That's unusable. You need results in under 100 milliseconds.

The solution most production vector databases use is called HNSW — Hierarchical Navigable Small World graphs. It was introduced in a 2018 paper by Malkov and Yashunin, and it's worth understanding the intuition before you configure it.

Imagine a layered map. At the top layer, you have maybe 50 nodes spread across the entire data space. These are your coarse landmarks. Each layer below has more nodes, more granular. When you run a search, you enter at the top, find the closest landmark, then descend to the next layer and search more precisely from that neighborhood. You keep descending until you're in the densest layer, where you return your final approximate nearest neighbors.

The key word is approximate. HNSW doesn't guarantee you find the absolute closest vector — it finds vectors that are very close, very fast. The tradeoff between accuracy and speed is controlled by a parameter called `ef` — the exploration factor. A higher `ef` means the algorithm checks more candidates at each layer. In Weaviate and Qdrant, you tune this at query time. In FAISS from Meta, it's called `efSearch`.

Here's a concrete benchmark from the ann-benchmarks repository, which is the standard reference for this. On the SIFT-1M dataset — one million 128-dimensional vectors — HNSW achieves about 1,400 queries per second at 99% recall. Brute force on the same hardware gets 99.9% recall but only 28 queries per second. That's a 50x throughput gain for a one percent accuracy drop. In most retrieval applications, that tradeoff is completely acceptable.

Now where does this matter practically. Suppose you're building a document retrieval system with LangChain and you're using Chroma as your local vector store. Chroma defaults to a brute-force search below a certain collection size, then switches to HNSW automatically. If you're using Pinecone in production, they abstract the index type from you entirely — it's HNSW under the hood but you configure it through their `metric` and `pods` settings.

The thing to actually remember here: when your vector search feels slow, you're probably either using flat brute-force search where you shouldn't be, or your `ef` parameter is too high for the latency you need. Lower `ef` to cut latency, raise it to improve recall. That's the actual lever you control.

Next part, we'll cover product quantization, which is how you compress vectors to reduce memory footprint without destroying recall.

Last time we looked at cosine similarity as our default distance metric. Today I want to challenge that assumption, because depending on your embedding model, you might be leaving significant retrieval accuracy on the table.

Here's the specific problem. OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small and Cohere's embed-v3 models are both trained with different normalization behaviors. When you pull vectors straight from the OpenAI API, they come back already L2-normalized, meaning every vector sits on the unit sphere. In that case cosine similarity and dot product give you mathematically identical results, so the choice doesn't matter. But Cohere's embed-v3 is trained with Matryoshka Representation Learning, which means the model deliberately encodes more important information in the earlier dimensions of the vector.

This changes your retrieval strategy entirely.

With Matryoshka embeddings, you can truncate a 1024-dimensional vector down to 256 dimensions and still get competitive retrieval performance. The MTEB benchmark, maintained by Hugging Face, actually tests this directly. If you look at the MTEB leaderboard for the retrieval task category, Cohere's embed-v3-english scores around 55.0 at full 1024 dimensions, but at 256 dimensions it only drops to roughly 52.8. A competing model without Matryoshka training loses five to six points under the same truncation.

Why does this matter practically? Storage and query latency. If you're running a vector database like Weaviate or Qdrant at scale, cutting your vector size by 75 percent means your index fits in RAM, your ANN search gets faster, and your costs drop. For a retrieval-augmented generation pipeline serving millions of queries daily, that tradeoff is worth measuring.

Here's the concrete thing to test in your own system. Take your document corpus, embed it with your current model at full dimensionality, then embed the same corpus with Cohere embed-v3 at 256 dimensions. Run both indexes against a held-out query set and measure recall at 10. If your documents are domain-specific, like legal contracts or medical records, you may find the full-dimensional generic model loses to the truncated Matryoshka model because the first 256 dimensions of embed-v3 capture semantic structure very efficiently.

One thing to watch: if you're using pgvector in Postgres, it has a dimension limit of 2000, and some newer models like Mistral's embed push toward 4096 dimensions. That's a hard constraint that might force the truncation decision regardless of accuracy.

So the takeaway here is that model selection and distance metric selection are not independent decisions. You need to know whether your model was trained with Matryoshka objectives, whether your vectors are pre-normalized, and what dimensionality your infrastructure can actually handle.

Next part we'll get into re-ranking with cross-encoders, specifically when it's worth the added latency and when it isn't.

What does it mean for a country to have a healthy economy? What does a healthy economy  even look like? Does it look like this? What about like this? Economist Kate Raworth shared a pretty interesting answer to this question on the TED Interview podcast. And it challenges an idea that most economists take for granted. We live— particularly in the West,  particularly in the last 150 years— in a society that has a very strong belief that growth is the sign of progress. And to a certain extent, it’s true. We love to see our kids grow. We love to see nature growing in spring. Growth is a wonderful, healthy phase of life. But in our economies, it’s like we’ve turned to Peter Pan economics— the economy that never wanted to grow up. It wanted to grow and grow and grow forever. And it becomes this permanent phase. But we already know, in our own bodies, in our own lives, that there’s another side to this metaphor of growth that we love so much. If I told you, my friend had gone to the doctor, and the doctor told her she had a growth, that already feels completely different. Because in the space of our own bodies, we know that when something  tries to grow endlessly within this healthy, dynamic living whole, it is a threat to the health of the whole, and we do everything we can to stop it. But when we step into our economies, for some reason, we think that endless growth is progress. And we are now running into severe problems because we are addicted to endless growth. Simon Kuznets, he was asked in the 1930s by US Congress to come up, for the first time, with a single number  to measure the output of the economy. Most databases are built to answer exact questions. You ask for every user where the age column equals 32, and the database scans for that exact value. That works perfectly for structured data. But it completely breaks down when you're trying to find things that are *similar* rather than identical — like finding images that look alike, or documents that mean the same thing in different words.

That's the problem vector databases solve, and it's worth understanding precisely how.

First, you need to understand embeddings. When you run text, an image, or audio through a neural network — something like OpenAI's text-embedding-ada-002 or the open-source model sentence-transformers from Hugging Face — the model converts that input into a list of floating point numbers. A typical embedding might be 1,536 numbers long. That list is a vector. The critical property is that inputs which are semantically similar end up with vectors that are geometrically close together in that high-dimensional space.

So "car" and "automobile" produce vectors that are very nearby. "Car" and "photosynthesis" produce vectors that are far apart. Distance in the vector space corresponds to meaning.

Now the engineering challenge: if you have ten million documents, each represented as a 1,536-dimensional vector, and you want the 10 nearest vectors to a query vector, brute force cosine similarity calculation is too slow for real-time applications. You'd need to compute roughly 15 billion multiplications per query.

Vector databases solve this with approximate nearest neighbor algorithms, commonly called ANN. The most widely used is HNSW — Hierarchical Navigable Small World graphs — introduced in a 2016 paper by Malkov and Yashunin. HNSW builds a layered graph structure during indexing that lets you navigate toward similar vectors quickly, skipping most of the dataset. You trade a small amount of recall accuracy for massive speed gains.

Pinecone, Weaviate, and Qdrant are purpose-built vector databases using this approach. Pgvector is a PostgreSQL extension that adds vector search to a relational database if you'd rather not add a new system to your stack. Facebook's FAISS library is the underlying toolkit many of these build on.

A concrete use case: customer support automation. You embed every historical support ticket and resolution. When a new ticket arrives, you embed that query and retrieve the five most similar past tickets in under 100 milliseconds, even with millions of records. You're not keyword matching — you're matching meaning. Someone asking "my login stopped working" and someone who asked "can't access my account" will match correctly even though they share no words.

The key thing to take away: vector databases aren't magic AI infrastructure. They're an index structure optimized for a specific mathematical operation — nearest neighbor search in high-dimensional space. Understanding that distinction helps you evaluate when you actually need one versus when a keyword search tool like Elasticsearch will do the job more simply.

Last time we talked about how to start conversations at networking events — but getting in the room is only half the job. Today we're focusing on what happens after. How do you turn a brief conversation into an actual professional relationship?

Here's the core problem most people run into: they collect contacts but never convert them. You exchange LinkedIn connections, maybe send a "great to meet you" message, and then nothing. Six months later you need a referral and you're reaching out to a stranger.

The fix is a follow-up system, and it starts within 24 hours.

Step one: send a specific follow-up message. Not "great to meet you" — that's noise. Instead, reference something concrete from your conversation. "You mentioned your team is moving toward async workflows — I came across this article on remote standup alternatives and thought of you." This does two things: it proves you were actually listening, and it gives the other person an easy reason to respond. Research from LinkedIn's own talent blog found that personalized messages get response rates nearly three times higher than generic ones.

Step two: use what's called the "Give Before You Ask" framework. This comes from Adam Grant's work in *Give and Take*, and it maps directly onto networking. Before you ever ask someone for a favor, introduce them to someone useful, share a resource, or offer feedback on their work. Most people treat networking transactionally — they reach out when they need something. If you flip that, you become the person in someone's network they actually want to hear from. Practically, this means keeping a short mental note of what each contact is working on or struggling with. When you see something relevant, send it. It takes two minutes and it compounds over time.

Step three: schedule what I call a "no-agenda check-in" every 90 days for your most important contacts. Not a coffee meeting where you're secretly hoping for a job lead — just a brief message asking how a project is going or what they're focused on lately. Calendar this out. Treat it like a maintenance task. The goal is to stay visible without being transactional. Relationships that go silent for eight months and then suddenly reactivate when someone needs a favor feel hollow to both sides.

These three steps form a loop: a strong follow-up opens the door, consistent giving builds trust, and regular low-stakes check-ins keep the relationship warm without forcing it.

The takeaway you can act on today: look at your last three networking conversations — from an event, a meeting, wherever. Pick one. Last time we covered attention heads and how they let tokens look at each other. Today we're going to go deeper into what actually happens inside a transformer block, specifically the feedforward layer, which gets far less attention than it deserves.

Here's the problem that motivated its design. After the attention mechanism runs, each token has gathered weighted information from other tokens in the sequence. But that information is still kind of raw. The model needs somewhere to actually process and store factual associations. Where does that happen? Not in attention — attention is mostly about routing information. The actual knowledge lives in the feedforward layers.

Each transformer block has two sublayers: the attention sublayer and the feedforward sublayer. The feedforward network, or FFN, takes each token's vector independently — no interaction between tokens here — and passes it through two linear transformations with a nonlinearity in between. In GPT-style models, that nonlinearity is typically GELU. The hidden dimension of this FFN is usually four times the model's embedding dimension. So in GPT-2, with an embedding dimension of 768, the feedforward hidden layer is 3072 units wide.

Why so wide? A 2020 paper by Geva and colleagues, titled "Transformer Feed-Forward Layers Are Key-Value Memories," showed something striking. Each row in the first weight matrix acts like a pattern detector — it fires when the input matches a certain linguistic or factual pattern. The second weight matrix then maps that activation to a specific output vector, which can be interpreted as a memory retrieval. So the FFN is essentially doing a learned lookup: if the token representation matches pattern X, add concept Y to the output.

Here's a concrete example. If you're processing the token "Eiffel" in a sentence, attention might pull in context from surrounding words. But the feedforward layer is where the model might retrieve "located in Paris" or "built in 1889" — associations baked in during training. Researchers at Anthropic and EleutherAI have both run activation patching experiments that support this interpretation.

In PyTorch, this layer is straightforward to implement. You have two linear layers, `nn.Linear(d_model, 4 * d_model)` followed by `nn.Linear(4 * d_model, d_model)`, with a GELU in between. That's it. But those two matrices together hold the majority of parameters in the entire model. In GPT-2 medium, roughly two-thirds of all parameters are in feedforward layers.

So the practical takeaway is this: when you're debugging a transformer or trying to understand why a model knows or doesn't know something, the attention heads tell you what information was retrieved from context. The feedforward layers tell you what the model has memorized from training. They're doing fundamentally different jobs.

Next time we'll look at how layer normalization interacts with both of these components and why its placement — pre-norm versus post-norm — meaningfully affects training stability.

Last time we covered how attention lets a model look at all input tokens simultaneously and compute weighted relevance scores. Today we're going one level deeper: multi-head attention, and specifically why using a single attention function is actually a bottleneck.

Here's the problem. When you compute attention with a single set of query, key, and value matrices, you're forcing the model to blend all its "reasons for attending" into one representation. A word like "bank" in a sentence might need to simultaneously track syntactic role, semantic meaning, and positional context. A single attention head tries to capture all of that at once, and it compresses information in ways that lose resolution.

The solution introduced in the Vaswani et al. 2017 paper, "Attention Is All You Need," is to run multiple attention heads in parallel. Here's how it works mechanically.

Instead of one set of Q, K, V matrices of dimension d-model, you split into h heads, each operating on a lower-dimensional subspace of size d-model divided by h. In the original Transformer, d-model is 512 and h is 8, so each head works in 64 dimensions. Each head learns its own projection matrices, runs scaled dot-product attention independently, and produces its own output. Those outputs are then concatenated and passed through one final linear layer.

The key insight is that different heads specialize. If you visualize attention weights using something like BertViz, which is an open-source tool from Jesse Vig, you'll see this concretely. One head might strongly attend to the subject of a clause when processing the verb. Another head might track coreference relationships, linking pronouns back to their antecedents several tokens earlier. A third might attend to adjacent tokens for local syntactic structure. None of this is explicitly programmed. It emerges from training.

Here's a quick way to think about it in code terms. In PyTorch, torch.nn.MultiheadAttention handles all of this for you. You specify embed-dim and num-heads, and it internally manages the projection matrices, the parallel attention computations, and the final output projection. The API makes it easy to miss that you're running eight separate attention functions simultaneously.

Where this matters practically: in BERT, researchers from Google AI analyzed what individual attention heads learn and found heads that reliably track direct objects, heads sensitive to sentence boundaries, and heads that appear to detect passive voice constructions. This is documented in the Clark et al. 2019 paper, "What Does BERT Look at?"

So when you see "12 attention heads" or "16 attention heads" in a model card, that's not an arbitrary number. It's a capacity decision about how many independent relational patterns the model can track simultaneously.

Next time we'll look at how attention scales with sequence length and why that became the central engineering problem behind sparse attention methods like Longformer and BigBird.

LinkedIn's algorithm treats your profile like a search engine result — and most people are optimizing for the wrong thing. Let me walk you through how to actually fix that.

Start with your headline. The default pulls your job title, which is almost always too vague to be useful. Instead, use what I call the "Value + Audience" formula: lead with what you do, then name who you do it for, then hint at the outcome. So instead of "Marketing Manager at Acme Corp," you write something like "Helping B2B SaaS companies turn content into qualified pipeline." That's specific enough that the right person reading it immediately knows whether you're relevant to them. LinkedIn's own internal data shows profiles with keyword-rich, role-specific headlines appear in up to 40% more search results than those using default job titles.

Now, that headline only works if your About section follows through. Most people treat it like a resume summary — third person, chronological, dry. Don't do that. Your About section should answer one question from the reader's perspective: "Why does this person's work matter to someone like me?" Open with the problem you solve, not your background. Your background can come in the second paragraph as proof. Think of it as a two-paragraph argument: here's the problem, here's why I'm the one to talk to about it.

The third piece is what actually drives inbound, and almost nobody does this deliberately — it's your Featured section combined with consistent content. Here's how these work together. Your Featured section is pinned real estate at the top of your profile, so link something that shows your thinking: a detailed LinkedIn post that performed well, a case study, a short Loom video walking through a project. Then your content strategy reinforces it. You don't need to post every day. Research from the Content Marketing Institute found that professionals who post even once or twice a week with substantive, specific content — not motivational fluff — see three to four times more profile views than those who post sporadically or not at all. The key word is substantive. Write posts that take a real position, share a specific lesson from something that happened, or break down a process step by step.

Here's how these three things connect: your headline gets you found, your About section converts the visitor into someone who wants to engage, and your Featured section plus content proves that what you said in your headline is real. Each layer does a different job, but they only work if they're consistent with each other. If your headline says you're a demand generation expert and your content is about personal productivity, you're sending mixed signals and you'll attract the wrong people — or nobody.

The one thing you can do today: rewrite your headline using the Value plus Audience formula. Open LinkedIn right now, look at your current headline, and ask yourself — if a stranger read this with no context, would they know exactly what you do and who you serve? If the answer is no, that's your starting point.

Last time we talked about how a compiler takes your source code and breaks it into tokens — a process called lexical analysis. Today we go one level deeper: parsing, and what compilers actually *do* with the structure of your code.

So you have a stream of tokens. The question is: what do they *mean*? And this is where something beautiful happens.

Compilers build what's called an Abstract Syntax Tree, or AST. Think of it like this. If I give you the sentence "the cat sat on the mat," you don't store it as a flat list of words. Your brain immediately organizes it — subject, verb, prepositional phrase. You extract *structure*. Parsers do exactly that with code.

Take a simple expression: 3 plus 4 times 5. As a flat token list, that's ambiguous. Is it 3 plus 4, then times 5? Or 3 plus the result of 4 times 5? Mathematically those give different answers — 35 versus 23. The parser resolves this using grammar rules, encoding operator precedence directly into the tree structure. Multiplication becomes a child node of addition, not a sibling. The tree *is* the meaning.

This idea of formal grammars comes largely from Noam Chomsky's 1956 work on the hierarchy of formal languages. Computer scientists immediately recognized that programming languages could be described using what Chomsky called context-free grammars, and that insight became the theoretical foundation of every parser you've ever used.

Now here's where it gets counterintuitive.

Most programmers assume the compiler's job is to translate their code into machine instructions. And eventually, yes. But the intermediate representation — the AST — is often *more powerful* than either the source code or the final binary.

Because once you have a tree, you can do things to it. You can walk the tree and check types without executing anything. You can transform the tree — rewrite a slow pattern into a faster one. This is what optimization actually is: not magic, but tree surgery. When your compiler "unrolls a loop" or "inlines a function," it's manipulating nodes in a graph.

This is also why languages like Lisp, designed in 1958 by John McCarthy, are philosophically fascinating. Lisp's source code *is* already a tree — those nested parentheses aren't syntax noise, they're the AST written down directly. McCarthy collapsed the distance between code and data structure entirely. When Lisp programmers say "code is data," they mean it literally.

So the non-obvious insight is this: the most important thing a compiler produces isn't the binary. It's the intermediate representation. That's where understanding lives. The binary is just the shadow the tree casts onto hardware.

Next time, we'll look at how semantic analysis uses that tree to enforce meaning — catching errors that have nothing to do with syntax but everything to do with intent.

Welcome back. If you've been following along, we've covered researching the company, answering behavioral questions, and handling compensation conversations. Today we're going into the part most people completely skip — what you do in the 48 hours after the interview.

Here's why this matters. A 2019 survey by TopInterview found that 68% of hiring managers say a thank-you note influences their hiring decision, yet fewer than half of candidates actually send one. That gap is your opportunity.

So let's build a post-interview system that works in three steps.

Step one: Send a strategic thank-you email within 24 hours — not a generic one. Most people write something like "Thanks so much for your time, I really enjoyed our conversation." That's forgettable. Instead, reference something specific from the actual interview. If the hiring manager mentioned that the team is struggling to hit Q3 pipeline targets, your email should acknowledge that directly. Something like: "After our conversation about the pipeline challenge, I kept thinking about the segmentation approach we discussed — I actually used something similar at my last role and saw a 20% improvement in qualified leads within two quarters." You're not bragging. You're reminding them why you're the answer to their specific problem.

Step two: Audit your answers while they're still fresh. Within a few hours of leaving the interview, write down the two or three questions that felt shaky. Don't judge yourself — just document them. Then use the STAR method — Situation, Task, Action, Result — to rebuild those answers properly. This serves two purposes. If you get a second round, you'll be sharper. And over time, you're building a personal answer library that makes every future interview easier. Treat each interview as data, not just an outcome.

Step three: Follow up strategically if you don't hear back. The standard advice is "follow up after a week," but that's vague. Here's a more specific structure — if they gave you a decision timeline, wait until two days after that date, then send a brief, confident check-in. Not anxious, not apologetic. Something like: "I wanted to follow up since we'd discussed a decision around this time. I'm still very interested in the role and happy to provide any additional information that would be helpful." That framing positions you as organized and genuinely interested, not desperate.

These three steps work together as a system. The thank-you email reinforces your fit, the answer audit improves your process, and the follow-up keeps you visible without being annoying. Each one compounds the others.

Here's what you can do today, even if you don't have an interview scheduled: draft a thank-you email template with two blank lines — one for a specific detail from the conversation, one for a relevant result from your experience. Build the structure now so you're not scrambling to write it in a parking lot after your next interview.

Making a career transition isn't about starting over. It's about identifying which of your existing skills transfer — and then filling only the gaps that actually matter.

Let's talk about how to do that systematically.

The first step is to run what career researcher Jenny Blake calls a "Skills Audit." Instead of looking at your job titles, you look at the actual activities you've spent the most time doing — writing, analyzing data, managing people, building systems, whatever it is. Write down your top ten activities across your entire work history. Then, look at the job descriptions in the field you're moving into and highlight the overlapping language. Most people are shocked by how much overlap exists. A teacher moving into instructional design, for example, shares roughly 70 percent of core competencies — the gap is mostly technical tooling, not foundational ability.

That brings us to step two: closing the right gaps, not all the gaps. This is where people waste the most time. They go back to school, they collect certifications, they spend a year preparing when they needed six weeks. Once you've done your skills audit, you can see clearly what's actually missing. If you're moving into UX design and you already understand user psychology and communication, you probably need to learn one or two specific tools — Figma, maybe some basic user research methodology — and build a portfolio of three to five projects. That's a targeted, bounded effort. Compare that to getting a full degree in human-computer interaction, which adds years and debt to solve a problem that didn't require that solution.

Step three is what I'd call building proof before you make the leap. Credentials tell someone you completed a program. Proof shows them you can do the work. The most effective career transitioners spend time creating concrete artifacts — a case study, a side project, a piece of writing, a design sprint done for a nonprofit or a friend's business. LinkedIn data from 2022 showed that candidates with portfolio evidence in adjacent career moves were significantly more likely to get interviews than those with only formal qualifications. You're essentially giving hiring managers a way to say yes with confidence before you've officially made the transition.

These three steps compound on each other. The audit tells you where you actually are. The gap analysis tells you exactly what to build. And the proof gives you something tangible to show before your resume catches up to your new direction.

Here's what you can do today: open a document and list ten things you've actually done at work — not your job title, not your responsibilities, the real activities. Then pull up two or three job postings in the field you're curious about. Look for the language that already matches yours. That overlap is your foundation. Everything else is just building from there.

Last time we covered attention heads and how they let the model focus on different parts of the input sequence simultaneously. Today we're going into feedforward layers — specifically why they exist and what they're actually doing computationally.

Here's the problem attention alone doesn't solve. Attention is essentially a routing mechanism. It decides which tokens should influence which other tokens, and it aggregates information accordingly. But routing information around doesn't transform it. You can pass the right context to the right place, but you still need something that actually processes that context and extracts meaning from it. That's where the feedforward sublayer comes in.

Every transformer block has two main components: the multi-head attention layer, and immediately after it, a feedforward network. In the original "Attention Is All You Need" paper from Vaswani et al., this feedforward network is remarkably simple — just two linear transformations with a ReLU activation in between. In PyTorch terms, you're looking at a Linear layer, a ReLU or GELU activation, then another Linear layer. The hidden dimension is typically four times the model's embedding dimension. So in GPT-2 small, where the embedding dimension is 768, the feedforward hidden size is 3072.

Now here's what makes this interesting. Research from Geva et al. in 2021, specifically the paper "Transformer Feed-Forward Layers Are Key-Value Memories," showed that these feedforward layers behave like lookup tables. The first linear projection acts as a key matrix — each row corresponds to a pattern the layer has learned to recognize. When the input activates certain neurons strongly, the second linear projection retrieves associated values, which are essentially distributional facts about language stored during training.

The practical implication is significant. When researchers at Anthropic and EleutherAI analyze which model components are responsible for factual recall — knowing that Paris is the capital of France, for example — it's overwhelmingly the feedforward layers, not the attention heads. Attention handles syntax and token relationships. Feedforward layers handle semantics and world knowledge.

You can verify this yourself with TransformerLens, a library built by Neel Nanda specifically for mechanistic interpretability. You can hook into individual feedforward neurons on any GPT-2 or GPT-Neo checkpoint, find neurons that fire strongly on specific concepts, and ablate them to watch model outputs degrade in predictable ways.

So to summarize the structure concretely: attention moves information between positions, feedforward networks transform information at each position. Both are needed. A model with only attention would route context perfectly but couldn't encode factual knowledge. A model with only feedforward layers would have rich semantic representations but no way to integrate context across a sentence.

Next video we'll look at layer normalization — specifically the difference between Pre-LN and Post-LN architectures and why that choice affected training stability enough that most modern models switched to Pre-LN by default.

Welcome back. In the last three parts we covered your headline, your about section, and your experience descriptions. Last time we compared embedding models on semantic similarity tasks. Today we're going deeper — specifically looking at how these models perform when your retrieval task involves longer documents and asymmetric queries.

Here's the problem. Most benchmarks test embedding models on sentence pairs of roughly equal length. But in production RAG systems, you're almost never doing that. You're embedding a short user query — maybe eight words — and retrieving against document chunks that are three hundred words long. The model has to understand that "what's the refund policy" should match a paragraph that never uses the word "refund" but describes return windows, eligibility conditions, and processing times. That's asymmetric retrieval, and it exposes real differences between models that look similar on standard leaderboards.

Let's look at MTEB, the Massive Text Embedding Benchmark from Hugging Face. If you sort by average score, you'll see models like text-embedding-3-large from OpenAI and E5-mistral-7b from Microsoft clustered near the top. But MTEB has a specific subset called BeIR — the Benchmarking Information Retrieval benchmark — and that's where asymmetric performance shows up. When you filter to BeIR tasks only, the rankings shift. E5-mistral-7b actually outperforms text-embedding-3-large on several dense retrieval tasks, particularly on the TREC-COVID and NFCorpus datasets, which have highly technical, domain-specific documents.

Why does this happen? E5-mistral uses instruction-tuned prefixes. You prepend your query with something like "Instruct: Retrieve relevant passages for this question. Query:" and that conditioning changes how the model encodes the input. It's essentially telling the model this is the asymmetric side of the retrieval pair. text-embedding-3-large doesn't use this mechanism — it encodes everything symmetrically. For short factual queries against long technical documents, that instruction prefix gives E5-mistral a measurable edge.

Now, practical application. If you're building with LangChain or LlamaIndex, switching between these models is straightforward. In LlamaIndex you instantiate your embedding model with HuggingFaceEmbedding and pass in the model name and the instruction prefix as a separate parameter. In OpenAI's case, you're calling their embeddings endpoint — no prefix needed, nothing to configure. The tradeoff is deployment complexity versus performance on your specific data distribution.

The right choice depends on what you're actually retrieving. If your documents are relatively short and your queries are full sentences — think customer reviews — text-embedding-3-large is simpler and performs well. If you're building a technical documentation search, a medical literature retrieval system, or anything with long, dense chunks, benchmark on BeIR categories that match your domain before you commit.

Run your own evaluation. Take two hundred real queries from your users, retrieve against your actual corpus, and measure recall at ten. That number tells you more than any leaderboard average.

Next part, we'll look at multilingual embedding models and where they break down.

Last time we covered feature pipelines and training infrastructure. Today we're going to talk about one of the most underestimated problems in production ML: training-serving skew.

Here's the problem. You train a model, it looks great on your validation set, you deploy it, and performance degrades immediately. Not because of concept drift, not because of bad data — the model is literally receiving different numbers than it was trained on. Same raw input, different features. This is training-serving skew, and it's responsible for a significant portion of unexplained model regressions in production systems.

Let me make this concrete. Say you're building a fraud detection model at a company like Stripe or a smaller fintech. During training, you compute a feature: the average transaction amount for a user over the last 30 days. You write a pandas function that pulls historical data from a data warehouse, computes this rolling average, and feeds it into your training pipeline. Works perfectly in notebooks.

In production, you need that same feature in real time. An engineer rebuilds the logic in Java for your serving layer. The pandas version handled nulls by defaulting to zero. The Java version returns the mean of available values, ignoring the null entirely. These are now two different features wearing the same name. Your model was never trained on what it's actually seeing.

The solution that serious ML teams use is a feature store. Tools like Feast, Tecton, and Hopsworks are built specifically to enforce consistency between training and serving. The core idea is you define feature computation logic once, in a single place, and both your training pipeline and your serving infrastructure call that same definition. Tecton, for example, lets you write feature transformations in Python, and it handles materializing those features to both an offline store — typically something like S3 or BigQuery — for training, and an online store — typically Redis or DynamoDB — for low-latency serving.

The application looks like this. In training, you call something like `feature_store.get_historical_features(entity_df, feature_refs)`. In serving, you call `feature_store.get_online_features(entity_rows, feature_refs)`. Same feature refs, same transformation logic, guaranteed consistency.

There's a 2022 paper from Meta called "Monolith: Real Time Recommendation System With Collisionless Embedding Table" that describes how they handle feature consistency at scale — worth reading if you want to see what this looks like when the stakes are high and the traffic is enormous.

One practical check even without a feature store: log your features at serving time and compare their statistical distributions to your training set weekly. Mean, variance, null rates. If those distributions diverge, you have a problem. This isn't a perfect solution, but it catches skew before it silently destroys model performance.

Training-serving skew is boring to talk about. It's also one of the first things senior ML engineers check when a deployed model underperforms. Now you know why.

In many workplaces, there are rules that are completely unspoken. Understanding them is crucial for success, and yet you won't find them in your employee handbook. Unless you're lucky enough to have a mentor or manager who pulls you aside to help, you just kind of have to figure them out on your own. [The Way We Work] Starting a new job can feel like starting at a new school. You're meeting new people, you're getting thrown into unfamiliar situations, and you’re constantly wondering: What does everyone think of me? But whether you're starting your first job ever or just starting a new role, making a great impression doesn't have to be so scary. It all comes down to deciphering those unspoken rules and demonstrating what I call the three Cs: competence, commitment and compatibility. The first C is competence. On day one, your new colleagues are probably looking at you and thinking: Can you do your job? The way to convince others, not to mention yourself, that yes, you can, is to show your homework and to give others something to react to. Let’s say it’s your first day, and it’s utter chaos. People are forwarding you messages with nothing more than a "See below" at the top and pulling you into meetings without any notice or context. Your manager just gave you a task, and two minutes in you're already confused. Instead of going back to them right away and saying, “I don’t know what to do next, help!” Or doing what I did, which is not asking questions at all, think about a similar task that you tried in a previous job, in school or just for yourself. What steps did you take then? Then make your best guess and just give it a try. Then go back to your manager and say, “I’m working on X, and I'm not quite sure of what to do next. I've taken X steps and spoken to so-and-so and I understand this part, but I'm still unclear about this other part. What do you think about this approach?" No, I'm not suggesting that you fake it till you make it, but what I am suggesting is that you try until you can't try anymore. Time matters, though, so give yourself a limit. And depending on your deadline, figuring things out on your own. Then ask and then learn from what others tell you. It's your first bit of knowledge around how things work here. The second C is commitment. Besides looking to see if you can do your job, your colleagues are also looking to see if you're dedicated. During your first week in a new job, try asking your manager questions about expectations. My favorites are one, what are my top priorities in this role? Two, how would you like to communicate day-to-day and week-to-week? And three, what have your highest performers done that you'd suggest that I do too? For your peers, try to go more casual. In your first few days, your manager will be probably, or well, at least hopefully, taking you around and introducing you to your other teammates. In those moments, think about how you can transform some of those "Hi's" into conversations and then those conversations into relationships. One option is to ask how others' weeks are going. For those you really get along with, try involving them in your work and maybe even your life with phrases like, “Oh, I’d love to get your input on A.” Or “Could I get your advice on B?” These can all go a long way in deepening a relationship. The third C, it's compatibility. Every time a new person joins a team, the others on it want to know: Will you get along with us? By initiating conversations, you're already expressing compatibility. But while you're at it, use this time to try and identify who's in charge of what or what I call “invisible swim lanes.” Let's say you've just started a new remote job. First, ask your manager if there's an org chart. It's a diagram showing who reports to whom. And if there is one, amazing. But formal job titles and reporting lines can only tell you so much about how things really work. So when you're in meetings and group chats, pay attention. What's each person's role? Who reports to whom? Who's on whose side, who commands respect, which behaviors get rewarded and which behaviors get punished? Understanding these norms, loyalties, power brokers and swim lanes is what people are really referring to when they say that it’s important to understand a team’s “culture.” And by the way, while you're mapping out these swim lanes, look around to see if there's any swim lane that might be unoccupied or in other words, what tasks might be available that aren't currently being done that can help your team accomplish more, better, faster, cheaper, safer, more securely. By doing this, you'll show that you're competent enough to spot gaps, committed enough to fill them and compatible enough to not overstep. Here’s the reality of starting a new job. Doing your job is only part of it. The rest is about how you get along with other people. But as much as we've been talking about others sizing you up, don't forget to do the same. Is this an environment where you can flourish and do your best work? Can you see yourself getting along with your coworkers? Is this even work you're excited to do? Every job is a two-way street. You can do well only if you want to do well. And if you're set up to do well. So look around, reflect and be honest with yourself about whether this is a good place for you.

Today we're going deeper — specifically into how you maintain and activate your network over time, because most people are pretty good at making initial connections and terrible at everything that comes after.

Let's start with what researcher David Burkus calls "dormant tie reactivation." His research shows that reconnecting with someone you've lost touch with — a former colleague, an old classmate — often produces more value than reaching out to a stranger. Why? Because you already have established trust, you just haven't used it recently. The relationship has history built in. So your first concrete step is to identify three dormant ties this week. Not to ask for anything — just to genuinely check in. Reference something specific: a project you worked on together, a conversation you remember. That specificity signals that the relationship mattered to you.

Now, once you've re-engaged someone, the second step is creating what I'd call a lightweight touchpoint system. This doesn't mean a CRM or a spreadsheet with color-coded follow-up dates — that turns people into tasks. Instead, it means building small habits. When you read an article that reminds you of someone, send it to them with one sentence about why it made you think of them. When someone in your network gets promoted or publishes something, acknowledge it directly, not just with a LinkedIn reaction. These micro-interactions compound. Over six months they're what separates a warm network from a cold one.

The third step is about reciprocity architecture — setting yourself up to give before you need anything. A practical way to do this is to become a connector. When you notice two people in your network who would genuinely benefit from knowing each other, make the introduction. Write a double opt-in email: ask both parties separately if they'd like to be introduced, then send a warm note when they both say yes. This does two things. It delivers real value to both people, and it positions you as someone who makes things happen. That reputation travels.

Here's how these three steps connect: dormant ties give you a warm starting pool, consistent touchpoints keep relationships alive between meaningful moments, and proactive introductions make you genuinely useful rather than someone who only shows up when they need a favor.

The thing to take away today is simple. Look at your phone contacts or LinkedIn right now and find one person you haven't spoken to in over a year but genuinely respected. Send them a message today — not a pitch, not a vague "let's catch up," but something specific. Reference a real moment or something you know they care about. That's it. One message. That's where this starts.

In part one, we covered the basics of retrieval-augmented generation — embedding your documents, storing them in a vector database, and pulling relevant chunks into the prompt. Today I want to address the most common failure mode people run into after that initial setup: retrieval quality.

Here's the problem. Your system works fine on simple queries, but when users ask something ambiguous or multi-part, the retrieved chunks are technically related to the question but don't actually contain the answer. The model either hallucinates or says it doesn't know. The embedding similarity score looks fine, but the output is wrong.

This happens because dense retrieval — matching query embeddings to document embeddings — is good at semantic similarity but struggles with exact terms, rare keywords, and negation. If a user asks "what are the limitations of BERT," your vector search might return documents about BERT's strengths because those documents are semantically close to BERT as a topic.

The standard solution used in production systems is hybrid retrieval. You combine dense retrieval with sparse retrieval, specifically BM25, which is a keyword-based ranking algorithm that's been around since the 1990s. BM25 scores documents based on term frequency and inverse document frequency. It's not semantic, but it's extremely precise for exact matches.

In practice, you run both retrieval methods in parallel, then merge the results using a technique called Reciprocal Rank Fusion. Each document gets a score based on its rank in each retrieval list, and those scores are added together. The formula is simple: for each document, you sum one divided by k plus rank, where k is usually 60. Documents that appear near the top of both lists score highest.

LangChain has an EnsembleRetriever class that implements this directly. You pass it your vector store retriever and a BM25Retriever, set the weights, and it handles the fusion. Elasticsearch and Weaviate both support hybrid search natively with their own implementations.

Now here's where it gets more practical. The BEIR benchmark — that's Benchmarking Information Retrieval — tested 18 different retrieval methods across 18 datasets. Pure dense retrieval with models like OpenAI's text-embedding-ada-002 outperforms BM25 on several datasets, but BM25 still wins on others, particularly those with technical terminology. Hybrid consistently sits at or near the top across the full benchmark. That's the key result: neither method dominates alone.

One more thing worth adding: after you retrieve your chunks, consider adding a reranking step. Cohere's Rerank API and cross-encoder models from Hugging Face, like cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2, take your top twenty retrieved chunks and re-score them using a more expensive but more accurate model. You then pass only the top three or four to the language model. This cuts down on noise in the context window substantially.

So the production RAG pipeline looks like this: hybrid retrieval, reciprocal rank fusion, reranking, then generation. Each step is independently improvable, and each one has measurable impact on answer quality.

In the last three parts of this series, we covered how to identify transferable skills, how to reframe your resume for a new industry, and how to start building credibility before you've made the switch. So the first step is what researchers at Stanford call "informed curiosity." Before you reach out to anyone, go deep on one specific topic in your target field. Read two or three recent industry reports. Follow a handful of practitioners on LinkedIn and actually read what they post. Your goal is to develop a genuine, specific question — not "how did you get into this field?" but something like "I noticed most job descriptions in UX research now ask for SQL skills — is that actually being used day-to-day or is it more of a filter?"

That specificity changes the entire dynamic. You're not asking someone to mentor you. You're inviting a conversation between two people who are both thinking about the same thing.

Second step — when you do get a conversation, resist the urge to pitch yourself. Use the first fifteen minutes to listen. Ask about the friction in their day-to-day work. Where do things break down? What does the team always wish they had more of? This does two things: it gives you real intelligence about what actually matters in that role, and it lets you naturally surface your relevant background in context. Not as a sales pitch, but as a connection. "That's interesting — in logistics we dealt with something similar by doing X" lands completely differently than "here's why I'd be great at your job."

Third step — follow up with something tangible within 48 hours. Send a short note, but attach something useful. An article related to what you discussed. A brief summary of a framework you mentioned. Even a relevant job posting you noticed, if it's not the one you're targeting. This positions you as someone who gives value, not just someone who's collecting favors.

A former teacher I know used exactly this approach to move into instructional design at a tech company. Three informational conversations over six weeks, each one better than the last because she kept refining her questions. She had a job offer before she ever applied through a formal posting.

Your takeaway for today: identify one person working in your target role, spend thirty minutes researching something specific about their field, and send them one precise, curious question. Not a favor request. Just a real question. That's the whole job today.

In parts one and two, we established that entangled particles share a quantum state, and that measuring one particle instantly determines something about its partner, regardless of distance. Today I want to go deeper into what that actually means, because the naive interpretation leads most people to a wrong conclusion.

Let's start with what you might naturally assume. If measuring particle A instantly affects particle B, couldn't you use that to send a message faster than light? It seems obvious. Alice measures her particle, Bob's particle responds. Communication achieved. This intuition is reasonable, and it was taken seriously enough that physicists had to work through it carefully.

Here's why it fails, and this is the key insight of today's video.

When Alice measures her particle, she gets a random result. Spin up or spin down, with no control over which one. Bob's particle then takes the correlated state, but Bob also just sees a random result on his end. Neither of them chose what happened. The randomness is fundamental, not a limitation of their instruments.

Think of it this way. Imagine you and a friend each take one glove from a pair, seal them in boxes, and travel to opposite sides of the world. When you open your box and find the left glove, you instantly know your friend has the right glove. Information about their glove reached you at the speed of thought, not the speed of light, because the correlation was established when you packed the boxes.

Classical entanglement is like that. But quantum entanglement is stranger, and this is where the analogy breaks down productively.

In the glove scenario, the gloves always had definite identities. The quantum version, confirmed experimentally through Bell test experiments, most famously by Alain Aspect's team in 1982, shows that the particles did not have definite states before measurement. The correlation isn't pre-written. It gets determined at the moment of measurement. This was the finding that ruled out what Einstein preferred, which was that hidden variables were carrying the information all along.

So we have a genuine non-local correlation, but one that contains no usable signal. Bob's results look completely random until he compares notes with Alice through a conventional channel. Only then do the correlations become visible. This is sometimes called the no-communication theorem, and it's not a loophole or a technicality. It's structural. The randomness that makes quantum mechanics non-local is the same randomness that prevents it from being used for faster-than-light signaling.

The counterintuitive conclusion is this: reality can be non-local and still be consistent with relativity, as long as the non-locality carries no information. Nature threaded that needle precisely.

You've built an LLM application, and it seems to work. But "seems to work" is not a measurement. Today we're going to look at how practitioners actually evaluate language models in a rigorous way.

The core problem is that LLM outputs are strings. Unlike classification models where you compare a predicted label to a ground truth label, a generated sentence can be correct in dozens of different phrasings. You can't just run an equality check. So we need methods designed specifically for this problem.

Let's start with the oldest approach: reference-based metrics. BLEU score, developed at IBM in 2002, compares an output against one or more human-written reference answers by counting overlapping word sequences called n-grams. If your model says "the cat sat on the mat" and your reference says "the cat rested on the mat," BLEU will penalize that because "sat" and "rested" don't overlap, even though the meaning is equivalent. BLEU is still used in machine translation benchmarks, but for open-ended generation it's largely inadequate because it's entirely surface-level.

A better approach for semantic similarity is embedding-based evaluation. BERTScore, introduced in a 2020 paper by Zhang et al., encodes both the candidate and reference sentences using a pretrained model like RoBERTa, then computes cosine similarity between the token embeddings. This captures that "sat" and "rested" are semantically close. It's not perfect, but it correlates better with human judgments than BLEU on most tasks.

Now, the method getting the most traction in production systems right now is LLM-as-a-judge. You pass the model's output to a separate, typically more capable model, like GPT-4 or Claude, and ask it to score the response on dimensions like accuracy, coherence, or relevance. The Alpaca Eval benchmark and MT-Bench from LMSYS both use this approach. The practical upside is that it scales and handles open-ended tasks where there is no single reference answer. The downside is well-documented: LLM judges have positional bias, favoring responses listed first, and verbosity bias, favoring longer answers even when they're less accurate. Research from the paper "Judging the Judges" published in 2024 quantified these failure modes systematically.

In practice, teams at companies like Anthropic and Cohere use a combination. Automated metrics like BERTScore or LLM-judging handle the bulk of evaluation across thousands of examples, and human evaluation is reserved for targeted samples where you need ground truth on ambiguous cases.

The tool you should know is RAGAS if you're evaluating retrieval-augmented systems specifically. It gives you metrics like faithfulness, which checks whether claims in the answer are actually supported by the retrieved documents, which is something neither BLEU nor BERTScore can tell you.

The takeaway is this: pick your evaluation method based on your task structure. Reference-based metrics work when answers are constrained. Embedding similarity works for semantic tasks. LLM-as-a-judge works for open-ended quality, but you need to audit the judge itself for bias.

The human brain is divided into three major parts: - The cerebrum - the largest part of the human brain. The cerebrum enables sensory perception and controls voluntary motor actions. - The cerebellum – the cerebellum lies inferior to the cerebrum at the back of the head. It is mostly involved in coordination of movement and fine tuning of motor activities. - The brainstem - the brainstem is located at the base of the brain and is continuous to the spinal cord. It houses all nerve connections between different parts of the central nervous system. The brainstem provides innervations to the head and neck via cranial nerves. It also contains nuclei associated with important body functions such as regulation of blood pressure, respiration, swallowing, bladder control, sleep cycle, among others. On top of the brainstem, and sometimes classified as part of it, is the diencephalon. The main components of the diencephalon are: - The thalamus – the thalamus serves as a gateway relaying sensory signals originated throughout the body to the cerebral cortex. It is also involved in emotional and memory functions. - The hypothalamus – the hypothalamus is the major control center of the autonomic nervous system and plays essential role in homeostatic regulation. The hypothalamus links the nervous system to the endocrine system via the pituitary gland. It also contains nuclei involved in regulation of body temperature, food and water intake, sleep and wake cycle, memory and emotional behavior. The cerebrum consists of two cerebral hemispheres. The left hemisphere controls the right half of the body. The right hemisphere controls the left half of the body. The two hemispheres are separated by a deep groove called the longitudinal fissure. Each hemisphere has a number of folds called gyri separated by grooves called sulci. A major landmark is the central sulcus. The cerebrum has four major lobes. The frontal lobe is situated anterior to the central sulcus. It is associated mainly with voluntary motor functions, planning, motivation, emotion and social judgment. Posterior to the central sulcus is the parietal lobe. This lobe is mainly concerned with sensory functions of the somatosensory category such as touch, stretch, movement, temperature and pain. The temporal lobe is separated from the frontal and parietal lobes by the lateral sulcus. The temporal lobe is associated with hearing, learning, visual memory and language. The occipital lobe is located at the rear of the cerebrum. This is the visual processing center of the brain. At first glance, the two hemispheres look identical, but research has found a number of differences between them. This is called lateralization of brain function. For example, the language formation areas - the Wernicke’s and Broca’s areas - are usually located in the left hemisphere of right-handed people. Lesions to these areas result in language comprehension deficits or speech disorders. The corresponding areas in the right hemisphere are responsible for emotional aspect of language. Lesions to these areas do not affect speech comprehension and formation, but result in emotionless speech and inability to understand the emotion behind the speech such as sarcasm or a joke.
