Instead, they begin at random, meaning the model just outputs gibberish, but they're repeatedly refined based on many example pieces of text. One of these training examples could be just a handful of words, or it could be thousands, but in either case, the way this works is to pass in all but the last word from that example into the model and compare the prediction that it makes with the true last word from the example. An algorithm called backpropagation is used to tweak all of the parameters in such a way that it makes the model a little more likely to choose the true last word and a little less likely to choose all the others. When you do this for many, many trillions of examples, not only does the model start to give more accurate predictions on the training data, but it also starts to make more reasonable predictions on text that it's never seen before. Given the huge number of parameters and the enormous amount of training data, the scale of computation involved in training a large language model is mind-boggling. To illustrate, imagine that you could perform one billion additions and multiplications every single second. How long do you think it would take for you to do all of the operations involved in training the largest language models? Do you think it would take a year? Maybe something like 10,000 years? The answer is actually much more than that. It's well over 100 million years. This is only part of the story, though. This whole process is called pre-training. The goal of auto-completing a random passage of text from the internet is very different from the goal of being a good AI assistant. To address this, chatbots undergo another type of training, just as important, called reinforcement learning with human feedback. Workers flag unhelpful or problematic predictions, and their corrections further change the model's parameters, making them more likely to give predictions that users prefer. Looking back at the pre-training, though, this staggering amount of computation is only made possible by using special computer chips that are optimized for running many operations in parallel, known as GPUs. However, not all language models can be easily parallelized. Prior to 2017, most language models would process text one word at a time, but then a team of researchers at Google introduced a new model known as the transformer. Transformers don't read text from the start to the finish, they soak it all in at once, in parallel. The very first step inside a transformer, and most other language models for that matter, is to associate each word with a long list of numbers. The reason for this is that the training process only works with continuous values, so you have to somehow encode language using numbers, and each of these lists of numbers may somehow encode the meaning of the corresponding word. What makes transformers unique is their reliance on a special operation known as attention. This operation gives all of these lists of numbers a chance to talk to one another and refine the meanings they encode based on the context around, all done in parallel. For example, the numbers encoding the word bank might be changed based on the context surrounding it to somehow encode the more specific notion of a riverbank. Transformers typically also include a second type of operation known as a feed-forward neural network, and this gives the model extra capacity to store more patterns about language learned during training. All of this data repeatedly flows through many different iterations of these two fundamental operations, and as it does so, the hope is that each list of numbers is enriched to encode whatever information might be needed to make an accurate prediction of what word follows in the passage. At the end, one final function is performed on the last vector in this sequence, which now has had a chance to be influenced by all the other context from the input text, as well as everything the model learned during training, to produce a prediction of the next word. Again, the model's prediction looks like a probability for every possible next word. Although researchers design the framework for how each of these steps work, it's important to understand that the specific behavior is an emergent phenomenon based on how those hundreds of billions of parameters are tuned during training. This makes it incredibly challenging to determine why the model makes the exact predictions that it does. What you can see is that when you use large language model predictions to autocomplete a prompt, the words that it generates are uncannily fluent, fascinating, and even useful. If you're a new viewer and you're curious about more details on how transformers and attention work, boy do I have some material for you. One option is to jump into a series I made about deep learning, where we visualize and motivate the details of attention and all the other steps in a transformer. Also, on my second channel I just posted a talk I gave a couple months ago about this topic for the company TNG in Munich. Sometimes I actually prefer the content I make as a casual talk rather than a produced video, but I leave it up to you which one of these feels like the better follow-on.

Welcome back. In the first two parts of this series, we covered researching the company and structuring your answers using the STAR method. Today we're going deeper into the preparation that actually separates good candidates from great ones — and it starts the night before, not the morning of.

Step one is preparing your questions, and I mean really preparing them, not just scribbling down two generic ones at the last minute. Here's the thing: the questions you ask signal how seriously you've thought about the role. A question like "What does success look like in this position at the 30, 60, and 90-day mark?" tells the interviewer you're already thinking about performance, not just getting hired. Write out five to seven genuine questions. You won't use all of them, but having depth gives you options depending on where the conversation goes.

Step two is doing a targeted skills audit the night before. Pull up the job description and highlight every skill or requirement they mention more than once. Those are the competencies they're most worried about. Then for each one, identify a specific story from your experience that demonstrates it. If they mention "cross-functional collaboration" twice, you should have a ready example — not a vague summary, but an actual situation with a result you can quantify. Research from the National Association of Colleges and Employers consistently shows that hiring managers rate preparedness and communication as the top factors in final decisions, so the candidates who can give concrete answers quickly have a measurable edge.

Step three is what I call a friction audit of your logistics. This sounds simple but it removes a specific kind of stress that tanks performance. Confirm your interview time and time zone if it's remote. Test the video platform — Zoom, Teams, whatever they're using — the night before, not five minutes prior. If it's in person, map the route and add fifteen minutes of buffer. Identify where you're going to sit and what's behind you if it's on camera. These aren't overthinking — they're removing cognitive load so your brain is free to actually think during the interview.

Now those three steps build together intentionally. When you're not stressed about logistics, you can listen more carefully. When you've already matched your stories to their priorities, you can answer confidently. And when you have thoughtful questions ready, you end the interview on a high note instead of going blank when they ask "so what questions do you have for us?"

The one thing you can do today: take the job description for any role you're currently pursuing, highlight the repeated skills, and write one specific story for each one. Just that. You don't need two hours — you need thirty focused minutes. That's what real preparation looks like.

When you're building a retrieval system, one of the most consequential decisions you'll make is which embedding model to use. And most people pick one based on name recognition and never revisit it. That's a mistake, because the differences between models are large, measurable, and domain-specific.

Here's the core problem. Embedding models convert text into vectors, and those vectors need to capture semantic similarity in a way that actually matches your use case. A model trained primarily on web crawl data will behave very differently from one fine-tuned on scientific literature or legal documents. Accuracy on a general benchmark tells you almost nothing about performance on your specific data.

The standard reference point for comparing these models is the MTEB benchmark — Massive Text Embedding Benchmark — published by Hugging Face. It evaluates models across 56 tasks covering retrieval, classification, clustering, and semantic similarity. The leaderboard is public and updated regularly. Right now, models like text-embedding-3-large from OpenAI, E5-mistral-7b from Microsoft, and GTE-Qwen2-7B from Alibaba sit near the top of overall scores. But overall scores are averages, and averages obscure a lot.

Let me give you a concrete example. Suppose you're building a customer support retrieval system. Your queries are short — three to ten words — and your documents are longer support articles. This asymmetric retrieval task maps directly to the "Retrieval" subcategory on MTEB. If you sort by that subcategory specifically instead of the overall score, you get a different ranking. A model like BGE-M3, developed by Beijing Academy of Artificial Intelligence, consistently performs well on asymmetric retrieval because it was explicitly trained with that structure in mind using a technique called multi-granularity training.

Now here's what you actually do with this information. Pull three to five candidate models from the MTEB retrieval subcategory. Use the sentence-transformers library in Python, which supports most of these models with a consistent API. Encode a sample of your real queries and documents — even a few hundred pairs is enough for a meaningful signal. Measure recall at K, meaning how often the correct document appears in the top K results. Do this before you commit to any infrastructure.

One more thing worth knowing: embedding dimensions matter for latency and storage. text-embedding-3-large produces 3072-dimensional vectors by default. BGE-M3 produces 1024. At scale, that difference affects both your vector database costs and your query speed. OpenAI's model does support dimension reduction through their API, but you need to evaluate whether the compressed version still meets your accuracy threshold.

The decision tree is straightforward. Check MTEB for your task type, test on your actual data, and measure the tradeoff between accuracy and operational cost. That's the whole process. Name recognition is not a methodology.

Welcome back. In the last three parts we covered how to audit your current skills, how to prioritize which gaps to close first, and how to build a weekly practice schedule that actually holds. Today we're going to talk about the part most people skip entirely — how to know when you're actually getting better, and how to adjust your roadmap when the evidence tells you something isn't working.

Here's the problem. Most people conflate time spent with progress made. They practice for three weeks, feel busy, and assume they're improving. But busyness and growth are not the same thing.

So step one is to build what researchers call a performance feedback loop, and do it before you start practicing, not after. This means defining a specific, measurable indicator for each skill you're developing. If you're learning to write more clearly, your indicator might be whether a non-expert reader can summarize your main point back to you accurately within sixty seconds. If you're developing data analysis skills, it might be whether your conclusions hold up when you swap in a different dataset. The indicator needs to tell you something concrete, not just whether you felt good about the session.

Step two is to run what Anders Ericsson's deliberate practice research calls "diagnostic repetitions" — short, focused attempts where you're specifically testing the weak point, not rehearsing the comfortable parts. Most people practice what they're already decent at because it feels productive. Instead, identify the one micro-component of the skill that's causing failures and isolate it. A guitarist who keeps stumbling on chord transitions shouldn't run through whole songs. They should slow that transition down to half speed and do it thirty times in a row. Same logic applies to public speaking, negotiation, coding — any complex skill.

Step three is to schedule a weekly fifteen-minute roadmap review. Not a vague reflection, an actual structured check-in using three questions: What's my performance indicator showing this week? Did I do diagnostic work on my actual weak point, or did I default to comfortable practice? Does anything need to change in next week's plan based on that? This review is what separates a roadmap from a wish list. Without it, your roadmap just drifts.

Here's why this sequence matters. The feedback loop gives you real signal. The diagnostic repetitions make sure your practice is actually targeting the gap. And the weekly review closes the loop so your roadmap stays responsive to reality instead of becoming a document you ignore.

One concrete example: a product manager I know was trying to improve stakeholder communication. She tracked one indicator — whether stakeholders replied to her project updates with clarifying questions or with alignment. Within four weeks of diagnostic practice and weekly reviews, the clarifying questions dropped by more than half.

The takeaway you can apply today: pick one skill you're currently developing, write down one specific performance indicator for it, and schedule your first fifteen-minute review for the end of this week.

Welcome back. In the last four parts, we've covered money supply, velocity, supply shocks, and expectations. Today I want to tie something together that most explanations skip entirely — why inflation can persist even after the original cause disappears.

Let's start from scratch with a simple observation. Prices are set by people. A store manager decides what to charge for bread. A landlord decides what to charge for rent. These aren't automatic processes. They're decisions, and decisions are based on information.

Here's the key question: what information are those decision-makers using?

Mostly, they're using the past. If bread cost two dollars last year and their costs went up five percent, they charge two dollars and ten cents. That's rational. But it means today's prices are partly a function of yesterday's prices, which were a function of the day before that. Economists call this price stickiness combined with adaptive expectations — the formal term from Milton Friedman and Edmund Phelps's work in the late 1960s.

Now here's the thought experiment. Imagine a small economy where, for one month, there's a genuine shortage of wheat. Bread prices spike. The shortage ends. Wheat is plentiful again. But what happens to bread prices?

You might expect them to fall back immediately. They usually don't. The baker's costs went up during the shortage. She raised wages to retain workers during the chaotic period. Her supplier locked in a higher contract. Each of those adjustments made sense at the time, and now they're embedded in the cost structure. The original cause — the wheat shortage — is gone, but the price level has ratcheted up.

This is sometimes called second-round effects, and it's why central banks worry about what they call wage-price spirals. Workers see prices rise, negotiate higher wages to keep up, which raises business costs, which raises prices further. No one is acting irrationally at any step. Each person is just responding logically to their own situation. But the system as a whole keeps pushing upward.

The counterintuitive conclusion here is this: inflation is not simply a symptom of current conditions. It's partially a symptom of what people believe future conditions will be, based on past conditions. If people believe inflation will continue, their behavior makes it continue. The belief itself becomes a cause.

This is why the Federal Reserve in 1979 under Paul Volcker raised interest rates to nearly twenty percent — a dramatic and deliberately painful move. The goal wasn't just to reduce money supply. It was to break the expectation. To credibly signal that the feedback loop would be interrupted. It worked, though at the cost of a severe recession.

So inflation can be self-sustaining not because of ongoing shocks, but because of memory built into the pricing system itself.

Next time, we'll look at how different economies have broken that cycle — and what the evidence says about what actually works.

Welcome back. Last time we covered research and storytelling — knowing the company and framing your experience well. Today we're going one level deeper, into the part of interview prep most people skip entirely: practicing under realistic conditions, handling difficult questions, and closing the interview strategically.

Let's start with practice. Most people prepare by reading their answers in their head. That feels like practice, but it isn't. Speaking activates different cognitive processes than reading — your brain has to retrieve, organize, and articulate simultaneously. So the first concrete step is this: do at least three full mock interviews out loud before the real one. Not bullet points. Full answers, spoken aloud, timed.

You can do this with a friend, with a tool like Yoodli or Interview Warmup by Google, or even just recording yourself on your phone. The goal isn't to memorize scripts — it's to get comfortable with the discomfort of thinking on your feet. Research from the University of Michigan found that retrieval practice, actually pulling information from memory rather than re-reading it, improves performance under pressure by roughly 50 percent compared to passive review. That principle applies directly here.

Now, the second step is preparing for behavioral questions using a tighter version of the STAR framework. You've probably heard of STAR — Situation, Task, Action, Result. The problem is most people spend 80 percent of their answer on Situation and Task, which is background. Interviewers want Action and Result. So restructure your prep: write out five to seven career stories, and for each one, make sure your Action section has at least two or three distinct decisions you made, not just what happened. Then quantify the Result wherever possible. Not "we improved sales" but "we grew Q3 revenue by 23 percent by shifting outreach to a segment we'd been ignoring."

That specificity is what separates forgettable answers from memorable ones.

Third step: prepare three strong questions to ask at the end. This is not a formality. It's your chance to demonstrate strategic thinking and genuine engagement. Avoid questions you could Google. Instead, ask things like: "What does success look like in this role after the first 90 days?" or "What's the biggest challenge the team is navigating right now?" These questions signal that you're already thinking like someone in the role, not someone hoping to get the role. That framing shift matters.

Then, before you leave — or end the video call — briefly restate your interest. Something like: "I want to be straightforward — this role is a strong fit for where I'm heading, and I'd be excited to continue the conversation." Direct, not desperate.

Here's your one action for today: pick one role you're preparing for and write out three STAR stories with the results quantified. Just that. Get those on paper. Everything else becomes easier once the foundation is solid.

Welcome back. In the previous four parts we covered cosine similarity, tokenization tradeoffs, and why bigger isn't always better for retrieval tasks. Today we're focusing on one specific problem: domain mismatch, and how it breaks embedding models in predictable ways.

Here's the core issue. Most general-purpose embedding models — OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small, Cohere's embed-english-v3, sentence-transformers like all-MiniLM — are trained on web crawls, Wikipedia, and book corpora. When you deploy them in a specialized domain like legal contracts or biomedical literature, the vocabulary distribution shifts dramatically, and the model's vector space no longer captures meaningful semantic distance for your use case.

Let me make this concrete. Take the phrase "the party failed to perform." In a general corpus, "party" clusters near social events. In a legal contract, it means a signatory. If you're building a contract review system and you embed "breach of obligation by signatory," it may not retrieve "party failed to perform" because those terms live in different neighborhoods of the general model's vector space, even though they're semantically equivalent in a legal context.

You can measure this directly using BEIR, which is a benchmark suite from the paper "BEIR: A Heterogeneous Benchmark for Zero-shot Evaluation of Information Retrieval Models." It includes domain-specific datasets like TREC-COVID for biomedical and NFCorpus for nutrition science. When you run text-embedding-3-small on TREC-COVID using BEIR's evaluation harness, you get an nDCG@10 around 0.57. When you run a model fine-tuned specifically on biomedical text, like BioLORD from the Hugging Face model hub, you get closer to 0.72 on the same dataset. That's a 15-point gap from domain alignment alone, not architecture.

So what do you do with this? Three practical paths. First, evaluate before you build. Pull the relevant BEIR subset for your domain, or create 50 to 100 labeled query-document pairs from your actual data and run a quick nDCG or MRR calculation before committing to a model. Second, consider fine-tuning with sentence-transformers' `MultipleNegativesRankingLoss`. You can start with a general checkpoint and adapt it to your domain with as few as a few thousand positive pairs. Third, if you're working with a vendor API, check whether they offer domain-specific endpoints. Cohere, for instance, has separate embed models optimized for English search versus multilingual versus code.

The takeaway is simple: embedding model selection is an empirical question, not a theoretical one. The model that wins on MTEB's general leaderboard may rank third on your actual retrieval task. Measure on your domain, with your queries, against your documents.

Next part we'll look at late interaction models, specifically ColBERT, and why token-level scoring changes the tradeoff between index size and retrieval quality.

Welcome back. Last time we covered the basic mechanics of the tragedy of the commons — how individually rational decisions can produce collectively irrational outcomes. Today I want to push further, because the original framing has a flaw that most people never hear about, and fixing that flaw actually changes what we should conclude.

Garrett Hardin published his famous 1968 essay assuming that a shared resource with open access will always collapse. His logic was clean: if I add one more animal to a shared pasture, I capture all the benefit while the cost of overgrazing gets divided among everyone. So everyone keeps adding animals until the pasture is destroyed. Rational individuals, catastrophic collective outcome.

But here's what Hardin got wrong, or at least incomplete. He conflated two very different things: open access resources and commons. A true commons, historically, was not an open-access free-for-all. It was a managed shared resource with rules, boundaries, and enforcement — governed by the community that depended on it.

Elinor Ostrom spent decades studying actual commons around the world: Swiss alpine meadows, Japanese forests, Spanish irrigation systems, Maine lobster fisheries. And she found something that directly contradicted Hardin. These communities had developed stable, self-governing institutions that prevented collapse — sometimes for centuries. In 2009, she became the first woman to win the Nobel Prize in Economics for this work.

Her central finding was that communities can solve commons problems without either privatizing the resource or handing it to a central authority — the two solutions Hardin implicitly assumed were the only options. The key ingredients she identified included clear boundaries around who gets to use the resource, rules matched to local conditions, collective decision-making, and credible monitoring and sanctions.

Here's the thought experiment that makes this concrete. Imagine you and eleven neighbors share a well. Hardin predicts disaster. But Ostrom would ask: do you know each other? Can you talk? Can you agree on who draws water when, and catch someone who cheats? If yes, you have the raw materials for a functioning commons. The tragedy isn't inevitable — it's a failure mode that emerges under specific conditions, particularly anonymity and absence of communication.

This leads to the non-obvious conclusion: the tragedy of the commons is really a tragedy of the wrong institutional design, not an unavoidable feature of shared resources. When people can communicate, build trust, and create accountable rules, they routinely solve these problems from the bottom up.

What this means practically is that before you assume a shared resource will be destroyed, ask what the governance structure actually looks like. The pasture isn't doomed because it's shared. It's doomed if — and only if — no one has any reason to care about what happens next.

Welcome back. Last time we established Godel's first incompleteness theorem — that any consistent formal system powerful enough to describe basic arithmetic must contain true statements it cannot prove. Today we go further, and the destination is genuinely strange.

Let's start with a quick recap using our library analogy. Imagine a library with a complete rulebook for determining which books are valid. Godel showed that some true books can never be approved by that rulebook. Now the question becomes: can the library at least prove that its own rulebook is consistent? Can it prove it won't approve contradictions?

This is the second incompleteness theorem, and the answer is no.

Here's why, built from the ground up.

Godel's method involved encoding statements about a formal system *inside* that same system. He showed you can construct a statement that essentially says "I am not provable." Call it G. If the system is consistent, G is true but unprovable. That's theorem one.

Now notice something subtle. The *reasoning* we just did — concluding that G is true because the system is consistent — was done by *us*, standing outside the system. We assumed consistency and derived a result. But that chain of reasoning can itself be encoded inside the system. In other words, the system can represent the logical argument: "If I am consistent, then G is unprovable."

This is a perfectly valid theorem the system can prove about itself.

Now suppose the system could also prove its own consistency. Then it would have two things: "If I am consistent, G is unprovable" and "I am consistent." From those two, it could prove G is unprovable. But G being unprovable *is* exactly what G says about itself, so it would have proved G. And that contradicts the fact that G is unprovable in a consistent system.

The only escape from this contradiction is to reject the assumption that caused the problem — the assumption that the system proved its own consistency. It cannot. Not without being inconsistent.

Hilbert famously spent years trying to build a complete, consistent foundation for all of mathematics — a program where mathematics would prove its own bedrock. Godel published his theorems in 1931, and they ended that dream permanently.

Here's the counterintuitive conclusion worth sitting with: the stronger and more capable a mathematical system is, the more certainly it cannot vouch for itself. Power and self-certification are fundamentally incompatible. Any proof of consistency must come from *outside*, from a stronger system — which then faces the same problem at the next level up.

This isn't a quirk or a gap waiting to be filled. It's a structural feature of formal reasoning itself.

Mathematics doesn't rest on a foundation it can see. It rests on one it must, in some sense, simply trust.

That's worth thinking about.

Welcome back. In the last four parts we've covered lexing, parsing, ASTs, and semantic analysis. Today we're going to look at what happens right before code generation: the intermediate representation, and specifically why compilers deliberately throw away information.

Let's start with something concrete. You write a for-loop in C. The compiler reads it, builds a syntax tree, checks the types. Now it needs to translate that into machine instructions. The naive approach would be to go directly from your C code to x86 assembly. And early compilers did exactly that — one compiler per language per machine.

The problem became obvious by the 1970s. If you had four programming languages and six processor architectures, you needed twenty-four different compilers. Each one a separate engineering project. This is what Grace Hopper's team and later the FORTRAN team were dealing with. The combinatorial explosion was brutal.

The insight that solved this — and it's genuinely elegant — is to split the compiler in half using a shared intermediate language. Now you need four front-ends and six back-ends. Ten components instead of twenty-four. LLVM popularized this architecture for a modern audience, but the idea goes back to earlier work like the UNCOL project in 1958, which explicitly proposed a universal intermediate language.

Here's where it gets interesting. The intermediate representation, or IR, is intentionally simpler than the source language. LLVM's IR, for example, looks almost like assembly — it has explicit registers, simple arithmetic instructions, no loops, just conditional jumps. All the rich structure of your source code has been flattened out.

Why would you destroy that structure? Here's the thought experiment. Imagine you're an optimizer trying to eliminate redundant calculations. In C, you have while loops, for loops, do-while loops — three syntactic forms. To optimize them, you'd need to recognize all three. But if everything gets flattened into the same conditional-jump form first, you only need one optimization pass. The flattening reveals what those constructs actually have in common underneath the syntax.

This is the counterintuitive part: the compiler understands your code better precisely because it stops thinking about it the way you wrote it. Your for-loop and someone else's while-loop, once lowered to IR, are identical. The optimizer doesn't see your intention, it sees the structure, and that structure is what actually matters for performance.

This is also why LLVM can optimize code from Rust, Swift, and C simultaneously using the same passes. They all lower to the same IR. The language differences dissolve.

So the intermediate representation isn't a stepping stone. It's a lens that makes certain truths about your program visible that were hidden by the syntax you used to write it.

Next part, we'll look at how those optimization passes actually work — specifically static single assignment form, which takes this idea even further.

Last time we covered dense retrieval with FAISS and vector embeddings. Today we're solving a specific failure mode you'll hit in production: your retriever returns semantically similar chunks that are actually useless for answering the question.

Here's the concrete problem. Say your knowledge base contains a 50-page technical manual. A user asks "what is the maximum operating temperature for component X?" Your vector search returns three chunks that all mention component X and temperature in general contexts, but none of them contain the actual specification table. The embedding similarity was high, but the information retrieval failed.

This happens because dense retrievers optimize for topical similarity, not answer presence. A chunk that says "component X requires careful thermal management" scores highly because it's semantically close to your query, even though it contains no actionable data.

The solution is reranking. After your initial retrieval step pulls back, say, 20 candidates, you run a second model that scores each chunk specifically on answer likelihood given the query. This two-stage approach is sometimes called retrieve-then-rerank.

The most practical reranker right now is Cohere's Rerank API, or if you want to run locally, the cross-encoder models from sentence-transformers work well. The key architectural difference is this: your initial retriever uses a bi-encoder, meaning query and document are embedded separately and compared via dot product. A cross-encoder takes the query and document concatenated together as a single input and outputs a relevance score. Cross-encoders are far more accurate but too slow to run against your entire index, which is why you use them only on the shortlist.

In code terms, using sentence-transformers, you'd load CrossEncoder with the model ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2, pass it a list of query-document pairs, and call the predict method to get scores. You then sort your 20 candidates by those scores and take the top 3 or 5 into your generation step.

The benchmark numbers here are meaningful. On the MS MARCO passage ranking dataset, bi-encoders like all-mpnet-base-v2 achieve around 33 MRR@10. Cross-encoders on the same dataset reach 39 to 40. That gap translates directly to fewer hallucinations in your final output because your language model is working with better source material.

Real deployment consideration: the latency cost is real. A cross-encoder reranking 20 documents adds roughly 100 to 200 milliseconds on CPU. For synchronous applications this matters. Cohere's hosted Rerank API runs faster and offloads the compute, which is why teams at companies like Notion and Glean have used managed reranking services in their document search pipelines.

So the pattern to remember is: retrieve broadly with your fast bi-encoder, then rerank narrowly with your accurate cross-encoder. You get the scalability of vector search with the precision of pairwise comparison.

Next part we'll cover chunking strategies, specifically why fixed-size chunks hurt performance on structured documents like contracts and API documentation.

What makes beer? Single-celled organisms. What makes us cough and feel like sleeping for twelve straight hours Straight? Single-celled organisms. AKA microbes, germs, the tiny machines that allow us to digest—and can kill us. “What is life?” Turns out, life is relentless, everywhere on earth! [Intro Music Plays] Microbes are invisible: they’re single cells. So to understand microbes—that is, to do microbiology—scientists first needed to study cells. German botanist Matthias Schleiden and physiologist Theodor Schwann proposed a modern cell theory around 1837: every living thing is made up of one or more cells. So the cell was the fundamental unit of life, the thing that gives structure to all forms of life. Amazingly, Schleiden—who studied plants—and Schwann—who studied animals—both noticed tiny dots in the middles of the cells they looked at under their microscopes. These nuclei must somehow be important to all life, they figured—correctly. Darwin’s chief public defender, Thomas Henry Huxley, also wrote a article called “The Cell–Theory” in 1853. Where the Germans were cataloging different microscopic forms, Huxley proposed a mechanistic model: cells are little factories with different parts that have different functions. Their functions make the parts add up into an organic whole. Not every scientist believed cell theory right away, but many came around, as microscope-enabled evidence mounted. And yet, being able to observe single-celled organisms didn’t immediately lead scientists to connect them to disease. In fact, medicine in the early 1800s mostly worked the way it had hundreds or even a thousand years earlier. It was still all about liquids called humors. But some diseases were understood differently. These were caused by a kind of invisible badness in the environment called a miasma. According to miasma theory, diseases were caused by foul-smelling airs that came from rotting meats, swamps, and other putrefying sources. Before you knock it, think about it: this theory mostly works. Dangerous microbes grow on smelly, dirty stuff. The smell is a byproduct of the microbes’ eating organic matter. But even today, we can’t see these microbes without instruments. So following our unaided senses is still smart: don’t eat gross trash! Sniff the turkey before you eat it. And then, just eat it anyway like I did this morning. By the mid-1800s, however, a few scientific rebels were pushing a germ theory of disease. This holds that germs, or pathogenic microorganisms, cause infectious diseases. [Snow] Around this time, a nasty germ called Vibrio cholerae provided an opportunity to test the theory out.h Cholera had ravaged England since the germ started to travel widely out of south Asia in the early 1800s, hitching rides on the expanding armada of merchant ships. Empire had all kinds of unpredicted downsides. In 1854, a bad cholera epidemic hit Broad Street in the Soho neighborhood of London, killing over six hundred people. One doctor named John Snow had a hunch about what was going on. Snow created a map of the outbreak and noted that the cases seemed to cluster around one public well. He hypothesized that cholera microbes were rapidly multiplying in its water. So he convinced the Soho council to remove the well’s pump handle. People went elsewhere for water, and the cholera infections decreased. John Snow became a hero! Saving Lives! Even though, it turned out, the outbreak was already in decline when he removed the handle. Thus proving that some John Snows, do know somethings. Snow’s quick thinking didn’t really prove germ theory. For germs to replace miasmas in their minds, scientists would require a better model of what germs are, and how they behave. For that, we turn to one of the first experimental microbiologists and a dude whose name you probably should know from milk labels: Louis Pasteur Born in France in 1822—the same year as Galton—Pasteur was a chemist, not a physician. But he is known for his work on germs and health. Pasteur is credited as the co-founder of microbiology and the founder of modern zymology, the science of fermentation. Fermentation is the biological and chemical process needed to make beer, wine, yogurt, cheese, bread, and many other foods. ThoughtBubble, show us what that means. Chemically, fermentation is the conversion of sugar molecules into ethanol, or alcohol, and carbon dioxide. In food, this usually happens thanks to bacteria or yeast. Looking good, Saccharomyces! Pasteur first connected yeast to the fermentation of wine in 1857. He observed that microscopic yeast on grape skins makes grape juice ferment into wine. If you sterilize the skins, killing the yeast, fermentation won’t happen. Pasteur even made the the analogy that the microbes growing in wine and beer were similar to the microbes causing diseases in humans and animals. He hypothesized that microbes are everywhere and must be responsible for all kinds of phenomena, like making food spoil. So in 1865, he patented a process in which liquids such as milk were heated to a temperature between sixty and one hundred degrees Celsius in order to kill microbes. This became known as pasteurization. It may sound kinda simple, but this was revolutionary! Earlier, Nicolas Appert had invented canning to make foods safe, so Napoleon could fight wars for longer. But Pasteur’s method used a lower temperature, thus preserving tastes and textures. Related to this idea of killing bad germs, Pasteur also helped develop hygienic medical practices, along with British surgeon Joseph Lister. No germs in the operating room! Most importantly for the history of biology, Pasteur got tangled up in a debate with a fellow scientist named Félix Pouchet who believed that living things spawn naturally from certain environments. This widely held belief was called spontaneous generation. Pasteur, based on his observations of grapes, reasoned that living things can only grow from other living things. So he created an ingenious experiment to prove the theory of biogenesis, or life from life. Pasteur showed that no germs would grow in sealed, sterilized long-necked flasks full of beef broth. But in sterilized but open flasks, microbes would eventually show up. Thanks Thoughtbubble. After proving biogenesis, in the 1870s, Pasteur worked on immunology and vaccination. Pasteur’s work with the rabies and anthrax microbes supported the germ theory and its use in medicine: if you could identify a microorganism, you could give someone a weakened version of it, or vaccine, and the patient taking the vaccine would develop immunity to the real bug. And in 1879, Pasteur developed the first lab-manufactured vaccine, for chicken cholera. It was around this time that germ theory was finally widely accepted, in part due to Pasteur’s work. But Pasteur’s ethics were questionable: when rabies vaccines for humans rolled out, Pasteur kept his data on their effectiveness secret. He even kept secret mortality data, or how many people his vaccines didn’t help. So unlike today, in Pasteur’s time, it wasn’t necessarily a norm that useful medical research should be shared publicly. But Pasteur was not the only notable germ wrangler. Born in 1843, the German physician Robert Koch painstakingly worked at a microscope to definitively establish the germ concept of disease. He identified different microbes, becoming the leader of a large research team in Berlin. They founded the discipline of bacteriology, or the study of bacteria, particularly in relation to human health. In the late 1880s and ‘90s, Koch and his team traveled into areas experiencing epidemics, including India and Egypt, to identify the microbes causing diseases there. By ID-ing these microorganisms, they hoped to create vaccines. First, Koch proved that the anthrax germ causes anthrax-the-disease. Later, he identified the bacterium that causes tuberculosis or TB, which was a very widespread disease. Koch also characterized cholera, vindicating the work of John Snow by showing that the bacterium lives naturally in the small intestine of humans. Snow wasn’t just high on ether: he’d correctly guessed how cholera spreads! Eventually, Koch’s team contributed to the identification of the germs that causes diphtheria, typhoid, pneumonia, gonorrhea, meningitis, whooping cough, tetanus, plague, leprosy, and syphilis. …I would not want to borrow this dude’s lucky microscope! It's been through a lot! Through this work, Koch developed the four postulates or steps for identifying infectious diseases. One, the microorganism must always be present, in every case of the disease. Two, the germ must be able to be isolated from a host containing the disease and grown in a lab, in a “culture.” Three, samples taken from the culture must cause the same disease when introduced into a healthy model animal like a mouse. Four, the germ must be isolated from that new animal, meaning it is the same organism that caused the disease in the original host. So structure matters: in all infections, germs grow out of hand, throwing the body out of whack and causing inflammation. These bad germs, or pathogens, develop and live within us, and can kill us. But with Pasteur’s vaccines and Koch’s scientific method of ID-ing bacteria, humans could now develop medicines to fight jerk-sauce germs. Go team us! Back in the world of multicellular organisms, another scientist at the intersection of biology and medicine was exploring the structure of living things. In 1892, German-American physiologist Jacques Loeb began his experiments on embryonic development, or how organisms grow from single-celled embryos into whatever they’re supposed to look like. So he started dunking sea urchin embryos into different salt solutions. And, in 1899, he got one to give birth… just from being salty! Loeb’s artificial parthenogenesis, or birth from the self, was mega-astounding news. It raised basic questions, such as what is sex? Could a human one day give birth by herself… or him-self? Causing sea urchins to give virgin births made Loeb a celebrity. But for the history of biology, it was revolutionary that Loeb’s sense of scientific understanding of life was tied to his ability to control it. His work with sea urchins can be seen as an early form of bioengineering, or treating cells and tissues as machine-parts to be used constructively by a human designer. Loeb’s notable students included the behavioral psychologists, B. F. Skinner and J. B. Watson, and a creator of birth control, Gregory Pincus. Biology, like other scientific disciplines, shows a historical arc from observations to experiments, and from less control to more. From the mid-1800s to the early 1900s, scientists moved from mostly believing that living things spontaneously appear out of nowhere to controlling fermentation on a massive scale. Cheers to that! Your professional reputation isn't built in big moments. It's built in small, repeated behaviors that other people start to predict about you. And that predictability is actually the whole game.

Let's start with the foundation: consistency over intensity. Most people try to make a strong impression in high-stakes situations — the big presentation, the important meeting. But research from organizational psychologist Heidi Grant shows that people form reliable impressions of us based on patterns, not peaks. What that means practically is that how you show up on a random Tuesday matters more than how you perform at the annual review. So step one is identifying one or two behaviors you can execute consistently — returning emails within 24 hours, always being prepared before a call, following through on small commitments. Pick something specific and make it non-negotiable.

Now once you have that baseline of reliability, you can layer on what I'd call visible expertise. This is step two. Reputation doesn't just come from doing good work — it comes from people knowing you do good work. That distinction is uncomfortable for a lot of people because it can feel self-promotional, but there's a difference between bragging and making your thinking visible. Writing a brief summary after a project wraps, sharing a relevant article with a short note explaining why it matters, contributing a specific perspective in a meeting instead of a vague agreement — these are all ways of demonstrating competence without announcing it. You're showing your reasoning, not just your results.

Here's where it gets compounding. Step three is what researchers call the "consistent contributor" effect — when you show up reliably and share your thinking openly over time, people start to associate your name with a particular kind of value. They begin recommending you for opportunities you don't even know exist. This is how reputation actually travels. Not through LinkedIn posts, but through someone in a room saying "you should talk to this person."

A concrete example: Adam Grant at Wharton has written about how the most successful professionals in his research weren't the ones who hoarded their expertise — they were what he calls "givers" who shared knowledge freely and built reputations as go-to resources. That reputation then attracted better opportunities back to them over time.

So here's how these three steps connect: reliability makes you trustworthy, visible expertise makes you memorable, and sustained contribution over time makes you recommendable. Each one only works because the one before it is in place.

The thing you can do today is simple. Think of one small commitment you've been vague about — to a colleague, a project, yourself — and make it specific. Put a deadline on it. Follow through. That's one data point in the pattern other people are quietly collecting about you.

Welcome back. In the last few parts of this series, we covered how to map your current skills, identify gaps, and prioritize what to learn first. Today we're going to talk about something most people skip entirely — how to actually structure your practice so that learning compounds over time instead of resetting every few weeks.

Here's the problem. Most people learn in isolated bursts. They watch tutorials, take notes, feel good about it, and then move on to something new before anything sticks. This is sometimes called the "collection trap" — you're gathering information without converting it into capability.

So step one is to shift from consumption sessions to production sessions. What this means practically: after every learning block, you build something tiny using what you just studied. Not a project, not a portfolio piece — just a small artifact that proves you can apply it. If you're learning SQL, you write three queries from scratch without looking at your notes. If you're studying persuasive writing, you rewrite a product description from a real website. The output doesn't have to be good. It just has to exist. This is the retrieval practice principle from cognitive science — specifically the work of Henry Roediger at Washington University, whose research consistently showed that testing yourself on material dramatically outperforms re-reading it for long-term retention.

Step two is to build what I'd call a "lag indicator review." Most people only track whether they're showing up to learn. That's a leading indicator. A lag indicator tells you whether the learning is actually working. Every two weeks, pick one skill you've been developing and do a cold performance test — no notes, no prep, just attempt the real task. Then compare it to where you were two weeks ago. This gap tells you your actual learning velocity, and it will often surprise you in both directions. Some things are sticking faster than you think. Others aren't sticking at all, which means your method is broken, not your ability.

Step three is to explicitly sequence adjacent skills. Skill development roadmaps fail when they treat every new skill as equally distant from where you are now. They're not. If you know basic Python, learning pandas is much closer than learning machine learning from scratch. Use your existing skills as bridges. Identify what skill you're building next, then ask: what do I already know that overlaps with this? Build from the overlap, not from zero. This cuts your learning curve significantly and keeps motivation high because you're never starting completely cold.

Put these together and your roadmap becomes a compounding system rather than a checklist.

Your takeaway for today: look at whatever you studied most recently this week. Before you move on to anything new, take ten minutes and produce one small output — a paragraph, a working snippet of code, a diagram drawn from memory. Do it right now if you can. That single habit, repeated consistently, changes everything about how fast your skills actually develop.

You've probably heard that transformers are behind most modern language models, but the actual mechanism is rarely explained clearly. Let's fix that.

The core problem transformers solve is this: how do you process a sequence of words and figure out which words are relevant to each other, regardless of how far apart they are in the sentence? Earlier architectures like LSTMs processed tokens one at a time, left to right, which made it hard to connect information across long distances. A word at position one had to pass its meaning through dozens of intermediate steps to influence a word at position fifty.

Transformers abandoned that sequential approach entirely. Instead, every token looks at every other token simultaneously, and this is done through a mechanism called self-attention.

Here's the concrete mechanism. Take the sentence "The animal didn't cross the street because it was too tired." What does "it" refer to? To a human reader it's obvious — the animal. Self-attention lets the model figure this out mathematically.

Each token is represented as a vector, and from that vector, three things are computed: a query, a key, and a value. Think of it like a search system. The query is what you're looking for. The keys are what each other token is advertising about itself. You take the dot product of your query against every other token's key, run it through a softmax to get a probability distribution, and then use those weights to take a weighted sum of all the values. The result is a new representation of your token that has absorbed relevant context from the entire sequence.

In the original 2017 paper "Attention Is All You Need" from Google, the authors showed that stacking multiple attention heads in parallel — each learning different relationships — and repeating this across layers gave models the ability to capture syntax, coreference, and semantic relationships all at once.

In PyTorch, you can call `torch.nn.MultiheadAttention` and pass in your query, key, and value matrices directly. The library handles the scaling, the masking for autoregressive generation, and the projection back to your model's embedding dimension.

Why does this matter practically? BERT, released by Google in 2018, used the transformer encoder to achieve state-of-the-art results on eleven NLP benchmarks at once — not by being trained on each task, but by fine-tuning a single pre-trained model. The transformer architecture is what made that transfer learning possible, because the representations it learns are rich enough to generalize.

So when you hear that GPT-4 or Gemini is a transformer, you now know the specific thing that means: at every layer, every token is querying every other token through learned attention weights, building up richer representations until the model can predict the next token with high accuracy.

That's the mechanism. Not magic — matrix multiplication and softmax.

In the previous parts of this series, we established the basic structure of the prisoner's dilemma and showed why rational self-interest leads both players toward a worse outcome than if they had cooperated. Today we're going to push further into something genuinely surprising: what happens when the game repeats.

Here's the setup. Two players meet once, they each choose to cooperate or defect, they collect their payoffs, and they never see each other again. Given that structure, defecting is the dominant strategy. You're better off defecting regardless of what the other person does. That's the one-shot logic, and it's airtight.

But almost nothing in real life is a one-shot interaction. You deal with your neighbors, your colleagues, your suppliers, your rivals — repeatedly, over time. So the interesting question becomes: does repetition change the math?

It does, dramatically. And we can see why from first principles.

When you know you'll meet someone again, your choice today carries future consequences. If you defect on me now, I can respond tomorrow. That threat of retaliation — or the promise of continued cooperation — creates an incentive structure that simply doesn't exist in the one-shot game. The shadow of the future, as political scientist Robert Axelrod called it, changes everything.

Axelrod tested this directly in the early 1980s. He ran a computer tournament where researchers submitted strategies to compete in repeated prisoner's dilemmas. The strategies were sophisticated — some highly complex, some retaliatory, some forgiving. And the winner, across multiple rounds of the tournament, was the simplest strategy submitted: Tit for Tat.

Tit for Tat works like this. Start by cooperating. Then, on every subsequent move, do exactly what your opponent did last round. That's it. Cooperate with cooperators, retaliate against defectors, and return to cooperation the moment they do.

What makes this result non-obvious is that Tit for Tat never actually beats any individual opponent. It can't — it only ever copies. What it does is avoid catastrophic losses and accumulate steady gains with cooperative partners. It's not the most aggressive strategy. It's the most robust one across a diverse environment.

This maps onto something biologists recognized independently. Robert Trivers formalized the idea of reciprocal altruism in 1971, explaining how cooperation can evolve among self-interested organisms with no central authority enforcing rules — provided interactions are repeated and individuals can recognize each other. You see this in vampire bats sharing blood, in fish cleaning parasites from larger fish, in humans building trade networks across thousands of years.

The deep insight here is this: cooperation doesn't require altruism, and it doesn't require enforcement from above. It can emerge spontaneously from self-interest — but only when the game has a future.

Next time, we'll look at what breaks cooperation even in repeated games, and why that matters for understanding institutions.

So in the last two episodes we traced how packet switching replaced circuit switching, and how ARPANET proved the concept worked. Today I want to talk about the moment the internet almost didn't scale — and the elegant idea that saved it.

By the late 1970s, ARPANET was growing, but different networks were speaking different languages. There were satellite networks, radio networks, Ethernet local area networks. Each had its own protocol, its own addressing scheme, its own rules. Connecting them together felt like trying to route mail through countries that each had incompatible postal systems and wouldn't agree on a common one.

Vint Cerf and Bob Kahn recognized that you couldn't force every network to become identical. That would never happen politically, and the diversity of underlying technologies was actually useful. So they asked a different question: what if, instead of standardizing the networks themselves, you standardized the conversation *between* networks?

This is the key insight of TCP/IP, published in their 1974 paper. The idea is called the End-to-End Principle, and it's worth understanding precisely. Put the intelligence at the edges — at the devices sending and receiving data — and keep the network in the middle as simple and dumb as possible. Routers just forward packets. They don't track conversations. They don't guarantee delivery. They don't even know what the data means.

Think of it this way. Imagine you're shipping furniture across three different countries. Each country has different trucks, different roads, different drivers. Rather than redesigning all the trucks, you just agree on one thing: every box gets a label with a destination address. Each carrier reads the label, moves the box closer to its destination, and hands it off. Nobody needs to know the full route in advance. Nobody needs to coordinate centrally. The system self-organizes.

That's essentially what IP does. And TCP — the Transmission Control Protocol — sits above it, running on the end devices, handling the hard stuff: making sure all the packets arrived, requesting retransmission if they didn't, reassembling them in order.

Here's the counterintuitive part. This design looks like it *sacrifices* reliability by making the middle of the network dumb. But it actually *produces* resilience. Because no single node carries critical state about your connection, the network can route around failures automatically. ARPANET demonstrated this under simulated nuclear conditions — packets found their own paths when nodes went down.

The internet didn't scale because someone built a perfectly engineered central system. It scaled because Cerf and Kahn deliberately pushed complexity outward and kept the core minimal. Every device that connects to the internet today — your phone, your laptop, a sensor in a factory — is honoring that original design decision from 1974.

In the next episode, we'll look at what happened when the internet opened to the public, and why that created problems this architecture wasn't designed to handle.

Welcome back. In the previous four parts, we traced the life cycle of massive stars and watched them collapse. Today we're going to look at something most people gloss over: what actually happens at the boundary of a black hole, and why that boundary is stranger than it first appears.

Let's start from scratch on one idea — escape velocity.

If you throw a ball upward, it slows down, stops, and falls back. Earth's gravity pulls it home. But if you throw that ball fast enough — about 11 kilometers per second — it escapes Earth's gravity entirely. That threshold speed is escape velocity. Every massive object has one.

Now here's the key move: as an object gets denser, its escape velocity rises. Pack Earth into a sphere the size of a golf ball, and the escape velocity at its surface exceeds the speed of light. At that point, nothing leaves. Not matter. Not light. That boundary — the point of no return — is called the event horizon. Karl Schwarzschild calculated its radius in 1916, literally while serving on the Russian front during World War One, working from Einstein's freshly published equations of general relativity.

Here's where it gets interesting.

Most people imagine the event horizon as a wall, a physical surface you'd crash into. But it isn't. It's a location in space defined entirely by mathematics and gravity. If you were falling into a stellar-mass black hole, tidal forces would destroy you before you arrived. But for a supermassive black hole — billions of solar masses — the tidal forces at the horizon are actually gentle. You could, in principle, cross the event horizon without feeling anything dramatic in that moment.

This is the counterintuitive part: crossing the event horizon is not an event you experience. You don't feel a jolt. No alarm sounds. The horizon is defined by what your future looks like, not what you feel right now. Once inside, every possible path you can take — every direction in space and time — curves back toward the singularity. Your future is geometrically redirected. Time itself, in a sense, points inward.

From outside, the picture is equally strange. An observer watching you fall would never actually see you cross. Due to gravitational time dilation, predicted by general relativity and confirmed in precise atomic clock experiments, your image would appear to freeze and redshift into darkness. Two observers, one falling in and one watching from outside, have irreconcilable accounts of the same event. Both are correct within their own reference frames.

This is what physicists mean when they say black holes sit at the intersection of general relativity and quantum mechanics — and why a complete theory of quantum gravity remains one of the most important unsolved problems in physics.

Next time, we'll look at Hawking radiation and what it suggests about whether black holes actually last forever.

In the early 20th century, David Hilbert proposed an ambitious program: build a complete, consistent set of axioms from which every mathematical truth could be formally proven. Think of axioms as the ground rules — statements you accept as true without proof — and from those rules, you derive everything else. The dream was a kind of perfect machine for mathematical certainty.

Gödel destroyed that dream. But the way he did it is what makes this so elegant.

Here's the key move. Gödel realized you can encode mathematical statements as numbers. Every symbol, every formula, every proof can be translated into a number — what we now call a Gödel number. This means a mathematical system can, in a sense, talk about itself. Statements about numbers become statements about statements.

Now here's the thought experiment. Imagine a statement that says, roughly: "This statement cannot be proven within this system." Call it statement G.

Ask yourself: is G provable?

If G is provable, then what it says is false — you've proven a false statement, which means your system is inconsistent. That's catastrophic.

If G is not provable, then what it says is actually true — but your system can't prove it. That means your system is incomplete. There are true statements it cannot reach.

Either your system is inconsistent, or it's incomplete. You cannot have both completeness and consistency. This is Gödel's First Incompleteness Theorem.

The Second Incompleteness Theorem goes even further: a sufficiently powerful consistent system cannot prove its own consistency. You can't use arithmetic to prove that arithmetic is trustworthy. Any proof of the system's reliability requires stepping outside the system.

Here's the non-obvious conclusion people often miss. This isn't a failure of mathematics. Gödel didn't find a bug — he found a fundamental feature of formal systems. Any system powerful enough to describe basic arithmetic will always contain truths it cannot prove from within. Completeness and consistency are simply incompatible goals beyond a certain threshold of complexity.

This has deep implications. It means mathematical truth is larger than mathematical proof. There are things that are true that no finite formal system will ever capture.

Philosophers like J.R. Lucas argued this has consequences for the nature of mind versus machines. Whether or not you accept that extension, Gödel demonstrated something precise and permanent: no sufficiently rich logical system can be both complete and consistent. The map can never fully describe itself.

What Hilbert wanted — a perfect, closed foundation for all of mathematics — is not just unfinished. It's impossible.

Welcome back. Last time we covered the basics of Bayesian updating — starting with a prior belief, gathering evidence, and revising. Today I want to show you where this gets genuinely surprising.

Let's start with a thought experiment. Imagine a disease affects one in a thousand people. A diagnostic test for this disease is ninety-five percent accurate — meaning if you have the disease, it correctly identifies it ninety-five percent of the time, and if you don't have it, it correctly clears you ninety-five percent of the time.

You test positive. How worried should you be?

Most people say very worried. Ninety-five percent accurate sounds convincing. But let's actually run the numbers on a population of ten thousand people.

Ten people have the disease. The test catches ninety-five percent of them, so it correctly flags about nine or ten of them. But nine thousand nine hundred and ninety people don't have the disease. Five percent of those people get a false positive — that's roughly five hundred people.

So you have around five hundred and ten positive tests. Only about ten of those represent actual disease. That means if you test positive, your probability of actually having the disease is roughly ten divided by five hundred and ten — about two percent.

This result shocked doctors when it was first formalized. The medical community encountered this problem rigorously through the work of Thomas Bayes in the eighteenth century, and later Pierre-Simon Laplace, who developed the mathematics more fully. The insight still trips up physicians today — a point documented extensively by cognitive psychologist Gerd Gigerenzer, who found that a majority of medical professionals misread exactly this type of test result.

The reason this feels wrong is that we naturally focus on the test's accuracy and ignore what statisticians call the base rate — the underlying frequency of the thing we're looking for. Our intuition anchors on the most vivid piece of information and discards the quiet statistical context.

Here's the non-obvious conclusion: a highly accurate test applied to a rare condition produces results that are mostly noise. Accuracy and reliability are not the same thing. Reliability depends on where you're looking.

This has practical reach well beyond medicine. It applies to fraud detection systems, content moderation algorithms, airport security screening — anywhere you're searching for rare events in large populations. The mathematics guarantees that even excellent detection tools will generate many false positives when the target is uncommon enough.

So the next time you hear that something is ninety-five percent accurate, the right first question isn't "how good is the test?" It's "how common is the thing being tested for?" That prior probability — that base rate — quietly dominates the outcome.

Bayes isn't just a formula. It's a reminder that context does more work than we typically credit it for.

Building scalable systems isn't just  about writing good code - it's about   anticipating and solving problems  before they become critical. Today, we'll explore eight system design  challenges that every growing system faces,   along with the solutions that  top companies use to tackle them. Every successful application eventually faces  the challenge of handling high read volumes. Imagine a popular news website where  millions of readers view articles,   but only a small team of  editors publishes new content. The mismatch between reads and writes  creates an interesting scaling problem. The solution is caching. By implementing a fast cache layer,   the system first checks for data there  before hitting the slower database. While this dramatically reduces database  load, caching has its challenges: keeping the cache in sync with the  database and managing cache expiration. Strategies like TTL on keys or write-through  caching can help maintain consistency. Tools like Redis or Memcached make  implementing this pattern easier. Caching is especially effective for read-heavy,   low-churn data, like static  pages or product listings. Some systems face the opposite challenge -  handling massive amounts of incoming writes. Consider a logging system processing  millions of events per second, or a social media platform managing real-time user   interactions. These systems need  different optimization strategies. We tackle this with two approaches. First,   asynchronous writes with message  queues and worker processes. Instead of processing writes immediately, the  system queues them for background handling. This gives users instant feedback while the  heavy processing happens in the background. Second, we use LSM-Tree based  databases like Cassandra. These databases collect writes in memory and  periodically flush them to disk as sorted files. To maintain performance, they perform compaction:   merging files to reduce the number  of lookups required during reads. This makes writes very fast but reads become slower as they  may need to check multiple files. Handling high write loads is  just one part of the puzzle. Even the fastest system becomes  useless if it goes down. An e-commerce platform with a single database   server stops entirely on failure - no  searches, no purchases, no revenue. We solve this through redundancy and failover,   implementing database replication  with primary and replica instances. While this increases availability, it introduces  complexity in consistency management. We might   choose synchronous replication to prevent  data loss and accept higher latency, or opt for asynchronous replication  that offers better performance but   risks slight data loss during failures. Some systems even use quorum-based replication  to balance consistency and availability. Critical services like payment systems need true   high availability. This requires both load  balancing and replication working together. Load balancers distribute traffic across  server clusters and reroute around failures. For databases, a primary-replica  setup is standard: the primary handles writes while multiple replicas handle reads, and failover ensures a replica can  take over if the primary fails. Multiple-primary replication is  another option for distributing   writes geographically, though it comes  with more complex consistency trade-offs. Performance becomes even more  critical when serving users globally. Users in Australia shouldn't wait for  content to load from servers in Europe. CDNs solve this by caching content closer  to users, dramatically reducing latency. Static content, like videos and  images, works perfectly with CDNs. For dynamic content, solutions like edge  computing can complement CDN caching. Different types of content  need different cache-control   headers - longer durations for media files, shorter for user profiles. Managing large amounts of data  brings its own challenges. Modern platforms use two types of  storage: block storage and object storage. Block storage with its low latency and high   IOPS is ideal for databases and  frequently accessed small files. Object storage on the other hand costs  less and is designed to handle large,   static files like videos and backups at scale. Most platforms combine these:  user data goes into block storage,   while media files are stored in object storage. With all these systems running, we  need to monitor their performance. Modern monitoring tools like  Prometheus collect logs and metrics, while Grafana provides visualization. Distributed tracing tools like OpenTelemetry help  debug performance bottlenecks across components. At scale, managing this flood of data is  challenging. The key is to sample routine events,   keep detailed logs for critical operations, and  set up alerts that trigger only for real problems. One of the most common issues monitoring  reveals is slow database queries. Indexing is the first line of defense. Without indexes, the database scans  every record to find what it needs. With indexes, it can quickly  jump to the right data. Composite indexes, for multi-column  queries, can further optimize performance. But every index slows down writes slightly  since they need to be updated as data changes. Sometimes indexing alone isn't enough. As a last resort, consider sharding - splitting  the database across multiple machines, using strategies like range-based  or hash-based distribution. While sharding can scale the system significantly,   it adds substantial complexity  and can be challenging to reverse. If you like our video, you might like  our system design newsletter as well.   It covers topics and trends  in large-scale system design. Trusted by 1,000,000 readers. Subscribe at blog.bytebytego.com Tools like Vitess simplify  sharding for databases like MySQL,   but it’s a strategy to use sparingly  and only when absolutely necessary.

If you've ever asked a language model a question about something that happened last month, or asked it about your company's internal documentation, you've run into the core limitation: the model only knows what was in its training data. RAG, or Retrieval-Augmented Generation, is the standard architectural solution to this problem.

Let me walk you through exactly how it works.

The problem is straightforward. A model like GPT-4 or Llama 3 has a knowledge cutoff. It also can't access private databases, internal wikis, or documents you haven't shown it. Fine-tuning helps in some cases, but it's expensive, slow, and still doesn't handle live or frequently updated information well.

RAG solves this by splitting the work into two stages. First, you retrieve relevant documents from an external store. Second, you pass those documents into the model's context window along with the user's query, and the model generates a response grounded in that retrieved content.

Here's the concrete flow. You take your document corpus, whether that's a PDF collection, a knowledge base, or database records, and you chunk it into smaller pieces, typically 256 to 512 tokens each. You then encode each chunk using an embedding model. OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small and Cohere's embed-v3 are common choices here. These embeddings are stored in a vector database like Pinecone, Weaviate, or the open-source option Chroma.

When a user submits a query, you embed that query using the same model, then perform a similarity search, usually cosine similarity, against your stored vectors. The top-k results, often three to five chunks, get retrieved and inserted into the prompt. The language model then reads both the question and the retrieved context and generates its answer.

The original RAG paper from Facebook AI Research, published in 2020 by Lewis and colleagues, demonstrated this on open-domain question answering benchmarks like Natural Questions and TriviaQA. RAG outperformed fine-tuned models on several tasks because retrieval gave the model access to specific factual content on demand rather than relying on compressed parametric memory.

One practical consideration that trips people up is chunking strategy. If your chunks are too small, you lose context. If they're too large, you introduce noise. LlamaIndex and LangChain both provide tooling for experimenting with different chunking approaches and retrieval configurations, which is usually where you spend most of your tuning time in a real deployment.

A real application: customer support systems at companies like Intercom use RAG to ground responses in current help documentation. The model isn't memorizing every article. It's retrieving the relevant one at query time.

The key insight is that RAG decouples what the model knows from what it can access. That's the architectural trade-off worth understanding before you reach for fine-tuning or assume the model just needs more parameters.

This periodic doubling is an example of exponential growth, and while we’re not in any danger of discovering a real-life golden goose-coin, something almost as consequential  has been growing like this for the past 200 or so years:  the global economy. Many economists think  that an eternally growing economy is necessary to keep improving people’s lives, and that if the global economy stops growing, people would fight more over the fixed amount of value that exists, rather than working to generate new value. That raises the question: is infinite growth possible  on a finite planet? We measure economic growth by tracking  the total financial value of everything a country (or the world) produces and sells on the market. These products can help  us meet basic needs or improve our individual and collective  quality of life. But they also, crucially, take resources  to invent, build, or maintain. For example, this smartphone. It’s valuable in part because it contains aluminum, gallium, and silicon, all of which took energy and resources to mine, purify, and turn into a phone.
