To answer this question, let's consider what goes into the economy and what comes out of it: its inputs are labor, capital— which you can think of as money— and natural resources, like water or energy. Its output is value. Over the past 200 years, economies have gotten exponentially more efficient at producing value. If we, as a species,  are able to keep upgrading our economies so that they get ever-more efficient, we could theoretically pump out  more and more value using the same— or, let’s be really ambitious here—  fewer resources. So, how do we do that? How do we increase efficiency? With new technologies. This is where we hit a snag. New tech, in addition to making things more efficient, can also generate new demand, which ends up using more resources. We’re actually not in imminent danger of running out of most resources. But we have a much bigger  and more immediate problem: the global economy, and in particular those of rich countries, is driving climate change and destroying valuable natural environments on which all of us depend— soil, forests, fisheries,  and countless other resources that help keep our civilization running. So, what should we do? This is where economists disagree. Most economists think that new ideas will be able to fix most of these problems. They argue that, in the same way that exponentially  increasing resource and energy use have fueled exponential economic growth, human ingenuity has also increased exponentially, and will rise to meet these challenges in ways that we simply can't predict. For example, between 2000 and 2014, Germany grew their GDP by 16%, while cutting CO2 emissions by 12%. That’s impressive, but it’s not cutting emissions fast enough to limit warming to 1.5 degrees Celsius. For this reason and others, some economists think the solution is  to reengineer our economies completely. They make the case that what we should really be doing is weaning ourselves from the addiction to growth  and shifting to a post-growth economy. What would that look like? A post-growth economy wouldn’t assume that the economy should grow; instead, it would require us to focus on improving what we really need— things like renewable energy,  healthcare, and public transportation. To do that, post-growth economists suggest that rich countries should do things like guarantee  living wages, reduce wealth and income inequality, and ensure universal access  to public services, like healthcare. In such an economy, people would be theoretically less dependent on their jobs to earn their living or get healthcare, so it might be more feasible  to scale down production of things deemed less necessary. But this raises other questions:  who gets to define what’s necessary? How would we resolve  the inevitable disagreements? Could we really do  away with entire industries? The “we’ll come up with new ideas to solve these problems” approach can seem as realistic as, well, a magical gold coin. And the “we have to fundamentally change  our economies” approach can seem politically daunting,  particularly in rich countries. One way or another, we have to find  a way to benefit everyone while also taking care of our planet.

So in parts one and two, we established that prompting is cheaper and faster to iterate, while fine-tuning gives you more consistent formatting and can compress long instructions into model weights. Today I want to get specific about one tradeoff that trips people up in production: latency versus instruction complexity.

Here's the problem. You have a model that needs to follow twenty rules consistently. Maybe it's a customer support bot that must always respond in a particular tone, never mention competitors, format prices a certain way, and handle edge cases like refund requests with specific escalation language. You can put all of this in a system prompt. It works. But now your prompt is 800 tokens, and you're paying for those tokens on every single request, and you're adding latency because the model has to process that context before generating a single word of output.

This is where fine-tuning starts to look attractive. When you fine-tune on GPT-4o mini or Llama 3.1 using a dataset of examples that already follow your rules, the model learns to produce the right output without needing those 800 tokens of instruction. Your system prompt drops to maybe 50 tokens. At scale, say a million requests per day, that's a meaningful cost reduction and measurable latency improvement.

But here's what the benchmarks actually show, and I'm drawing from Anthropic's documentation and some of the work coming out of groups using OpenAI's fine-tuning API. Fine-tuned models degrade when the distribution of inputs shifts. If your training data was mostly English-speaking customers and you start getting Spanish queries, your fine-tuned model may quietly drop some of the rules it learned. A prompted model fails more loudly, which is actually useful information.

The concrete application: use a hybrid. Keep a short system prompt with your highest-stakes rules, the ones you absolutely cannot violate, and use fine-tuning to handle style, formatting, and routine behavior. This is roughly how companies like Intercom and Zendesk approach their AI features. The safety rails live in the prompt. The personality and format live in the weights.

One implementation note if you're using the OpenAI fine-tuning API or Hugging Face's TRL library for open-source models. Your training examples should represent failure cases, not just success cases. If your model needs to refuse certain requests, include refused examples in your dataset. Models fine-tuned only on successful responses often lose their ability to decline gracefully.

The decision framework, simplified: if your instruction set is stable, long, and you're operating at high volume, fine-tune. If your instructions change weekly or you need to audit exactly why the model behaved a certain way, stay in the prompt. When you need both scale and auditability, you use the hybrid.

Welcome back. In the last two videos, we talked about capturing everything and organizing it into projects. Today we're going to focus on the part most people skip entirely — the weekly review. And I want to be specific about what that actually looks like in practice.

The weekly review comes from David Allen's Getting Things Done system, but a lot of people read about it and then never actually do it because the original description is vague. So let me give you a version that takes about 25 minutes and has a clear sequence.

Step one: close the loops from last week. Open your task list and go through everything that was supposed to happen. Not to judge yourself, just to update reality. Move things that are done to done. Move things that didn't happen to this week or to a someday list. The goal here is that nothing is just sitting there unexamined. Unresolved tasks create low-grade mental noise all week, and that noise compounds. A 2011 study from Florida State University — the Zeigarnik effect research — showed that uncompleted tasks occupy cognitive resources even when you're not actively thinking about them. Closing those loops literally frees up mental bandwidth.

Step two: look at the next two weeks on your calendar before you build your task list for this week. This is a step most productivity systems leave out, and it's a mistake. If you have a presentation Thursday, your Tuesday and Wednesday need to reflect that. If you don't look forward, you'll plan the week in a vacuum and then get blindsided mid-week. Once you've seen what's coming, build your task list around your actual available time, not some idealized version of your schedule.

Step three: pick three outcomes — not tasks, outcomes — that would make this week feel like a success. Cal Newport calls this the "weekly plan" in his writing, but the framing I find more useful is asking: if Friday afternoon I look back, what three things would make me feel like the week was genuinely productive? Then you make sure those three things have protected time on your calendar before anything else gets scheduled. Not every task matters equally. Most weeks, three things really move the needle and the rest is maintenance.

Here's how these three steps connect: closing last week's loops gives you an accurate picture of where you are. Looking at the calendar gives you an accurate picture of your constraints. And choosing three outcomes gives you direction inside those constraints. Without all three, you're either planning blindly or reacting all week.

The thing you can do today — even if it's Tuesday — is spend ten minutes on just step one. Go through your task list, update it to reflect reality, and notice how differently you feel when you sit down to work tomorrow. That's the whole game. Accurate information, then intentional choice, then execution.

Welcome back. Last time we covered how massive stars burn through their nuclear fuel. Today we're going to follow what happens next, because the ending is genuinely strange.

When a star exhausts its fuel, it loses the outward pressure that was holding itself up against gravity. Think of it like a tug of war where one side suddenly drops the rope. Gravity wins instantly, and the star's core collapses.

Now here's where we need to be precise about what "collapse" means physically. The core isn't just shrinking a little. We're talking about an object roughly the mass of our Sun compressing down to a sphere about the size of a city — maybe 20 kilometers across — in less than a second. The density becomes so extreme that electrons get forced into protons, producing neutrons. What you have left is a neutron star, essentially a giant atomic nucleus held up by quantum mechanical pressure, something called neutron degeneracy pressure.

For most stellar cores, the story ends there. But for the most massive stars — cores above roughly three solar masses — even neutron degeneracy pressure isn't enough. There's a theoretical limit here worked out by J. Robert Oppenheimer and George Volkoff in 1939, now called the Tolman–Oppenheimer–Volkoff limit. Exceed that mass, and quantum mechanics loses. Gravity continues collapsing the core with nothing left to stop it.

This is where the thought experiment gets useful. Imagine you're compressing a sphere of matter and you want to ask: at what point does even light stop escaping? The answer depends on something called the Schwarzschild radius, derived from Karl Schwarzschild's solution to Einstein's field equations — worked out, remarkably, in 1916 while Schwarzschild was serving on the Russian front in World War One. For any mass, there's a corresponding radius. Pack that mass inside that radius, and the escape velocity exceeds the speed of light. That boundary is the event horizon.

Here's the counterintuitive part that most people miss. A black hole isn't a special kind of matter. It's a region of space where geometry itself has been curved so severely that all paths — every possible trajectory through spacetime — point inward. You wouldn't fall into a black hole because something pulls you. You'd fall in because "forward in time" and "toward the center" become the same direction. There's no material object to hit. The singularity at the center is better understood as a breakdown in our current equations than as a physical location.

This means a black hole is less like a cosmic vacuum cleaner and more like a one-way door written into the structure of space and time.

Next time, we'll look at what we've actually observed — gravitational waves, the Event Horizon Telescope image from 2019 — and what those observations confirm or challenge about this picture.

Welcome back. In the last four parts of this series, we covered async communication, deep work scheduling, home office setup, and managing energy across the day. Today we're pulling it all together with something that trips up even experienced remote workers: how to actually measure whether your remote work setup is *working* — and what to adjust when it isn't.

Most people evaluate their remote work effectiveness by how busy they felt, or whether they hit a deadline. That's not measurement, that's vibes. Here's a more rigorous approach.

Start with what researchers at Microsoft called "Work-Life Sway" in their 2021 New Future of Work report. They found that remote workers often expand their working hours by 10-20% without any corresponding increase in output. The hours blur, effort goes up, but results don't follow. So step one is this: for one week, track your *output units*, not your hours. An output unit is whatever a completed, ship-ready piece of work looks like for you — a finished draft, a closed ticket, a delivered analysis. Count them at the end of each day. Don't judge them yet, just count.

After one week, you have a baseline. Now step two: look at that data through the lens of your three most cognitively demanding tasks from the week. Identify *when* you did them — morning, afternoon, evening — and how long each took. Cal Newport's research on deep work suggests most people have a four to five hour window of peak cognitive performance per day. If your hardest tasks are consistently landing outside that window, you're working against your own biology. Move them. Even shifting a single high-stakes task from 3pm to 9am can cut completion time by 30 to 40 percent for many people.

Step three is the adjustment review. Every Friday, spend ten minutes asking three questions: What took longer than it should have? What did I avoid? What did I finish faster than expected? The answers reveal your friction points — not in the abstract, but in the specific context of *your* week. If the same task type keeps appearing in the first two columns, that's a signal. Maybe it needs a different time slot, a different environment, or to be broken into smaller pieces before you sit down with it.

Here's a concrete example: a product manager I spoke with noticed through this process that her weekly stakeholder summaries always appeared in the "took too long" column. She realized she was writing them from scratch each time. She created a ten-minute template on Sunday evening, and the same task dropped from ninety minutes to twenty.

That's the real leverage in remote work effectiveness — not working harder, but finding the specific friction that's costing you the most and removing it systematically.

This week, run the output unit count. Just that. Seven days of data will tell you more about your actual effectiveness than any productivity book.

Large Language Models (LLMs)  have gotten so good that by now,   virtually everyone agrees that they are going  to fundamentally change society. And each new   generation of LLMs is bigger and better  than the previous. It seems obvious that   just by scaling up the size of these models,  they will keep getting better and better,   and eventually surpass human intelligence. Except  that, actually no, that isn’t obvious at all.   In fact the entierty of statistical learning  theory, developd over the past 300 years,   predicts the exact opposite: larger models should  perform worse than smaller models, not better.   This is why, just 5 years ago, nobody would have  predicted that LLMs would be this successful.   I mean the foundational technology behind LLMs,  that is autoregressive transformer neural nets,   has existed pretty much unchanged since 2017.  But back then, nobody bothered to train extremely   large models, because, well the theory predicted  that the models would get worse with scale,   not better. So then why was everyone wrong? Well it all comes down to the concept of   overfitting. In machine learning, we train a  model to do what we want it to do by giving   it a bunch of examples. Suppose you wanted to  train a neural net to add 2 numbers together.   You would give it a list of examples of a pair  of numbers as input, and the corresponding sum   as the desired output. The neural net then  learns, that is it adjusts its weights, until   it can successfully map the inputs to the outputs.  And, ideally, the result of this learning process   will be that the neural net somehow manages to  set its weights to just the right the values so   that it effectively performs the algorithm for  adding 2 numbers together, digit by digit. If   it does this then, great, we can now use this  neural net to add any 2 numbers together.    However, another way that the model can solve this  task would be to simulate a sort of look-up table,   which just lists every single training example and  its corresponding output. Remember, the model only   cares about making correct predictions for the  training examples, nothing else matters. So this   lookup table is a perfectly valid solution. But of  course this lookup table is a terrible solution,   because if you apply it to new numbers,  which aren’t in the training data, you get   a completely random output. It’s like memorizing  all the answers to a test, instead of actually   studying and unerstanding the material. And in fact, most possible configurations of   weights that solve the training set  will behave like this lookup table,   not the algorithm. So if you think of the model  as just randomly picking one of the possible   solutions, then it will almost always end up  learning something like this look up table.   This is known as overfitting. The model memorizes  all of the details of the training data,   but fails to learn the underlying  mechanism or rule behind the data,   so it can’t generalizes to new situations. One way to prevent overfitting is to reduce the   size of the neural net, so that it simply  does not have enough capacity to memorize   all of the training examples. In order for  a neural net to simulate a lookup table,   it needs to somehow encode all of the information  in the training data into its weights. If   there’s 100,000 training examples, all of that  information needs to be in the weights somewhere.   But if there aren’t enough weights, then it’s  simply impossible to store all that information.    But even a very small neural net can still  implement the algorithm for addition. Afterall,   it only takes about 5 lines of code, regardless  of how many training examples there are. So if   we choose the size of the neural net just  right, then the only solution that it can   implement will be the correct one, and it will  be forced to learn that. And then the model will   be able to generalize to new inputs. And this is a very general phenomenon.   Almost always, the underlying rule or mechanism  behind the data is going to be the smallest way   of representing that data. So choosing  the model’s capacity to be just large   enough to accurately fit the training data,  but no larger, should be the best strategy   to produce models which generalize well. And this is exactly what we observe in practice.   Here are the results you get training neural  nets of varying sizes on an image classification   dataset. As you can see, the test error, that  is the error rate when evaluated on new inputs   not in the training data, decreases as the neural  net is made bigger, up to this point, but then it   begins to go up again when the size is increased  further. It’s at this point that the neural net   has just enough capacity to learn the mechanisms  behind the data, and adding more capacity just   results in memorization. The very largest  neural net actually achieves 0 training error,   it perfectly memorizes the entire training set.  But it’s not the best model. The model with the   lowest test error, is this one, in the middle. This is why, if you were to ask AI researchers 5   years ago, do you think training gigantic neural  nets with trillions of parameters on internet text   is a good idea? They would have said “No, it’s  just going to overfit”. Sure it’ll learn the   training dataset, but it will just be a stochastic  parrot, repeating things that it’s memorized.   It won’t have any real understanding.  Nobody was thinking about scaling up models,   because it didn’t work. Instead, AI researchers  were all trying to come up with better learning   algorithms and better neural net architectures  to improve model performance. The designing of   these algorithmic improvements is an intensely  creative process, and there was no way to brute   force a solution with more compute. But this would all change in 2019,   when a new paper came out demonstrating  something very interesting. In this paper,   the authors again trained various neural nets of  different sizes, but this time, they didn’t stop   when the neural net achieved 0 training error.  They kept increasing the size even further.   And what they found was, after the neural net  could perfectly memorize the training data,   the test error actually started to decrease again.  And it kept decreasing. In-fact it went even lower   than the previously best performing neural net.  And it just kept going lower, for all of the   sizes they tested. They dubbed this phenomenon  double descent: as you increase the size of a   neural net past the point of overfitting, the  test error begins to descend a second time,   and this time it’s seemingly without limit. This result came as quite a big surprise. It   seemed to open up a new avenue for improving  AI. Just keep making the models bigger. But   at the same time, this result flies in the  face of all statistical learning theory.   It shouldn’t be possible for models large enough  to memorize the training data many times over to   have real understanding that generalizes.  So what’s going on? Why do larger   models generalize better? Around the same time, another   paper came out which demonstrated that most of  the weights in a large neural net are useless.   The authors of this paper found a way to remove  up to 96% of the weights from a large neural   net without affecting its test error at all. The  process they used was as follows: To start with,   all weights are initialized to small random  values, as usual, and the network is fully trained   on the training data. They then removed the 10%  of weights with values closest to 0. The reasoning   here is that if a weight has a value is 0 then it  doesn’t do anything, so weights close to 0 should   be the ones which contribute the least amount. The  remaining weights are reset to the same initial   values they had before training started. This  whole process is then repeated around 30 times.   At the final iteration, they had a network  just 4% the size of the original which,   when trained from its initial random weights,  achieved the same test error as the full network.   This finding hints at what’s really going  on. Hidden inside of a large neural net,   is a much smaller sub-network that is actually  doing all of the work. The rest is just useless   fluff. That’s why it can generalize. But why does  it generalize better than a small network of the   same size as the sub-network? Remember, normally  when you train a smaller network it performs   worse. But that’s when the networks are trained  from initially random values. The only thing   that’s different about this sub-network is the  initial values it starts with before training.    To understand what’s going on here, you need  to know a bit about the way that neural nets   are trained: gradient descent. The gist of it is  that for each training example you make a small   change to every weight in order to make the nets  output closer to the label. We like to visualize   this process as a ball rolling down a hill.  The hill is the graph of the function which   maps possible weight configurations to training  error. So each point on this graph is showing you,   if you set the weights of the network to some  specific combination of values, what the training   error would then be. The ball represents the  current configuration of weights, and is initially   placed at a completely random point. The goal  is to move the ball to the lowest point on the   landscape, that corresponds to the weights with  the lowest training error. Gradient descent moves   the ball just like an actual ball rolling due to  gravity, in the direction of steepest descent.    The problem with this method is that you can  get stuck in a sub-optimal configuration. In   such a configuration, making any small change  to the weights results in more error, so the   weights just stop changing. There are better  configurations for the weights, with lower error,   but gradient descent will never find them. Because of this, depending on the initial weights   you start with, you can end up with different  final training errors. A neural net may end up   at 0 training error with some initializations, but  might get stuck early when started from others.   For large neural nets, it’s really  easy to fit the training data,   so you end with 0 training error no matter  where you start. However, for smaller networks,   the initialization becomes crucial, as  more possible initializations result   in sub-optimal performances. For small enough  networks, the vast majority of initializations   result in sub-optimal performance. Some  initializations may still result in 0 training   error, but it’s astronomically unlikely  to start there when picking at random.   Now, inside of a large neural net there are lots  of small sub-networks, which are just big enough   to fit the training data but only if they have  a really lucky initialization. Every subset of   weights can be thought of as a small neural net,  each with a different random initialization.   The authors of the pruning paper claim  that when you train a large neural net,   the result is the same as training all of these  different small sub-networks independently,   and keeping only the best one while discarding  the rest. Most of these sub-networks will have bad   initializations, and will be unable to learn. But  as long as one of them has a lucky initialization,   it will go on to solve the training task, all by  itself. When the author’s performed their pruning   procedure, they were essentially uncovering  the sub-network with the best initialization.   The authors call this the Lottery ticket  hypothesis. Each sub-network is like a   lottery ticket. It’s incredibly unlikely that  it will have a good initialization. But when   you have billions of tickets to choose from,  winning the lottery becomes a certainty.   And the number of tickets, equivalent to the  number of sub-networks in the full network,   grows exponentially in the size of the full  network. Training a larger network means   more initializations to choose from. But  with more initializations to choose from,   even smaller sub-networks will be able  to reliably fit the data. Remember,   the smaller a network is, the rarer it is for  it to have a good initialization, and the more   tickets you need for it to reliably win. So by  making the network larger, you get more tickets,   allowing even smaller sub-networks to win. Putting all of this together, we have the very   counter-intuitive result that the larger you  make a neural net, the smaller the winning   sub-network will be, and thus the simpler the  learned model. This is why larger neural nets   generalize better. Phew, statistical learning  theory is saved! The simplest model really is   the best generalizer. And it’s a good thing  too, because it’s not just machine learning,   all of science is built on this idea too,  though scientists call it Occam’s razor. 	   But this really is a miraculous result. Almost  by complete acciendent, we stumbled across a   learning algorithm that gets better simply  by dumping more computing power into it.   No longer, do we need to rely on human researchers  to come up with clever ideas to push the frontier   of AI forward. Now, we can just scale. I would like to point out that as of now,   no-one knows for sure why training a large  neural net behaves like training all of its   sub-networks in parallel and selecting  the best. It definitely isn’t obvious   that this should be the case. But one working  hypothesis is that the better initializations,   the ones which lead to lower training error, also  lead to faster learning. That is, they will fit   the data with fewer steps of gradient descent.  So the sub-network with the best initialization   learns to solve the task first, before the other  sub-networks have a chance to learn anything.   I would also like to point out that the pruning  strategy used in the lottery tick hypothesis paper   is nowhere near optimal. Choosing the smallest  weights at each iteration is not guaranteed to   result in the smallest possible network at  the end of multiple iterations. So the 4%   identified in the lottery ticket hypothesis paper  is an upper bound on the true size of the minimal   sub-network doing all of the work. The true  size, I suspect, is much smaller than that.   So what does all this mean for the future of AI?  Right now, there is a lot of debate about whether   making better language models will ever result in  human level intelligence. Prominent AI researcher   and turing award recepient Yann Lecunn has made  the argument that it isn’t possible for language   models to learn models of the world. He argues  that humans don’t bother to explicitly communicate   shared common-sense knowledge about the world, and  so this information isn’t present in the internet   text used to train language models. But it isn’t that simple. Remember,   the whole point of a good learning algorithm  is that it doesn’t just fit the training data,   it learns the mechanisms behind the data, so  that it can generalize. And it does this by   finding a small and simple model of the data. So  what happens when we apply this idea to language   modelling? Well, the smallest program that  generates all of the text on the internet is   going to be something like a physics engine that  simulates all of the particle interactions inside   a human brain. It only takes about a few thousand  lines of code to specify all of quantum mechanics   plus initial configurations for a human brain.  So in principle, a perfect learning algorithm,   when trained to predict the next word in internet  text, should learn to implement that program. And   that program would definitely possess a world  model, and have common-sense understanding,   and do everything a human can do, I mean  it’s literally simulating a human brain.   Now to be clear, neural nets are nowhere near as  good as this ideal perfect learning algorithm,   and the world models they induce are nowhere near  human level. But it is certainly clear that modern   LLMs do possess models of the world that allow  them to generalize beyond their training data,   to some extent. And we know that, as neural nets  are scaled up, they get better at finding smaller   and smaller models for their data. So there is  a very real argument to be made that just by   scaling up LLMs they will approach this ideal  learner, and hence human level intelligence.   I mean there’s a reason why every large  company and government in the world is   pumping biillions of dollars into scaling LLMs. Now personally, I don’t believe that scaling up   neural nets alone will be enough to get us to  human level intelligence. And the reason is just   that the rate of improvement is way too slow. Yes  neural nets get better when they are scaled up,   but It’s taking way too much compute to  get relatively modest gains in ability.   But I do think that language modelling  alone is enough to produce human level   intelligence. Even if scaling neural  nets eventually reaches a plateau,   I see no reason why better learning algorithms,  perhaps using entirely different architectures,   or maybe neural nets combined with  external tools, wouldn’t induce human   level intelligence when trained to predict  the next word in internet text. In fact,   if the learning algorithm is good  enough, it’s virtually guaranteed to.

In the last four parts, we've covered the basic structure of the prisoner's dilemma, why defection is the rational choice for individuals, how repeated games change the math, and how Robert Axelrod's tournaments in the 1980s showed that tit-for-tat strategies outperform purely selfish ones. Today we're going to push further into something that surprises most people: why cooperation can emerge even without communication, memory, or repeated interaction.

Here's the setup. Imagine two strangers who will never meet again. Classic game theory says they should both defect. The dominant strategy is clear. But here's what's interesting — in nature, we observe cooperation between organisms that have no capacity for memory, no ability to recognize each other, and no expectation of future interaction. Bacteria, for instance, engage in what biologists call mutualism constantly. So what's happening?

The answer comes from population dynamics, and the key thinker here is evolutionary biologist John Maynard Smith, who developed a framework called evolutionary game theory in the 1970s. Instead of asking what a perfectly rational individual should do, we ask a different question: what strategies survive over time in a population?

Think of it like this. Imagine a meadow with two types of wildflowers. One type produces nectar generously, attracting pollinators. Another produces very little. In the short run, the stingy flower uses fewer resources. But over generations, the generous flowers get pollinated more frequently, reproduce more, and gradually their strategy spreads through the population. No flower made a decision. No flower has memory. The environment itself selected for the cooperative behavior.

This is the concept of an evolutionarily stable strategy, or ESS. A strategy is evolutionarily stable if a population using it cannot be invaded by a small group using a different strategy. Defection feels like it should be the ESS, but Maynard Smith showed it often isn't. When cooperators cluster together — even by random chance — they interact with each other more than with defectors, and cooperation can take hold and spread.

This produces the non-obvious conclusion: rationality isn't actually required for cooperation to emerge. The math that explains why soldiers on opposing sides in World War One spontaneously developed truces during the Christmas of 1914 — which Axelrod himself analyzed — is the same math that explains cooperative behavior in ant colonies and even certain viruses.

The prisoner's dilemma isn't primarily a story about how people fail to cooperate. It's a story about the conditions under which cooperation becomes stable. Structure matters more than intention. The shape of the game matters more than the character of the players.

Next time, we'll look at how changing the payoff matrix — even slightly — can completely flip which strategies dominate. The numbers are more important than they first appear.

Welcome back. In the last episode we talked about how TCP gave the internet reliability — the ability to know whether your data actually arrived. Today I want to talk about what happened when reliability wasn't enough. Specifically, the moment engineers realized that being reliable could make you slow, and how that tension reshaped the internet we use today.

Let's start from scratch. Imagine you're moving apartments. You could hire a moving company that documents every single item, gets your signature at each step, and calls you if anything goes wrong. That's TCP. Or you could just start throwing boxes in a truck yourself — faster, but if something falls off the highway, nobody's calling you. That's UDP, the User Datagram Protocol, standardized in 1980 by David Reed.

Reed's insight was simple: not everything needs a guarantee. Some data is time-sensitive enough that a late packet is worse than a missing one. Think about a video call. If a single frame of your face arrives half a second late, the conversation falls apart. You'd rather skip that frame entirely than wait for TCP to carefully recover it.

So by the early 1980s, engineers had two tools: TCP for accuracy, UDP for speed. But here's where it gets interesting, and this is the non-obvious part most people miss.

TCP's reliability mechanism — the handshake, the acknowledgment signals, the retransmission of lost packets — creates a feedback loop between sender and receiver. In 1988, Van Jacobson published a landmark paper describing what he called "congestion collapse." The internet had actually experienced this in 1986. Network throughput on certain routes dropped by a factor of a thousand — not because the wires broke, but because too many TCP connections were all retransmitting lost packets simultaneously, which created more congestion, which caused more losses, which triggered more retransmissions. A death spiral of good intentions.

Jacobson's solution was congestion control built into TCP itself. The sender would now probe the network cautiously, back off when it detected loss, and gradually increase speed again. The protocol would learn the network's capacity through behavior rather than asking anyone.

This is the counterintuitive conclusion: the internet became stable not by adding more infrastructure, but by making the endpoints smarter and more self-restrained. There's no central traffic controller. Billions of devices today are each running Jacobson's algorithm independently, and collectively they prevent collapse without coordinating with each other.

That emergent stability — order from distributed restraint — is philosophically striking. It looks designed from the outside, but it arises from simple local rules each device follows on its own.

Next episode, we'll look at how the internet scaled from thousands of machines to billions, and why that required rethinking the address system entirely with IPv6.

Imagine you wake up one morning with a headache. Your first thought might be: do I have a brain tumor? Let's think through that carefully, because the way most people answer that question reveals a fundamental flaw in how we reason about probability.

Here's the key idea. When you're trying to figure out whether something is true, you can't just ask "does the evidence fit?" You have to ask "how much does the evidence *update* my belief from where it started?" That starting point is called the prior probability, and ignoring it is one of the most common reasoning errors humans make.

Thomas Bayes, an 18th century English minister, worked out the mathematics for this, and the framework carries his name. But the intuition is older than the math.

Let's build it from scratch. Suppose brain tumors affect roughly 0.02% of the population. Headaches are present in about 10% of brain tumor cases. So yes, some brain tumors cause headaches. But headaches are *also* present in about 70% of all people on any given day, from dehydration, stress, poor sleep, or tension. Now ask: if you have a headache, what's the probability it's a tumor?

Most people feel the answer should be substantial, because the symptom fits. Bayesian reasoning tells you to compare two competing explanations. How many people in a room of ten thousand have a tumor *and* a headache? About two. How many have a headache from ordinary causes? About seven thousand. So the probability your headache is a tumor is roughly two in seven thousand. Negligible.

This is the counterintuitive insight: evidence that fits a scary hypothesis can still barely move the needle if the hypothesis was extremely unlikely to begin with, and if the evidence is equally explained by mundane alternatives.

Statisticians call the failure to account for prior probability the base rate neglect fallacy. It's not just a medical reasoning error. It shows up everywhere. In the 1970s, psychologists Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky ran experiments demonstrating that people consistently ignore base rates when given vivid case descriptions, even when the statistical information is right in front of them. Their work eventually earned Kahneman a Nobel Prize in Economics.

So how do you actually use Bayesian thinking in daily life? When you encounter surprising news, ask: what did I believe before this? How likely is this source to report something like this if it's true, versus if it's false? Multiply those together. You're not doing formal math, you're developing a habit of calibrated updating rather than binary switching between believing and disbelieving.

The goal isn't certainty. The goal is to hold beliefs that are proportional to your evidence, and to move them appropriately when new information arrives.

That's it. That's the whole game.

Last time we covered attention heads and how they let the model weigh relationships between tokens. Today we're going one level deeper: the feedforward sublayer, which sits right after attention in every transformer block, and which most introductions skip over too quickly.

Here's the problem. After the attention mechanism aggregates information across the sequence, you have a vector for each token that's been updated with contextual information. But that vector still needs to be transformed into something more useful — a richer representation that captures patterns the attention step alone can't encode. The attention mechanism is good at routing information. It's not particularly good at storing and retrieving factual associations. That's a different job.

The feedforward sublayer handles it. The structure is straightforward: two linear transformations with a nonlinearity in between. In the original "Attention Is All You Need" paper from Vaswani et al., the hidden dimension of this sublayer is four times the model dimension. So in GPT-2 small, where the model dimension is 768, the feedforward layer expands to 3072, applies a GELU activation, then projects back down to 768.

Why the expansion? Research from Geva et al. in 2021 — the paper is called "Transformer Feed-Forward Layers Are Key-Value Memories" — showed something concrete. Each row in the first weight matrix acts like a key that detects a pattern in the input. The corresponding row in the second matrix acts like a value that gets added to the output if that key fires. So the feedforward layer is functionally a large lookup table of factual associations. When GPT-2 completes the phrase "The Eiffel Tower is located in," a significant portion of that retrieval happens in the feedforward sublayer, not in attention.

You can verify this in practice using the TransformerLens library from Neel Nanda, which was built specifically for mechanistic interpretability work. It lets you hook into individual layers and inspect what each feedforward neuron activates on. Run the library on a small GPT-2 model, probe the feedforward neurons in the middle layers, and you'll find clusters that respond specifically to things like date formats, country names, or syntactic roles.

The practical implication is important if you're doing fine-tuning. When you fine-tune a model on a new domain using something like LoRA from the Hu et al. 2021 paper, you're often inserting low-rank adapters into the attention projection matrices. But if your task requires the model to recall new factual associations — say, proprietary terminology — you may also need to update the feedforward weights, because that's where factual storage lives.

So to summarize: attention routes information between positions. The feedforward sublayer stores and retrieves learned associations through what are effectively key-value memory pairs. Both are necessary, and they're doing fundamentally different things.

In the last few episodes we've been building up the tragedy of the commons from scratch — the basic mechanism, why individual rationality produces collective ruin, and why simple privatization doesn't always fix it. Today I want to challenge something most people assume after hearing this framework: that the commons is fundamentally doomed without top-down control.

That assumption got seriously disrupted by the work of Elinor Ostrom, who won the Nobel Prize in Economics in 2009 — the first woman to do so. Her contribution was empirical. She went and looked at actual commons. Fishing grounds in Maine, irrigation systems in Spain, alpine meadows in Switzerland. And she found something the theoretical models weren't predicting: many of these resources had been managed sustainably for centuries, without privatization, without government intervention, without any of the solutions economists said were necessary.

So what was actually happening?

Here's a useful way to think about it. Imagine you and nine neighbors share a well. The standard model says someone will eventually pump too much and ruin it for everyone. But that model makes a quiet assumption — that you and your neighbors are strangers who never talk, never see each other again, and have no way to make binding agreements. Change any one of those conditions, and the outcome changes dramatically.

Ostrom identified what she called design principles — recurring features of commons that survive. A few of the most important: the people using the resource have a genuine role in setting the rules. There are graduated consequences for violations, not just tolerance followed by catastrophe. And crucially, there are low-cost mechanisms for resolving disputes. These aren't complicated institutions. They're often informal, local, and evolved over generations.

The counterintuitive insight here is that communication changes the math. When participants can make credible commitments to each other and monitor compliance, they're no longer playing the one-shot prisoner's dilemma that produces defection. They're playing a repeated game with reputation, relationships, and shared identity at stake. Under those conditions, cooperation becomes individually rational.

This matters because it reframes the question. The tragedy of the commons isn't really about shared resources — it's about specific social conditions. Anonymity, short time horizons, no communication, no enforcement. When you engineer those conditions in a laboratory, yes, you get over-extraction. But real communities aren't laboratory experiments. They carry history.

The practical takeaway is this: before assuming a commons needs to be either privatized or regulated from above, ask whether the community of users can define the resource boundary, monitor usage, and sanction violations themselves. Often they can, and often they do it better than external managers who lack local knowledge.

Ostrom called this polycentric governance, and her findings shifted how development economists think about resource management in the field.

Next episode, we'll look at where these self-governing commons fail — because they do sometimes fail — and what distinguishes the cases that collapse from the ones that persist.

Hey, so let's talk about how to actually build a skill development roadmap — not the vague "set goals and work hard" version, but a structured approach that tells you exactly what to learn, in what order, and how to know when you're ready to move on.

The core problem most people run into is they treat skill-building like a to-do list. They find a course, finish it, find another course, and six months later they have certificates but they can't actually do the thing. A roadmap fixes this because it's built around *output*, not input.

Here's how to build one properly.

**Step one: define the target competency in behavioral terms.** Don't write down "learn data analysis." Write down what a competent data analyst actually does — cleans messy datasets, builds dashboards stakeholders can interpret without help, communicates uncertainty in findings. That specificity is what makes the rest of the roadmap concrete. If you can't describe what the skill looks like in action, you don't know what you're aiming at yet.

**Step two: break the skill into sub-skills and sequence them by dependency.** This is where most roadmaps fail — people randomize their learning. Borrowed from software engineering, there's a concept called a *prerequisite graph*: you map which skills are required before others become learnable. For data analysis, statistical thinking has to come before regression modeling. Spreadsheet fluency has to come before SQL is useful. When you follow dependency order, each new thing you learn has something to attach to, which is why research on interleaving and prior knowledge — like what Roediger and Karpicke showed with retrieval practice — consistently finds retention improves when new material connects to existing structure.

**Step three: set performance thresholds, not time thresholds.** Instead of saying "I'll spend two weeks on SQL," define the exit condition — "I can write a query joining three tables and aggregating by multiple conditions without referencing documentation." Time-boxing feels productive but it's arbitrary. Performance thresholds force you to actually test yourself, which accelerates learning on its own.

One more thing worth building into your roadmap: scheduled friction points. Every four to six weeks, pick a small real project that requires you to combine what you've learned so far. Not a tutorial project — something with an actual outcome you care about. This is where isolated sub-skills start becoming an integrated capability. The first time you do this it will feel uncomfortably hard, and that's the right signal.

So here's what you can do today: take one skill you're currently working on and write down three to five things a competent person visibly *does* with that skill. Then ask yourself — what would I need to know first before any of this is learnable? That dependency question alone will reorganize your entire approach.

Last time we covered feature stores. Today we're talking about a problem that quietly breaks most production ML systems: training-serving skew.

Here's what happens. You train a model on historical data, you get great offline metrics, you deploy it, and then performance degrades. Not because the model is wrong. Because the data your model sees at serving time is computed differently than the data it saw during training. That gap is training-serving skew.

Let me make this concrete. Say you're building a fraud detection model at a company like Stripe. One of your features is "number of transactions this user made in the last 7 days." During training, you compute this by running a SQL query over your data warehouse. Offline, you get a clean batch computation. But at serving time, you need that same number in under 50 milliseconds. So an engineer writes a separate Redis lookup that pulls from a real-time counter. Those two code paths are written by different people, maybe months apart. One handles timezone offsets one way, the other handles them differently. One counts pending transactions, the other counts only settled ones. Now your training distribution and your serving distribution are different, and your model is quietly making worse decisions on live traffic.

The fix is to enforce a single feature computation path. This is the core idea behind tools like Feast, Tecton, and Hopsworks. You define your feature logic once, and the platform handles both the offline materialization for training and the online serving. The same code, same business logic, same edge case handling. Feast, for example, lets you write a feature transformation in Python, and it will backfill that feature into your training dataset while also serving it at low latency through a Redis or DynamoDB backend.

But even with a feature store, you need monitoring. The practice is called feature distribution monitoring. At serving time, you log the actual feature values your model receives. Then you compare that distribution against the distribution from your training set using statistical tests like the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test for continuous features, or chi-squared tests for categorical ones. Tools like Evidently AI or WhyLogs can automate this. If the distribution of your "transactions in last 7 days" feature drifts significantly, you get an alert before your precision metric collapses.

Here's a rule of thumb from the paper "Hidden Technical Debt in Machine Learning Systems" by Sculley et al. at Google: the more places you define the same feature, the more debt you're accumulating. Every duplicated transformation is a future skew incident waiting to happen.

So your checklist for this: define features once, use a feature store if you have more than a handful of features in production, and instrument distribution monitoring from day one. Not after something breaks.

Welcome back. Last time we covered your headline and profile photo — today we're going deeper into the parts of LinkedIn that most people treat as an afterthought but that actually drive whether someone reaches out to you or clicks away.

Let's start with your About section. Most people either leave it blank or write a third-person bio that reads like a press release. Neither works. The About section is the first place on LinkedIn where you can actually speak in your own voice, and recruiters and potential clients spend real time there if your headline pulled them in. Write it in first person, and open with a specific sentence about what you do and for whom — not your job title, but the actual outcome you create. Something like: "I help B2B SaaS companies reduce churn by rethinking their onboarding sequences." That's concrete. It tells the reader exactly whether you're relevant to them within five seconds.

Now here's where these pieces connect. Your About section should naturally lead into your Featured section, which is the second thing people check after reading your summary. Think of Featured as your proof layer. If your About section makes a claim, Featured is where you substantiate it. Pin a project, a published piece, a case study, or even a well-performing post. LinkedIn's own internal data has shown that profiles with a completed Featured section receive significantly more profile views — and the key word is completed, meaning an actual piece of content with a thumbnail and a clear title, not just a link dump.

The third area is your Experience section, and this is where almost everyone leaves value on the table. Job titles and dates alone tell recruiters nothing useful. For each role, write two to three bullet points that follow this structure: action verb, specific task, measurable result. "Led a content strategy overhaul that increased organic LinkedIn impressions by 340% over six months" is infinitely more useful than "Managed content strategy." This is essentially the CAR framework — Context, Action, Result — applied to your work history. It's a simple structure, but it forces you to replace vague responsibilities with actual evidence of impact.

Here's why the order matters: your headline gets someone to your profile, your About section gets them to trust your voice, Featured gives them something tangible to engage with, and a strong Experience section converts a curious visitor into someone who actually wants to connect or hire you. Each section is doing a specific job in that sequence.

The one thing you can do today: open your LinkedIn Experience section, pick your most recent role, and rewrite just one bullet point using the CAR framework. Make it specific, include a number if you have one, and save it. That single change often makes the biggest difference because it's the section people trust most when they're actually making a decision about you.

Welcome back. Last time we covered how to anchor high and why the first number in a negotiation tends to set the entire range. Today we're going deeper — specifically into what happens after you've made your ask and the other side pushes back.

Because that's where most people fold. And folding early is where real money gets left on the table.

So let's talk about the three moves that actually matter in this phase.

**First: Separate the offer from the final answer.**

When a recruiter says "this is the best we can do," that is almost never literally true. It's a signal that they're uncomfortable going further without more justification. Your job is to give them that justification. Instead of accepting or rejecting, respond with something like: "I appreciate that. Can you help me understand what goes into the compensation structure at this level?" This buys time, signals professionalism, and often reveals flexibility you didn't know existed — things like signing bonuses, extra vacation, or a 90-day review with a built-in raise.

**Second: Use the bracketing technique.**

This comes from FBI negotiation training, specifically the work of Chris Voss in *Never Split the Difference*. Bracketing means you set your target number, then make your actual ask higher than that — so when they counter, you land near where you actually want to be. If you want $95,000, ask for $105,000. If they come back at $90,000, you negotiate to $95,000 and both sides feel like they've moved. Research from Columbia Business School found that negotiators who made precise, specific counteroffers — like $97,500 instead of $100,000 — were perceived as more informed and conceded less ground. Specificity signals that you've done your homework.

**Third: Name the silence.**

After you've made a counteroffer, stop talking. This is harder than it sounds. Most people fill silence with concessions — they immediately start softening their own ask. Instead, let the silence sit for three to five seconds. If you're on a call, you can acknowledge it with a simple: "I want to give you time to think about that." That phrase alone keeps the conversation open without giving anything away.

Here's how these three steps connect: You delay commitment, you anchor strategically using the bracket, and then you protect that anchor by staying quiet. Each step reinforces the last.

One more thing — none of this requires you to be aggressive or adversarial. The best negotiators sound collaborative the entire time. You're solving a problem together, not winning an argument.

**Your takeaway for today:** Before your next salary conversation, write down three numbers — your walk-away number, your target, and your opening ask. Most people only think about one. Having all three mapped in advance means you won't be doing math while you're also managing nerves.

That's the move. Use it.

Welcome back. In the last four parts we covered data ingestion, feature stores, training orchestration, and serving infrastructure. Today we're focusing on something that breaks production ML systems constantly: training-serving skew.

Here's the problem. You train a model offline. It performs well in evaluation. You deploy it. Accuracy drops by eight percent in the first week. The model didn't change. The serving infrastructure looks fine. What happened?

The answer is almost always that your training data was processed differently than your serving data. Not dramatically differently — just slightly. Maybe your training pipeline computed a rolling 7-day average using a pandas groupby with a specific sort order. Your serving pipeline computed the same feature using a Redis sorted set, which handles ties differently. The values diverge by small amounts. Across millions of examples, the model sees inputs it effectively never trained on.

This is training-serving skew, and the solution is not better monitoring — it's architectural. You need a single feature computation path.

The pattern is called a unified feature pipeline, and Uber, Airbnb, and LinkedIn all published engineering blog posts describing their implementations of this. The core idea: write your feature logic once, execute it in two modes.

Here's a concrete way to think about it. Suppose you have a feature called user_purchase_velocity — purchases in the last 30 days divided by account age in days. In training, you compute this over a historical snapshot. At serving time, you compute it on the fly for a live user. If those are two separate functions written by two different engineers, you have drift risk.

Instead, you define one function, something like a Python class with a compute method, and you run that same class through an offline executor like Apache Spark for training data, and a low-latency online executor backed by DynamoDB or Cassandra for serving. Feast, the open-source feature store, implements this pattern. Tecton, the commercial version built by some of the original Michelangelo team at Uber, goes further and manages the deployment of these pipelines automatically.

The application step: when you're building or auditing your pipeline, list every feature going into your model. For each one, trace the exact computation path in training and the exact computation path in serving. If they're not the same code or formally equivalent, you have a latent skew problem.

A useful benchmark: Google's paper on hidden technical debt in ML systems found that feature engineering code is the most common source of silent model degradation in deployed systems. Not model architecture. Not hyperparameters. Data processing.

So the takeaway is this: before you add more features or retrain your model, verify your feature computation is unified. One definition, two runtime contexts. That single discipline eliminates an entire category of production failure.

Next part we'll cover monitoring specifically for data drift versus concept drift — they require completely different responses. See you then.

Large language models refer to large general-purpose language models that can be pre-trained and then fine tuned for specific purposes. What do pre-trained and fine tuned mean? Imagine training a dog. Often, you train your dog basic commands such as sit, come, down, and stay. These commands are normally sufficient for everyday life and help your dog become a good canine citizen. However, if you need a special service dog such as a police dog, a guide dog, or a hunting dog, you add special trainings. The similar idea applies to large language models. These models are trained for general purposes to solve common language problems such as text classification, question answering, document summarization, and text generation across industries. The models can then be tailored to solve specific problems in different fields such as retail, finance, and entertainment using a relatively small size of field data sets. Let's further break down the concept into three major features of large language models. Large indicates two meanings. First is the enormous size of the training data set, sometimes at the petabyte scale. Second, it refers to the parameter count. In ML, parameters are often called hyperparameters. Parameters are basically the memories and the knowledge that the machine learned from the model training. Parameters define the skill of a model in solving a problem such as predicting text. General purpose means that the models are sufficient to solve common problems. Two reasons lead to this idea. First is the commonality of a human language regardless of the specific tasks. And second is the resource restriction. Only certain organizations have the capability to train such large language models with huge data sets and a tremendous number of parameters. How about letting them create fundamental language models for others to use? This leads to the last point, pre-trained and fine tuned, meaning to pre-train a large language model for a general purpose with a large data set and then fine tune it for specific aims with a much smaller data set. The benefits of using large language models are straightforward. First, a single model can be used for different tasks. This is a dream come true. These large language models that are trained with petabytes of data and generate billions of parameters are smart enough to solve different tasks, including language translation, sentence completion, text classification, question answering, and more. Second, large language models require minimal field training data when you tailor them to solve your specific problem. Large language models obtain decent performance even with little domain training data. In other words, they can be used for few shot or even zero-shot scenarios. In machine learning, few shot refers to training a model with minimal data, and zero shot implies that a model can recognize things that have not explicitly been taught in the training before. Third, the performance of large language models is continuously growing when you add more data and parameters. Let's take PaLM as an example. Prompt design and prompt engineering are two closely-related concepts in natural language processing. Both involve the process of creating a prompt that is clear, concise, and informative. However, there are some key differences between the two. Prompt design is the process of creating a prompt that is tailored to the specific task that this system is being asked to perform. Prompt engineering is the process of creating a prompt that is designed to improve performance. Welcome back. In the last four parts, we built up a complete picture of how your immune system recognizes threats, recruits the right cells, and clears infections. Today I want to focus on one specific mechanism that makes vaccines so elegant, and it leads somewhere genuinely surprising.

Let's start from scratch with a question: why does your immune system remember things at all?

When a B cell or T cell encounters an antigen it matches, it doesn't just activate once and disappear. It divides rapidly, creating thousands of copies of itself. Most of those copies become workers — they fight the current infection. But a small fraction become something different. They become memory cells, and they essentially go dormant. They stop working and just wait.

Here's the important part: that waiting population persists for decades. In some cases, possibly for life. A 2020 study published in Nature examined bone marrow samples from people who had received measles vaccines as children. Some of these individuals were in their seventies. The memory B cells were still there, still specific to measles, still ready.

Now here's the thought experiment. Imagine you're trying to train a very large, complicated organization — say, an army — to respond to a specific threat. One approach is to send every soldier through full combat. Expensive, dangerous, and you lose people. A better approach is to show them a realistic simulation. They train against it, develop specialized skills, and when the real thing arrives, they already know exactly what to do.

That's what a vaccine is doing biochemically. It presents your immune system with something that looks enough like a pathogen — an antigen, a protein coat, a weakened version — that your B and T cells activate and generate that dormant memory population. No actual infection required.

But here's where it gets counterintuitive.

You might assume the strength of your immune memory is proportional to how sick you got. More inflammation, more danger signals, stronger memory. That turns out to be largely wrong. What matters most isn't the intensity of the response — it's the quality of the antigen presentation and the duration of antigen exposure. A well-designed vaccine, by precisely controlling what your immune system sees and for how long, can actually produce more durable memory than the natural infection itself.

We've seen this with the human papillomavirus vaccine. The antibody levels it generates are several times higher than those produced by natural infection. This isn't a paradox once you understand the mechanism — it's the result of deliberate engineering of the antigen presentation process.

So the non-obvious insight is this: your immune system doesn't care whether a threat was real or simulated. It only cares whether it received a clear, accurate signal. A vaccine, done well, is a cleaner signal than a real pathogen, because real pathogens are also trying to hide.

That's the core principle. Next time, we'll look at how different vaccine platforms — mRNA, subunit, live-attenuated — each make different tradeoffs in delivering that signal.

Imagine yourself in the middle of a game of  catch . A player throws the ball towards you,   and as the ball jets closer , you quickly  recognize where to position your hand in order to   catch the ball safely. Have you ever wondered  how your brain is able to quickly make that   decision ? How does information flow so quickly  from your eyes to your brain and back to your hand   in order to successfully catch the ball? All information from our environment—   in this case, information from the eye about the  size and speed of the ball coming towards you—   is converted by nerve cells or neurons into  electrical signals that are carried to the brain.   Similarly, information from our brain to our  body, such as an order to move our hand towards   the ball’s trajectory, is also sent in the form  of these signals. These electric signals are   called action potentials or nerve impulses.  Because these signals are instantaneous,   our brain is able to help us quickly detect  and respond to things in our environment,   both good and bad. It is thanks to the speedy  nature of such signals that you can immediately   recognize your favo rite song, or quickly pull  your foot away when you step on a pin by accident!  So how do neurons send these  signals or “fire” action potentials?  The human brain contains tens of billions  of neurons that are connected to each other.   Each neuron has a cell body with several branches.  The smaller branches— called dendrites— receive   signals that come together and then the total  signal is transmitted through the longest branch—   the axon. Similar to how a wire carries  electricity from a switch to an electric bulb,   the axon carries nerve impulses  from a cell body to the next cell.  The outer or extracellular surface of a neuron is  bathed in a ‘salty’ solution of positively charged   Sodium and negatively charged Chloride ions. The  inner surface has positively charged Potassium   ions and proteins that are negatively charged. The  negatively charged proteins inside the cell are   not balanced by an equal number of positive ions,  making the inner surface of the neuron slightly   more negative than the outside. This gives  rise to an “electrical gradient”. Furthermore,   there is a higher concentration of potassium ions  inside, giving rise to a “diffusion gradient”   that forces them to flow outside. Thus,  there is both an electrical gradient and a   chemical gradient— an electrochemical gradient—  forcing ions from inside the cell to go out.  Each neuron, when at rest, maintains a voltage  difference arising from the electrochemical   gradient, just as a battery maintains a  certain voltage between its poles. This   voltage of approximately -70mV is referred  to as the “resting potential” of a neuron.  The cell membrane of the  neuron contains “ion channels”.   These are pores formed using proteins that  selectively allow certain ions to pass through,   similar to how an airport security guard checks  visitors and only allows those with a ticket to   proceed to their gates . Some of these channels  are special, in that they open and close only at   a certain voltage; these are called voltage-gated  ion channels. Ion channels allow ions in and out,   causing the voltage of the cell to  move away from its resting potential.  At any given time, a neuron keeps receiving small  amounts of incoming signals, which results in   small fluctuations in the membrane voltage. These  are called “graded potential”, but they are not   strong enough to result in the firing of a neuron. A neuron fires or generates a nerve impulse only   when the total sum of input signals is large  enough to cross a “threshold potential” of -55mV.   This begins because it triggers  voltage-gated Sodium channels to open.   These channels, when opened, cause a rush of  many Sodium ions into the cell from outside.   This causes “depolarization” of the  neuron, or a reversal of charge in   its inner surface from negative to positive. The voltage difference continues to become   more positive. When the voltage reaches 30 mV, it  causes voltage-gated Potassium channels to open.   These channels allow Potassium ions to move from  inside to outside, slowly “repolarizing” the cell   or restoring the more negative voltage difference.  However, the voltage- gated Potassium ion channels   are much slower to close, causing the voltage  to drop below the resting potential of -70mV.   This is called “hyperpolarization”. After a neuron fires in this way,   its voltage would be drained, like a  rechargeable battery after prolonged us e.   A reusable battery can be “recharged” by  passing an external current through it.  The neurons outsource this job to “pumps” located  on the membrane by spending energy currency called   ‘ATP’. These pumps act like revolving doors in  buildings; they let out more Sodium ions from   inside the cell, while simultaneously bringing  in fewer Potassium ions. This brings the neuron   back to its resting potential. This entire event of neuron   firing follows certain rules: (1) It is an all or none event.  Nerve impulses are much like digital signals.  They occur in the same magnitude, regardless of   how strong or weak the stimulus is. However, the  threshold voltage must be crossed to generate it. (2) They are unidirectional. Once a segment of the membrane becomes   “depolarized”, it passes the signal forward to the  next segment. Ion channels, after finishing their   job of passing the signal, will pull down their  shutters and ‘take a break’, becoming inactive for   a short while. This is called their “refractory  period”. Therefore, a signal cannot return to them   and has no choice but to travel forward  to the next segment on the membrane.  The process of generating a nerve impulse is  fairly quick. However, in certain situations where   this is not fast enough, our body uses special  neurons with a coating called a myelin sheath   to speed up signalling. This coating covers the  surface of the axon, while leaving small spots   called “Nodes of Ranvier” open. In such covered  axons, the signal doesn’t have to travel through   the entire length of the axon, but only through  these gaps, making the signal travel faster.   This type of “jumpy” signal transmission  is called “saltatory” conduction.  In our sense organs, these nerve impulses  are caused in response to a sensation,   such as odo r, taste, touch, sound  or light. For example, in the eye,   a nerve impulse is generated in response to light  reaching the cells in the retina, which are then   passed on through the optic nerve to the part  of the brain that processes visual information.   The nerve impulse can also have a variety  of effects based on where it is sent . When   you accidentally step on a pin on the ground,  nerves in the spinal cord respond by sending a   nerve impulse to your leg muscles, asking  it to contract, thereby pulling your leg   away from the pin in a fraction of a second. The brain constantly makes sense of the pattern,   frequency and area of origin  of the incoming nerve impulses   to interpret them , and then responds by making  appropriate commands to the relevant body parts.   In a way, n erve impulses or action potentials  are a digital language of the brain itself!

Last time we covered how to prepare for a performance review before it happens. Today we're going deeper — specifically what to do during and after the conversation, because that's where most people leave real value on the table.

Let's start with the meeting itself. The biggest mistake people make is treating it as a passive experience, where your manager talks and you listen and nod. Instead, come in with what researchers at Gallup call a "strengths conversation framework" — meaning you're not just waiting to hear feedback, you're actively steering the discussion toward your contributions, your growth, and your next goals.

Here's how to do that practically.

First, open with a brief self-summary before your manager sets the tone. Something like: "Before we dive in, I wanted to share where I feel I made the most impact this quarter and where I think I have room to grow." This does two things — it signals self-awareness, and it anchors the conversation around your narrative instead of someone else's. Managers consistently rate employees who demonstrate self-reflection as higher performers, even independent of actual output.

Second, when you receive critical feedback, use what's called the clarification loop. Don't just say "okay, got it." Ask: "Can you give me a specific example of when you noticed that?" and "What would better look like to you?" This isn't pushback — it's precision. Vague feedback like "you need to communicate more" is almost impossible to act on. A specific answer like "I'd like you to send a project update every Friday by 3pm" is immediately actionable. You leave the room with a real target.

Third, before the meeting ends, explicitly ask about the path forward. Most reviews close without a concrete next step, which means both sides walk away with different assumptions. Ask: "Based on what we've talked about today, what's the one thing you'd like to see me prioritize over the next 90 days?" Then write it down, repeat it back, and follow up with an email summary within 24 hours confirming what you discussed. That email alone sets you apart — it shows ownership and creates a paper trail you can reference in your next review.

Now, the post-review phase. Within one week, put your key development goal into your calendar as a recurring task. Not just a note — a scheduled action. A 2021 study published in the British Journal of Health Psychology found that people who planned the when and where of a goal were significantly more likely to follow through. The same principle applies here.

The takeaway you can use today: if you have a review coming up, write down one question you'll ask before the meeting ends — specifically about what success looks like in the next 90 days. That single question can completely change the quality of feedback you walk away with.
